LUCY STONE.

BY REV. LOUISE S. BAKER.

HER saintly face, flower-like, turned ever toward 
	the Light,
Seeking that radiance divine which could be 
	lent again
To teach those of less steady look the vision
	bright;—
Tracing a pathway on life’s flowing moor or
 	marshy fen. 
That kindly face has met the Day with eye un-
	blenched
Its quivering light with rosy fingers beckond
	her at last,
E'en on the earth. Athwart her heart with fires
	unquenched
It lay—and, smiling, with the hope of action
	new, she passed.
 Nantucket, Oct, 22,1893.
— Woman's Journal.


THANKSGIVING HYMN.

We thank Thee, O Father, for all that is bright—
The gleam of the day and the stars of the night;
The flowers of our youth and the fruits of our
	prime,
And blessings that march down the pathway of
	time.
We thank Thee, O Father, for all that is drear—
The sob of the tempest, the flow of the tear;
For never in blindness, and never in vain,
Thy mercy permitted a sorrow or pain.
We thank Thee, O Father, for song and for feast,
The harvest that glowed, and the wealth that in-
	creased;
For never a blessing encompassed earth’s child,
But Thou in Thy mercy looked downward and
	smiled.
We thank Thee, O Father of all, for the power
Of aiding each other in life’s darkest hour,
The generous heart, and the bountiful hand,
And all the soul help that sad souls understand.
We thank Thee, O Father, for days yet to be--
For hopes that our future will call us to Thee;
That all our eternity form, through Thy love,
One Thanksgiving day in the mansions above.
						WILL CARLETON.


AUTUMN.

How beautiful the passing days,
	That quickly hurry by;
Whose life breathes out unclouded rays,
	Then gloriously they die.


The ash and stately poplar trees,
	Seem filled with yellow light
That nodded in the summer breeze,
	So dark and dense a sight.
The sun comes sparkling on his way,'
	Unmindful of his charm
The grass is rustling, Stiff and gray,
	To steps that tread nor harm.
Unsheltered flowers hang their heads,
	O'er bent and blackened stems,
That humbly droop towards their beds,
	'Fore sunshine that condemns.
In sympathy the sun imparts,
	A warm and healing breath,
To bind their little broken hearts,
	Which only hastens deaths
The ivy trails the old rail fence,
	Enlivened here and there
With red and yellow leaves from whence
	Shine purple berries rare.
The bright-eyed squirrel seeks his store,
	Of nuts that fallen lie;
Then speeds away the branches o’er,
	As if by winds blown by. 
The beauty of the leaves is brief,
	And by some magic spell,
When morning sparkles white, each leaf
	Seems earthward to have fell
Flowers fall with the germ of life.
	Unaided and forlorn,
To rise again like human lives,
	In spring-times’ early morn.
O death, life's most relentless foe,
	Withold thy cuel breath;
Nor bring earth's fairest ones to woe,
	Slay not, O bitter death. 
HYACINTH
Nov. 3, 1893.

[Column 2]
much to say of what would be likely to
transpire and the extravagance and style
which would be kept up, but as no one of
the party was interested in his views, he
grew silent and inclined to be morose
somewhat, and was not an agreeable com-
panion.
	On board the vessel were two or three
ministers, dissenters as they were designated
in those days, and Dr. Bland entered into
conversation with them on the first day out
and thoroughly investigated their religious
faith. They with some of their followers
were fleeing from the persecution heaped
upon them in their own native land, and
preferred hardships and privation in the
wilderness to the scorn ridicule and op-
probium of those among whom their lot
was cast in their own country.  Still they
talked much of the home they were leaving,
and the green fields so dear to every
English heart village-born, but above all
was the desire to exercise the freedom of 
conscience and religious views—for this
they sacrificed home, friends and the graves,
of their ancestors, not knowing what fate
had in store for them in an unknown
country.
	The ladies were too sea-sick after being a
few hours out of the sight of land to enjoy 
anything, and young Walton, who had lit-
tle courage at any time was quite as help-
less as his wife, so that what had seemed a
pleasant undertaking on the first day, was a
miserable failure before many hours had
passed.  Dr. Bland and one or two of the 
servants managed to take care of the rest of
the party, and the Doctor kept up his
interest in the Puritans on board, though
he could scarcely hold with the extreme
views entertained by them, being a man of
very broad mind and liberal education.
	One young and ardent disciple of the
faith Barrington by name became greatly
attached to the Doctor and his party and
was ever after a friend to the two families.
He sought by his persuasive eloquence and 
kindlinessto help Lord Walton morally and
to uplift him from the despondency which
had fallen upon him after leaving the shores
of England, and partly perhaps in conse-
quence of sea sickness, and the peculiar
combination of circumstances, that had
brought about his consent to take this
journey.  His influence was good upon
Maitland though not permanent.  In fact
so devoted was young Barrington that his
friend sometimes distrusted his motives
and fancied he was in love with his wife.
He had explained to her the need of re-
ligion and every day after she was
convalescent read passages from the Bible
commenting upon them, and she listened
dreamily to it all, as though she was half
distrustful it was not real but another de-
ception.
	Finally without any serious happenings
the company landed on the New England
coast, and having arranged beforehand
with British residents, were warmly
welcomed and soon comfortably established
in dwellings previously fitted up befitting
their station in life  All was strange and 
there were none with whom they had ever


IN RURAL ENGLAND.

				XXXV.
	OUR readers may think that when
England is left behind  the title should no
longer be England, but it will appear in the
future that the green fields of the mother
country bear strongly upon the lives of the
subjects of the story.
	The little party consisting of Lord and
Lady Walton their son Edward and five
servants, Dr. Bland and wife, son and two
servants thirteen in number embarked at
Ipswich in the ship Elizabeth on a fine
morning in May, the weather most
propitious and even Lady Anna was almost
jubilant though she had been at times
greatly depressed. Just before leaving
Maitland received word that his mother
would soon return to Walton Hall and look
after things during his absence, which she
added would be only temporary as neither
himself or Anna would remain long in so
wild and unsettled a country as America.
Young Lord Walton was almost unwilling
to go when he found his mother was about
to take up her abode in the home he con-
side sacred to the Waltons, and he had


[Column 1]
been acquainted, yet they were people of 
social distinction, and had come to America
some of them in official positions, and were
well posted concerning affairs in the new
land, and eager for news from the opposite
side of the Atlantic. The first few days
were all bustle and excitement, and Lord
and Lady Walton were made much of in
a social way, and received with enthusiasm,
and for a time this was very gratifying to
Maitland, but the novelty soon wore off.
Afterward he became restless and as he was
wholly unemployed and did not interest
himself in any public affairs, either political
or religious, relating to the colonies, or in-
deed to his neighbors and best friends, he
was a sort of tax on the nervous energy
of his wife, and she had little to spare, for
her health was very delicate which was a
constant source of anxiety to her cousin
Jane, who had hoped to see her improve in
the new country, where there was so much
to divert her thoughts, and turn her at-
tention from the past.
	Dr. Bland himself was not in the best of
health, though he kept up his daily round
of business, and found plenty of occupation
for many needed medical advice, and he
soon became a great favorite. He was also
intensely interested in all that concerned the
Puritans, which somewhat detracted from
his personal influence with those, who still
maintained their allegiance to the crown.
His wife was one with him in this respect,
if not really more ardent and devotedly
religious in sentiment. Her cousin Anna
shared her feelings and this drove Maitland
farther away from her, and caused him
more jealous feelings towards young Bar-
rington, who quite innocent of any such
misunderstanding still visited at the house,
unconscious of causing discord or enstrange-
ment between husband and wife. It was
through young Barrington’s consistent
teaching that Lady Walton had been con-
verted, and she was so pure-minded and
honest-hearted that Barrington held her in
the highest esteem and their friendship was
such as exists between fervent devotees of
the same religious faith.
	Maitland’s jealousy made him unfeeling
aud he taunted Lady Anna one day with
her devotion to Barrington in such a coarse
way that she broke down, and became
hysterical for the first time since she had
grasped a faith which satisfied her soul.
	Dr. Bland was called and having, noticed
beforehand Maitland’s unreasonable sus-
picions lectured him severely after attending
to his patient. He reproached him with
wooing a beautiful innocent and un-
sophisticated girl surreptitiously, and then
after making her his wife without mercy or
manly honor even breaking her heart, by
his own misdemeanors, and now accusing
her of inconstancy of which he himself had
been guilty. Lord Walton could not de-
fend himself, and therefore remained silent,
but obstinate still, hating his friend unre-
lentingly, yet conscious of his faults;
after rating him soundly the Doctor appeal 
led to his wife, telling her if there was
really anything good in him, she could
find the way to his heart. Upon this sug-
gestion Mrs. Bland sought an interview and
found him a little mellowed, because of the
danger to his wife from his unfeeling re-
marks. Mrs. Bland made plain the facts,
she said, ‘‘You have brought my cousin
here of your own volition, and she is in a
strange country away from all ties and as-
sociations except us, and remember were
anything to happen to her in an unknown

[Column 2]
land, you would be called in question by
her brother, and Mr. Harrison, who would
not leave a stone unturned, or a question
unanswered in reference to the circum-
stances, I warn you now to beware how
you trifle with one who has given up all
else for you, and seriously consider how
you have repaid her.”
	Maitland grew pale and trembled for he
saw how dreadfully in earnest Mrs. Bland
was, and knew how indifferent and in-
constant he had been. He did not attempt
to reply only to bow his head upon his
hands like one condemned. After this
stringing rebuke Mrs. Bland left him to his
own reflections saying, ‘‘Should anything
of this kind ever occur again I shall send
for my cousiu Sir Clair Hilliard to come
and he will settle it with you, for patience
has ceased to be a virtue in this case and
Anna is too dear to me and her life is too
precious to be shattered thus mercilessly.
	Maitland’s remorse was so keen that he
begged the Doctor to let him see his wife
and ask forgiveness for the great wrong he
had done, and he would then at once seek
some sort of occupation in the colony, and
no longer remain an idler and would thus
make some amende honorable for his past,
folly.
	It was not long after this that Lady
Walton recovered for it was her mind more
than her body that was affected, though
she was indeed a frail, delicate woman.
Maitland obtained an official position as
collector of revenues for Great Britain not-
withstanding his title, he had not so much
money to support it, and he had made
up his mind to work. When he wrote his
mother the office he had accepted she
answered him disdainfully, though it was
evident from her tone that she was rather
pleased he had decided to remain away for'
the present.
	By and bye both Lord Walton and Dr.
Bland concluded to build and selected a
place not far away from the location then 
occupied. The purchasing of land and the
building of two spacious mansions afforded
some real pleasure to the ladies, and
was a source of happiness to all, perhaps
more to those English servants, who felt it
quite out of place not to have handsome
homes and elegant surroundings, and
longed for the luxuries of “dear old
England.”
	“For Master and Miss’us to be here in
this Injun country, where we might all be
chopped into mince meat and nobody the
wiser, and what would that proud Lady
Walton say! I’ll never call her by that ’talian
name', never! and our sweet Lady—bless
her, she’ll die here any way, not a thing
comfortable, and if it were not for her.
cousin she’d die anyhow, for she don’t care
a straw for anybody here, and is allays
pinin for the old castle, and what for no-
body knows, for her father was an old brute,
Baronet or not, and everybody hated him,
but she’s the dearest, sweetest cretur ever I
laid eyes on,” said the cook who had come
from Walton Hall all the way to ’Meriky
as she called it.
	Such conversation as this was common
among the old and tried servants, they hated
America and were always gossiping about
the good old times, and yet they would al-
lude now and then to the superstitions about
Walton Hall. However they were discreet
in talking to a chance acquaintance, and held
themselves far above the “hired help” of
the Puritans.


WOMAN S EXPONENT


SALT LAKE CO. W. S A.

	THE regular monthly meeting of the W.
S. A. of Salt Lake Co. convened in the
13th, Ward Assembly Rooms, Sep. 19th,
1893. President Nellie Taylor in the chair.
opened by singing “Why do we seek" &c.
Prayer by Vice President E. M. Price, sang.
“O, ye fair ones” &c. Roll called,
minutes of the previous meeting were read
and approved. President Nellie Taylor
announced that four delegates were to be
appointed for the convention to be held in
Oct. it was moved and carried that the
President appoint them accordingly Vice
President E. M. Price, Julia Druce, Vice
President E. Mc. Farlane, and President
Taylor were appointed. Vice President E.
M. Price read a very instructive and in-
teresting lecture upon silver.
	Dr. M. H. Cannon gave a recitation en-
titled “The Sioux chiefs daughter,” which
was much appreciated by those present.
	Dr. E. B. Ferguson felt willing to add
her voice in the important question of suf-
frage, that is near and dear to every wo-
man’s heart. Many evils that now exist
would certainly be quicker and more easily
remedied if once woman had the franchise.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells thought more should
interest themselves in this great and impor-
tant question, made mention of the Woman’s
Congress at Chicago, and said that suffrage
was surely advancing. Let us not allow
this subject to lie neglected and forgotten.
Meeting adjourned until the third Tuesday
in October.
				MAGGIE BASSETT, Sec.


THE regular monthly of the W. S. A.
of Salt Lake Co. was held in the 13th,
Ward Assembly Rooms, Oct. 17th, 1893.
President Nellie Taylor in the chair: opened
by singing “Our Country,” prayer by Vice
President E. McFarlane. Sang “Oh, come
come away” &c. Roll called, minutes of
the previous meeting read and approved.
	A few excellent clippings were read by
the secretary. A duet was rendered by
Mr. T. Ashworth and Mrs. Maggie Bassett.
	Mrs. May Talmage read an appropriate 
selection. Mrs. C. C. R. Wells and Mrs.
Julia Druce each spoke encouragingly.
	President Nellie Taylor recited “The
Eagle Rock or A Mother’s Love.” Re-
marks were made by Vice President E. M.
Price, Mrs. M. I. Horne, Vice President
E. McFarlane and President Nellie Taylor.
Meeting was adjourned until the third
Tuesday in November.
			MAGGIE BASSETT, Sec.


IN South Australia, full woman suf-
frage is under consideration. On Aug.
15, the Minister of Education moved the
second reading of an adult suffrage bill.
On motion of Mr. Caldwell the debate was
adjourned to Aug. 17. We shall look
with interest for further advices. At the
last accounts the debate was in progress.
—Ex.
	EXERCISES in memory of Mrs. Lucy
Stone were held at the public hour at
Chauncy Hall Wednesday morning Nov-
ember 1st: A poem by Mrs. Julia Ward
Howe was read by Blanche E. Ware, fol-
lowed by the reading of a part of “Eternal
Goodness” by Katharine A. Whiting.
Mrs. Emily A. Fifield addressed the school
on the “Life and Character of Lucy Stone,’’
—Ex.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


SOMETIME WE’LL MEET AGAIN.
	TO  E. B W.	

SOMETIME we’ll meet again dear,
	We cannot know just when,
But once again you will be near
	And I must wait till then.
We’ll joy as when we’re young dear
	And bask in summer smiles,
Nor think of winter cold and drear
	While flower and sun beguiles.

You’ll greet me as of old dear
	And I’ll remember you, 
Your presence shall uplift and cheer 
	The heart that’s ever true, 
And though the world is cold dear
	The tie which binds us two,
Shall stronger grow each year dear
	And link my soul to you.

We’ll bravely lift our eyes dear
	To future’s page unrolled,
Believing that there lies dear 
	Beyond, the Gates of Gold.
We’ll patient be and strong dear
	And hopeful every day,
We know the hour is drawing near
	And God will find the way.
Aug. 5, 1893.				E. L. G.


THE TRIALS OF COLUMBUS.

In the morning of the long night,
	Of bigotry and false learning,
When science began to shed its light,
	Over kings, and men more discerning,
God chose from the city of Genoa,
	A brave youth of humble birth,
His lands beyond the sea to explore;
	Lands of good and princely worth.
That youth, scarce higher than mother's knee;
	Treading adversities rugged path,
Pledged his life to the “Ocean Sea,”
	Contented with its love or wrath,
Imbued with the love of science,
	And thirst for geographical knowledge,
He placed in “Holy writ” his reliance,
	Surprising learned men of Pava’s College.

In the imagination of his youth,
	He pictured scenes of a world sublime;
The grandeur of which proved the truth,
	Of a scheme that grew with time,
For years, twice nine and more,
	While sunny Spain lay half asleep,
He toiled for the Western Shore;
	The mystic lands beyond the deep,
Kings wondered, wise men scoffed, 
	At the bold Explorer’s great theme
Of discovery, the whole world laughed
	As if it was some mad dream.
Cast down by poverty’s long defeat,
	He wandered, hungry and forsaken,
From Courts, to La Rabida’s Retreat,
	His faith in foreign lands unshaken,
Proving to his cause true and just,
	From the fair Isabella of God elected, 
He received, at last the needed trust,
	By foolish kings, so long rejected
Seeing afar, with wondrous knowledge,
	The truth of “Colons” scheme, she declared,
“For love of Castile, my jewels will pledge.”
	Thus the brave Queen, discovery’s hope shared
And with womanly courage untold,
	Drew from Aragon’s drained coffers
The needed thousands of precious gold,
	That silenced forever, Columbus scoffers,
When the explorer’s youth was dimmed,
	By the frost of half a century’s winds,
The Santa Maria, was fitted and trimmed,


For discovery’s voyage over the brines, 
Thus, with souls an hundred and more,
	The ships brave and proud commander,
Sailed the quiet or stormy seas o’er,
	Trusting, in a new world to land her,
Near the lands of wealth uncoined,
 	In Salvador’s early dawn of dew,
The last link of discovery’s chain joined,
	Binding the old world to the new.
						   L.S. Dickinson.
Salt Lake, July 4th, 1892.


U. W. P. CLUB.
	THE U. W. P. C. met as usual October
31st, with an extra number of mem-
bers in attendance, Mrs. E. B. Wells, the
President for the past two years, in the
chair.
	After an opening prayer by Mrs. Zina D.
H. Young the reading of minutes and roll
call, the Halloween program was carried
out as follows.
	Mrs. Ella W. Hyde read a paper stating
the Origin and Meaning of the observance,
which dated back to a very early period.
A number of the members gave interesting
descriptions of the superstitious rites and
ceremonies of the harvest festival which is
celebrated, not only in Scotland, but in
England and America alike, and many
pretty festive home pictures were presented
to the imagination of those present.
	Mrs. Phebe C. Young read Burns’ poem
on the subject. Miss Pearl Russell
favored the club with a vocal selection, fol-
lowed by Miss Belle Salmon who recited a
Scotch selection and when encored respond-
ed with that most-humorous selection “The
Mouse.” Mrs. Z. D. H. Young told an
interesting Halloween story in which the
magic or charm practiced really ended
propitiously for the parties concerned.
“Alonzo the Brave.” was read by Mrs.
Lizzie S. Wilcox. Mrs. E. B. Wells read
a paper relating to the ancient Druids and
Druidesses and their observance of the 31st
of October; it being the eve on which all
fires and lights were extinguished to be
rekindled from the sacred fire at the Altar
on the payment of the yearly tithes or dues
exacted by the priests. Other stories of
various degrees of interest and weird-
ness were read or related as befitting the
evening and occasion. Miss Maude Pratt
then favored the club with instrumental 
music.
	Refreshments were served during a short
intermission, after which the annual election
of officers was effected by ballot. Miss
Patterson introduced a resolution to the
effect that Mrs. E. B. Wells who had so
efficiently filled the position of President,
for the past two years, and who had been
ever foremost in promoting the interests of
the club, should be sustained as honorary
President for life. The question was put
and the resolution unanimously carried.
Mrs. Wells made a short but fitting speach
in which she thanked the members, Miss
Patterson especially, for the honor shown
her and the confidence reposed in her. The
reading of the votes by the Secretary show-
ed the following officers elected.
	President, Ellis R. Shipp, 1st Vice, Prest.
Ruth M. Fox, 2nd Vice Prest, Mary A. Freeze
Rec. Sec. Gladys Woodmansee Asst. Rec.
Sec’y, Ella W. Hyde Cor. Secy, Romania
B. Pratt, Asst, Cor Sec’y, Lydia D. Alder,
Treasurer C. C. R. Wells, program Com-
mittee, Chairman,, Lizzie S. Wilcox, Ada
Patterson, Phebe C. Young. Vice presi-


dents at large A. J. Crocheron, J. A.
McDonald, L. L. Dalton and A. E. Ben-
nion, M. E. Shipp Auditor.
	GLADYS WOODMANSEE, Rec. Secy.


GEMS SELECTED.
	IN pastures green? Not always; some-
times He who knoweth best in kindness
leadeth me in weary way where heavy
shadows be; only for this I know, He
holds my hand, so whether led in green or
desert land I trust, although I may not un-
derstand, I oft would faint with sorrow and
affright. But He is near, and by “still
waters”—no not always so, oftimes the
heavy tempests round me blow, and o’er
my soul the waves and billows go. But
when the storm beats loudest, and I cry
aloud for help, the Master standeth by, and
whispers to my soul, “Lo, it is I.” Above
the tempest wild I hear him say; “Beyond
this darkness lies the perfect day in every
path of thine I lead the way.” So,
whether on the hilltops high and fair I
dwell, or in the sunless valley where the
shadows lie—what matter? He is there,
so, where he leads me, I, can safely go, and
in the blest hereafter I shall know why in
His wisdom He hath lead me so."
	“Father, I do not ask that thou wilt
choose some other task and make it mine.
I pray but this, let every day be molded
by thine own hand; my will be only thine.
Let me not simply do, but be content, sure
that the little crosses each are sent, and no
mistakes can ever be, with thine own hand
to choose for me.

Do thy duty; that is best, leave unto thy Lord the
	rest.
"lt is easy enough to be pleasant
	When life flows by like a song,
But the man worth while is one who will smile
	When everthing goes dead wrong.” 

	“The good are better made by ill
	As odors crushed are sweeter still”

	“Lost, somewhere between sunrise and
sunset, two golden hours, each set with
sixty diamond minutes.”
							E. H. LYON.


REORGANIZATION.
REORGANIZATION of the Relief Society
of Granite Ward Oct. 18th, 1893. Meet-
ing called to order by acting President
Catharine M. Staker, Singing “Let those
who would be Saints indeed.” Prayer by
Sister Staker, singing “Now let us rejoice.”
Roll called—minutes of the previous meet-
ing read and accepted.
	The Bishop being present took charge of
the business of the meeting.
	Sister Catharine M. Staker was chosen to
act as President of the society. Sister An-
nie E. Hanson and Sister Susan Despain
were chosen Counselors. These sisters
were unanimously sustained to fill the
above named offices and were set apart to
act therein; good counsel and encouraging
remarks were received from the Bishopric
and other brethren. 
	Our society is not what we might call
first class as yet, owing to a great many dis-
advantages, but we hope to make it such as
soon as possible. Meeting was adjourned
for three weeks, singing “We thank thee
O, God for a prophet.”  Benediction by
Bishop Alva Butler.
			ANNIE B. DESPAIN, Sec.
Wasatch Oct. 1893.

econd class matter.
    
SALT LAKE CITY, NOVEMBER 15, 1893.


[Column 1]
A TRIBUTE TO LUCY STONE.
	IN the death of Lucy Stone all advocates
and ardent workers for the enfranchisement
of woman must indeed feel they have lost a
friend and fellow laborer, whose strength
and individual effort was felt wherever her
name was spoken. Always logical, clear-
headed and intelligent in all her arguments
she convinced her audiences where many
more flowery speakers failed to make a last-
ing impression. She had a remarkable face,
full of kindness and true mother-love, and
this not only for her own but for the world
of mankind, the intense love and sympathy
she carried into her work wrought marvels
in the cause, to which she devoted her life,
her talents and her energies with untiring
diligence; and in which she succeeded so
admirably, not only in furthering the high-
er progress of women and of men, but
also in making friends of the opponents of
woman’s suffrage where great and wise
men failed. To Lucy Stone women owe
much of the present popularity of the
suffrage sentiment.
	The newspapers of the East and North
abound with beautiful testimonials of the
pure, true, womanly life of this friend of
humanity. Boston, and the state of Massa-
chussetts may well be justly proud of Lucy
Stone. No doubt a fitting monument of
marble will yet be erected in remembrance
of her faithful service. As a brilliant
example of heroism and meritorious work,
perserving under perplexing difficulties,
and rising superior to all obstacles that
opposed her onward way,her life is a fitting
illustration; the young women of the
present day, have had the way to success
in many directions, in a great measure
cleared of thorns and briers by such
brave women as Lucy Stone. As long as
we have known of woman suffrage and
even before we heard the sound,
when the anti-slavery work was in
progress, we can recall hearing the name of
this woman among women.
	Judge how gladly the writer embraced the 
opportunity of visiting her in her own
home when an invitation was given her, in
the winter of 1886 while spending a few
days in the grand old city of Boston.
Previously several letters had been ex-
changed between the editor of the Woman's
Journal and the writer, and we took the
liberty of making a fraternal call at the
Journal office Park Street, where we met
Mr. Blackwell. Subsequently it was ar-
ranged that we should go out to the home

[Column 2]
of the Blackwells and spend a few hours:
accordingly in company with Mr. Blackwell
we took the old colony R. R. to Neponset.
It was winter time and a sleigh was in
waiting at the depot and driving swiftly
and talking as we went up a steep ascent
Pope’s Hill, we soon reached the handsome
residence of the celebrated woman, whom
we were so eager to see. Standing on the
piazza to welcome us was Lucy Stone her-
self, the sun was just setting in the West
and the glory of that sunset was heightened
by the beautiful light in her face, and the
warm welcome to her stranger guest was
like a benediction. Once inside the cheerful
fire burning brightly we sat and talked of
this and that, recalling things familiar to
one born and bred in New England,and we
felt almost akin when told she was born in
Brookfield the same town where our own
dear mother was born and that we knew so
well. Other subjects such as woman’s
progress in the West, the practical workings
of the ballot for women in Utah which was
then enjoyed, the hopes we entertained of
future advancement etc. In all these and
kindred topics her daughter Alice Stone
Blackwell joined and the time flew on the
wings of the wind. The conversation was
so entertaining that it was difficult to break
off, though we had dined sumptuously and
the hour was growing late. The hostess
showed us her desk where she wrote those
strong and forcible editorials and remarkable
paragraphs that were often silent forces in
remote corners of the world, and as we sat
facing each other at each side of the open
grate just before parting, she said how strange
that two so widely separated, by the whole
width of continent nearly from the Atlantic
almost to the Pacific should meet as ’twere
casually and have so much in common to
talk about.
	Since then we have met several times,and
at the Woman’s Congress in Chicago last
summer she seemed as likely to live for
many years, and looked as fresh and rosy as
most of the younger women. But no doubt
her work here was done and she has passed
to another sphere, and doubtless to solve
some higher problems, leaving her daughter
and others to carry upward and onward the
glorious structure for which she laid so firm
and fair a foundation.
	Honor and praise to the heroine Lucy
Stone, her very name is a talisman of good
and her writings and sayings are an imper-
shable monument to her blessed memory.


EDITORIAL NOTES.
THE good news from Colorada that the
vote of the state for woman’s suffrage was
6,000 majority comes like good tidings to
the country, and presages the good time
when political equality will no longer be a
by word and a jest, but an acknowledged
fact the civilized world over.

	We wish to call the attention of our
many readers to the display advertisment of
the Dunford Shoe Co. which appears in
this issue of the EXPONENT.  They have
just moved from their old location on first
South St. to the elegant store of Spencer
Clawson’s No. 57 S. Main St., half a block
north of Deseret National Bank.  Every-
thing in the line of footwear is carried by
this popular house and the prices are such 1
as to be in the reach of all  "Style, fit and
durability” at hard time prices is the motto
at Dunford’s shoe store. Ladies will please


take notice this business is conducted by a
woman and should therefore receive their
patronage.


WOMAN’S FOREIGN AFFAIRS.
	[REPORT OF THE CONFERENCE OF THE
RELIEF SOCIETY OF THE HAWAIIAN
ISLANDS, HELD AT HONOLULU, SEP. 30TH,
1893.]
	CONTRARY to the usual custom, the
October Conference of the Relief Society, 
Sunday School and Improvement Associa-
tion were held in Honolulu together with
the Honolulu Branch Conference, instead
of at Laie; these Conferences were held a
week earlier than usual, in order to give
the Missionaries and the Saints time to re-
turn to Laie for general Conference. The
object in this change was to give those of
the Honolulu saints as well as many from
the other Islands, who have not animals or
conveyances to come to Laie, an op-
portunity of attending Conference and of
meeting with and listening to the teachings
of the Elders in a body.
	There was quite a family of us when we
were gathered together in our cosy little
Mission House at Honolulu, twenty in
number, and although somewhat crowded,
yet we felt happy and joyous and full of
the spirit of our mission.
	Saturday morning, Sep. 30th, at ten
o’clock the Relief Society of the Islands
convened in Conference, President Libbie
Noal presiding. After the usual opening
exercises, the reports for the last half year
were read by Secretary Kekuewa Nakuan
showing a membership of 498; number of
meetings held 337;  Receipts $297.45,
Disbursements $113.15;  Amount remain-
ing in Treasury $184.30.
	There were in all seventeen branches re-
ported, ten less than reported at our last
Conference, this deficiency is owing to the
fact that, through the moving about of the
Saints, many of the branches which other-
wise would be in a flourishing condition,
have not sufficient numbers to warrant an
organization.
	President Libbie Noal first addressed the
Conference; thanking the sisters for the
cordial reception extended, to the visiting
saints, for the interest manifested in the
work, as shown by the large attendance,
and for the spirit of love and good will
apparent in the hearts of the saints for the
Missionaries and for each other; dwelt up-
on the duties and obligations resting upon
the sisters as handmaids of the Lord; ex-
plained in detail many points relative to the
carrying on of the work of the organiza-
tion, that all might labor together as a unit,
and conduct their labors in a business-like
way.
	After listening to the verbal reports of
the condition of the various societies by
their respective “eleles,” or representatives,
several of the native sisters addressed the
Conference upon subjects previously as-
signed them and they handled them in a
very creditable and intelligent manner.
“The Life of Hannah, Mother of Samuel”
by Kaluapalaoa; “Mary, mother of Jesus”
by Kahuhu; “Life of Mary and Martha,
sisters of Lazarus,” by Niaulani.  “The
Woman of Samaria” by Ruth Kauahine.
Original song for the occasion by Kekuewa
Nakuan. Sister Maggie Davis spoke at
some length, explaining the object of the
society, the benefits derived from such an
organization and encouraging the sisters in


their labors. Sister Nellie Grace read an
essay upon the past, present and future of
Woman. Sisters Davis, Grace and Presi-
dent M. Noal favored the sisters with a
song, which they seemed to heartily enjoy.
The choir rendered a selection after each
speaker, which gave life and spirit to our
meeting.
	At the conclusion of the programme,
President M. Noal presented the names of
the general authorities of the Relief Society
before the Conference, all of whom were
unanimously sustained.
	Sister Libbie Noal was sustained as
President of the Relief Society of the
Hawaiian Islands, with all of the Mission-
ary sisters as her counselors, and with
Kekuewa, Kahuhu, Kaluapalaoa, Niaulani
and Luka as Aids, Kekuewa as Recording
Secretary with Laeka as her assistant, Nel-
lie Grace, Corresponding Secretary and M.
Noal Treasurer. At the close of our Con-
ference, we adjourned to the basement of
the Meeting House, when we were bedeck-
ed with two and three beautiful flower
“Leis," and where we found a grand
Hawaiian Feast prepared for us, consisting
of poi, beef, pork and chicken, several
varities of fish, kalo, sweet potatoes, bread,
cake of different kinds, oranges, melons,
banannas, pineapples and soda-water, to all
of which we did ample justice, not forget-
ting to appreciate the love and respect
which prompted the saints of Honolulu to
thus look after our comfort and welfare
in such a substantial manner. Through
the labors of the missionary sisters, a
circular has been prepared, which is to be
distributed among the different branches
of the society containing general
instruction and directions, pertaining to the
duties of the different officers and which
will serve as a guide for the government
and managment of the respective branches
of the society; this will supply a want long
needed, as many of the organizations have
never been visited personally by the mis-
sionary sisters, although letters of counsel
and instruction have been sent out to every
branch upon the Islands.
	As a band of sisters, we are united and
happy in our labors here, and our hearts
desire is, that the Lord will bless us that
we may be useful instruments in his hands
in helping to better the condition of our
native sisters upon these Islands; that by
precept and example we may implant in
their hearts a desire to aspire to something
higher and nobler than they have yet at-
tained, and that they may feel they have
been made better by our presence among
them.
	Mrs. Nellie Grace, Cor. Sec.


WOMANS EXPONENT.


UTAH COUNTY W. S. A.
 
	THE annual convention of the Utah
county branch of the Territorial Woman’s
Suffrage association will be held in the
Provo meeting house on Thursday, Nov-
vember 23, 1893.  The executive session
will convene at 10 o’clock a. m.  The con-
vention proper will commence at 2 p. m.
The Territorial president Mrs. E. B. Wells,
will be here, also delegates from the various
organizations of the county.  All are
cordially invited.

	Mrs. ELECTA BULLOCK, President.
	REBECCA H. DOOLAN, Secretary


LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	MINUTES of the Semi-monthly meeting
held in the 14th, Ward Assembly Rooms,
Oct. 21, 1893.  President M. I. Horne
presiding.  Opened by singing "O, say
what is truth” &c. Prayer by Sister
Julia Pack continued by singing “Our
God we raise to thee" &c.
	Minutes of last meeting read and ap-
proved, minutes of a concert held in the
15th, ward also those of a Primary held
there, read by a little girl.
	President Horne said she was pleased
with the minutes read by the little girl and
glad to hear the young voice in our midst.
	President Horne spoke of the death of
Sister Fletcher, President of the Relief
Society of the 8th ward, said she had been
a true and faithful Latter-day saint, a good
worker and had discharged every duty,
that had devolved upon her and had gone
to her reward, said she would come forth in
the morning of the first resurrection
crowned with honor and glory.  I have
thought much upon the sphere of woman
her mission and duties.
	The prophet Joseph when he sent the
missionaries out to preach the Gospel,
told them not to pull a man’s house down,
until they had built him a better one.  Now
have thought much on some women, who
want positions and do not appreciate being a
wife and mother.
	Woman should make her home happy
and beautify it, also take care of the little
ones who are given unto us.  Who fashions
the little garments, who makes the clothing
to lay out the dead, who sew the veil of the
Temple?  Do not women do all these
things?  We should meet together and have
our minds enlarged, and become more in-
telligent, so that we could perform all these
labors better.
	Our duties are not inferior to man’s, but
our natures are different and our missions
are different.  Do not women bear the
souls of men?  The highest mission that
can be given.  Is not her kingdom her
home? To keep that home orderly and
well needs as much intelligence as any one
can attain to.  She is a helpmate to her
husband, and all honor to her in her sphere.
	Remember these things sisters and let us
teach our daughters to be self-reliant and
industrious.  President Horne gave other
wise counsel, and prayed that the spirit of
God would be with us.
	Sister Hurst of Pleasant Green, bore a
faithful testimony to the truth of the Gos-
pel and desired to be faithful and true.
	Sister E. Stevenson, said I feel to say
God bless Sister Horne, she is a noble wo-
man and a faithful mother. I feel to endorse
what she has said to day.  Let us teach our
children to seek unto God, in their selec-
tions in marriage we can be so tried in
this way.  Let us adorn our spiritual
natures and be partakers of the Spirit of
God &c.
	Sister Mary P. Silver announced that the
sister who had been emigrated from the old
world had arrived, and was glad to be in
Zion.
	Sister Minnie Cook spoke of the wondeful
dealings of God to her and felt to praise
His Holy name.
	Sister M. L. Morris bore her testimony
and spoke of the many duties devolving up-
on a woman, added other good remarks.
	Sang Doxology, meeting was adjourned
for two weeks.  Benediction by Counselor
Webb. 	L. D. ALDER, Acting Sec.


R. S. REPORTS.

UTAH STAKE.

	MINUTES of Conference of Relief Society
of Utah Stake held in Payson City meeting
house Oct. 13, 1893. The Stake Presi-
dency of the Relief Society on the stand
also Sister E. B. Wells from Salt Lake
City Conference opened with singing and
prayer, reading of minutes of former Con-
ference etc.
	The President Sister John felt pleased to
meet in Conference with the sisters of Pay-
son, thought it a good plan to appoint our
Conferences around in the different settle-
ments, many were enabled thereby to
attend conference, who could not otherwise
do so; the sisters are faithful in helping the
poor, and in gathering means for that pur-
pose.
	We have Sister Wells with us today, and
many Presidents and will now hear from
them.
	President Pickard of Springville, Presi-
dent Colvin of Payson, President Stickney
of Santaquin, gave very favorable and
encouraging reports of their wards, all were
united in trying to do all the good they
could.
	Sister E. B. Wells the next speaker said,
“Sister Zina very much wished to be with
us but other duties prevented her, she de-
sires your faith and prayers, she needs your
faith and prayers just as much as the
brethren do, all you who have read Brother
Joseph’s remarks at the Relief Society know
what he said on that subject, of sustaining
with your faith and prayers those who are
called to lead. I have felt gratified in hear-
ing the reports read. I love to hear of the 
good work our sisters are doing, and it
pleases me to hear of our sisters saving
grain as they have been counseled to do,
am pleased to hear of the sisters building
houses to meet in.” spoke about the grain
that was being laid up thought that it be-
longed to the Lord, and the Church and
was for the poor, and the needy, and not
for any particular society, but would be
distributed as directed by the Presidency;
no matter who the needy were, whether
they were saint or not, if they were needy,
we must be charitable, she was very glad
to meet with us here in Payson.
	After. singing and prayer Conference ad-
journed until half past one p. m.
Sister Marilla Daniels spoke on various
subjects of interest such as storing our
grain, the good instructions that were given
us at Conference etc., etc., thought the
storing of grain was a word of warning to
the people, and we must give heed to it,
and take care of it, spoke of Dr. Barney’s
method of saving flour by packing it in oil
cans and sealing it up air tight etc. She
bore her testimony to the truth of this
work; we are here in the fulfillment of
prophesy, let us keep the spirit of God and
we will be all right.
	Sister John felt it a privilege to belong to
the Relief Society, it is a school for us, it
unites us with each other, we become ac-
quainted with each other by meeting to-
gether in these conferences, thought this
Stake was progressing in good works, ad-
vised the sisters to store up wheat, and be
faithful in all the duties devolving upon us,
tried to impress upon the sisters the idea of
all being Suffragists etc.
	Sister Wells spoke again for a short time,
on the importance of saving our grain, after
taking care of our poor, thought the teach


ers had very responsible positions teaching
the sisters temporally as well as spiritually,
said try to cheer and comfort them, do not
talk much about hard times, we do not
know much about hard times, but when she
thought of the hand-cart companies she
could not help shedding tears, “we are not
poor we are rich.” Sister Wells spoke of
the Silk exhibit from Utah, at the World’s
Fair, and of the notice it attracted, and of
the beautiful curtains sent from Utah being
on exhibition in a glass case, advised the
Sisters to raise cocoons and make silk, we
can make silk dresses, curtains, ribbons, etc.
if we will only work for it. President
Young said the air was fnll of silk, spoke
of the exertions of Brother Graves in trying
to get that industry started, said we must
promote home industries, we must sustain
our own industries and employ our own
people, and there will be plenty of money
soon, but we are very extravagant as a
people, in New England they are more
saving than we are. “Let us cultivate
kindness to each other, and not despise any
one that cannot dress as well as we can,
the Lord’s hand has been over us for good,
we have been preserved from storms and
cyclones, and we as a people should be
very grateful to our Heavenly Father for
his protecting care which hath been over
us.”
	After singing and benediction conference
adjourned for three months.
		Mrs. Caroline Daniels, Secretary.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


STAR VALLEY STAKE.
	The first Relief Society conference of'
the Star Valley Stake convened at Afton
Wyoming Monday, June 12, 1893, at 10
a. m. President Kittie E. Dixon presid-
ing. Meeting commenced by singing,
prayer by Prest.  A. V. Call, singing by
the choir. Opening remarks appropriate
to the occasion by Prest. Kittie Dixon. The
branches of the society were then represen-
ted as follows:
	Afton, Prest. Harriet Cazier, Grover,
Hannah Hepworth, Auburn, Catharine 
Holbrook, Glencoe, Elizabeth Turner.
All were represented as in good order.
The statistical and financial reports were
then read. Counselor S. A. Burton made
a few remarks in which she gave good in-
structions. Meeting adjourned by singing,
benediction by Secretary Lucy Call.
	Afternoon Session. Meeting opened by
singing, prayer by Sister Cazier, Singing
by the choir, Prest. Childs of Fairview, and
Sec. E. Walton of Cotton-Wood then re-
ported their wards as in good condition.
Some of the Sisters of the various wards
then made some very instructive remarks.
One of the Stake Presidency gave some
good counsel and encouraged the Sisters to
press on in the good cause in which they 
were engaged. Prest. Kittie Dixon made
a few closing remarks. Conference then
adjourned for six months. Singing. Bene-
diction by Bishop C. D. Cazier.
Kittie E. DIXON President.
Sarah A. Burton, 
Jane Parson, Counselors.
Lucy Call, Secretary.


CASSIA STAKE.
The conference of the Relief Society of
Cassia Stake convened at Oakley Aug. 18,
1893. On the stand were Prest. Louisa


Haight and counselors with the ward
presidents.
	The conference was opened by singing
and prayer; every ward represented and re-
ported in a favorable condition. Counselor
Helen E. Tuttle addressed the assembly,
spoke about forgiveness, and the necessity
of prayer. An article from the Exponent
was read “The World’s Congress of
Women.’, Coun. Ann Wilson said we
should do our duties td the best of our
ability, she felt to press on by the help of
the Lord. Brother Cyrus Tolman spoke in
regard to our doing work for the dead, and
preparing ourselves to be worthy to meet
those who had gone behind the vail. Ad-
journed until 2 p. m.
	Afternoon session. Opened with the
usual exercises and reading of Semi-
Annual reports of the branches of the Re-
lief Society. Sister Ann Bird expressed
herself in regard to our financial condition,
trials were to purify us, we must have charity
and love for each other. Brother Moroni
Pickett addressed the conference, spoke of
the organizations of women, and their
duties women have been secondary until
the present, they are to walk side by side
with their husbands what is man’s interest
is woman’s interest.
	Sister Sarah Worthington bore a faithful
testimony. Prest. H. D. Haight said. It is
encouraging to see so many here and to
hear the instructions we have had, spoke of
our financial affairs.
	Prest. Louisa Haight spoke of hard times
and the necessity of storing up grain.
General and Stake officers sustained.
Conference adjourned for three  months. 
  	Lucynthia Robbins, Sec.


[Column 1]
MESA ITEMS.

Editor Exponent.
	DEAR SISTER.:—I have often thought as
my mind reverted to the scenes I passed
through in the city, that I would like to
pen a few lines in regard to our condition
and prospects in this far off sun-kissed land,
although we are behind hand in our Con-
ference reports,—I am happy to say that
our Relief Society in the several wards is
still alive, and doing a good work,consider-
ing the number who are really interested in
it and I hope and trust there will always
be a faithful few, energetic sisters who will
strive to keep the car moving and will
never slacken their efforts for good, how-
ever discouraging the prospects are. There
has been quite a number of changes made
in the officers of the wards and Stake, the
nature of which I presume has been re-
ported by the proper officers. We fully
realize that the race is not to the swift, nor
the battle to the strong, but to those
who hold out faithful to the end, this the
majority of us are trying to do.
	The Mesa Society has a hall which the sisters
have reason to feel proud of. It was built
by the united efforts of our society with
some backing from our good brethren. It
is built of brick and neatly finished and
furnished. Its originial cost was $150.00
but lately they have had some repainting,
and furnishing, costing about $70.00 more.
It was dedicated by Apostle J. H. Smith,
and since then the sweet peaceful inspiration
of the Spirit of God, has met with us in all
our meetings, many have remarked that
this influence was felt as soon as they
entered the hall. However we feel truly
grateful, that we have been blessed with

[Column 2]
means to enable us to accomplish this much;
of course the building of it has kept our
treasury, pretty well drained; but we felt
that we were putting our means to good
use, and we were obeying the counsel of
our leaders; I remember the last Relief
Society Conference I attended in the
Assembly Hall in Salt Lake, Sister Zina
Young laid great stress upon this subject,
advising the sisters not to hoard up their
means to make a large showing, but after
the sick and needy were cared for to take
their surplus means and build them halls for
meeting purposes. I can say with pride
that our society has filled its mission faith-
fully in caring for the sick in our midst,
frequently hiring experienced nurses to
care for those who were not able to help
themselves, and also in assisting those
faithful sisters in the Indian Mission, per-
haps a few words in regard to this mission
would not be out of place. Sister Susan
Savage is President of the Relief Society
of the Papago Ward which is a mixture of
Papago Maricopa and Pimo tribes. She
has Sisters Tiffany and Webb for Counselors
also Sister Susan Brady to assist them.
I don’t believe there was ever a band of
missionaries sent to the nations of the earth,
who were more anxious to do good and fill
their missions faithfully than these sisters.
	They (the natives) have great faith in the
healing power and when they are sick they
frequently send for those sisters to
administer to them. Sister Savage and
counselors, with characteristic energy, have
started a manuscript paper amongst them
inviting the Stake sisters to contribute
articles that will be best suited to their un-
derstandings. Sister Savage has through
faith and prayer succeeded in gaining a
knowledge of the language, sufficient to
interpret to their understanding what is
written which pleases them and is of great
benefit to them. They have a fine choir of
beautiful voices and sing from the L. D. S.
Hymn Book. Sister Thankful Harmon
(now deceased) was very energetic in train-
ing them in their singing exercises and was
missed very much when she died about a
year ago.
	Perhaps I have extended this article too
much already, use it as seemeth thee good!
With good wishes for all Zion’s Workers.
I am as ever your friend, S. M. POMEROY.


VANITY.

	WE are told by an ancient Apostle that
we are subject to vanity as the sparks that
fly upward; this being the case would it
not be well to cultivate a sound mind a
sincere and devoted heart, that we may not
lose the prize of our high calling through
vanity, for this being called the spice of
life, is there not danger that we may not
lose the prize of our high calling through
vanity, for this being called the spice of
life, is there not danger that we flavor our
lives or character too much with these spices
so that the simple food so necessary to sus-
tain life becomes to a degree hurtful, losing
the nutritious quality which the plain food
would give us.
	To a hard student who is making it his
object and aim to arrive at some intelligence
which he greatly desires, he sometimes
needs a little recreation or amusement to
give his mind a rest, then he takes hold
again with more vigor and a joyful anticipa-
tion of gaining the victory eventually when
he has exercised and developed the abilities
God has given him. 
	But, how is it with those who have no
object of this kind, and only seem to live


here for the purpose of enjoying all that is
within their reach, as it is written. “Live
while you live” the Epicure will say “and
seize the pleasure of the present day.”
While another person cries, “Live while 
you live”, the sacred preacher cries, “and
give to God each moment as its flies.”
	We find many in these days are like the
Antideluvians were in the days of Noah.
Would they listen to his warning voice,
Oh, no! They feel to say, how does Noah
know all this? Why does not the Lord
speak to us if this be true?
	But did they seek to become acquainted
with their Heavenly Father while their
minds are so full of vanity is there any
room there for His good spirit? Could He
speak to one whose spirit is exactly op-
posite to His own; would it make an im-
pression if the Lord should speak to them?
I think not. But when this calamity
came upon them then they realized the
truth of Noah’s words: a subject they had
no time or disposition to listen to then, but
now can both see and feel the importance
of the same.
	But this is not all. Now I would ask
how did they feel when the Messiah came
into the prison as we read in first Epistle of
Peter 3rd,chapter 19 and 20 verses. They re-
joiced no doubt, because they had heard of
a Messiah who was to come to redeem a
fallen world and now he is here in prison
with them: no doubt he came to do us
good as Moses and all the prophets have
testified He would do: they could then
listen to them. Just fancy the joy they
felt in seeing the Lord of Heaven and
earth come to visit them. Do not you
think they had faith that this visit would
result in some good to them being a part of
the human family also. O yes, and the
Lord also had compassion. He who when
on the cross said, “Father forgive them for
they know not what they do” would surely
feel for those who were drowned by the
flood.  He told his disciples what they
must do to be saved, for faith without
works was dead.
	You know that He told Nicodemus he
must be born again to enter into his king-
dom, yes be born of water and the spirit or
he could not see the kingdom of God. Now
could the Antideluvians do this? Their
spirits were separated from the body, yes
their bodies were here with mother earth.
	Well what do we read in Acts 1st, chapter
3rd, verse, we read that Jesus was on the
earth forty days making arrangements for
the salvation of the human family. He
told his disciples to wait at Jerusalem until
they received the Holy Ghost and then
ascended into heaven in their sight. We
read in Book of Mormon He visited the
Nephites on this continent after His
resurrection, and taught them how to serve
God acceptably by the sermon on the mount,
and established the same organization that
he did at Jerusalem, &c.
	Now we see that baptism for the dead
was an established rule after his resurrection,
This being an important lesson can not we
lay aside vanity while we study such lessons
as these. We find much to say if we study
such lessons as this. We find much to say
if we study it properly. Your friend,
	ZION'S CONVERT.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT,


[Column 1]
THE WAR OF THE REBELLION 
		 (Concluded.)
	IN the meantime a vast army had been
collected near Washington under General

[Column 2]
McClellan, the object of which was to cap-
ture the Confederate capital. Early in March
an advance was ordered.
	At Fair Oaks McClellan’sarmy was attack-
ed May 31st, a fearful but indecisive battle
ensued. General Joseph E. Johnston, the
Confederate General-in-chief, having been
severely wounded, was relieved from duty
and'General Lee appointed to the command.
General Jackson popularly known as“Stone-
wall Jackson,” having defeated or eluded
the Union Armies in the Shenandoah val-
ley, joined Lee.
		MOTTO OF JACKSON.
	“We are now,” he wrote, ‘‘in a state of
war which will yield to nothing. The
whole south is in a state of revolution into
which Virginia after a long struggle has
been drawn; and though I recognize no
necessity for this state of things, and would
have forborne and pleaded to the end for
redress of grievances, real or supposed, yet
in my own person I had to test the question
whether I should take part against
my native state. With all my devotion to
the Union and the feeling of loyalty and
duty of an American citizen—I have not
been able to make up my mind, to raise my
hand against my relatives, my children, my
home. I have therefore resigned my com-
mission in the army, and, save in defeuce
of my native state with the sincere hope,
that my poor services may never be called
upon to draw my sword.’’
	At this time there were three armies in
the vicinity of Washington, one under the
command of Freemont, another of Banks—
and the third of McDowell. These were
united, forming one body, called the army
of Virginia. Pope, at his own request
having been relieved McClellan was again
intrusted with the command of the Federal
forces in and around Washington, the Con-
federate General had supposed that large
re-enforcements would flock to him in
Maryland, but in this he was dissappointed,
McClellan following Lee the invaders were
overtaken at South mountain, and though
they were defeated in the battle that ensued,
Sep. 14th, Harper’s Ferry with its garrison
of more than eleven thousand men, and its
vast munitions of war, was surren-
dered to a division of their army on the
following morning.
	Long before the next day broke, the na-
tional troops rising from their rest on the
bare ground, made ready their coffee, and
eating their simple breakfast, prepared
for a renewal of battle. They believed
that Lee had no escape. The river was at
his back. All day passed and a second
wearisome night and then there was news.
Lee had given McClellan the slip he had
actually crossed the Potomac unmolested,
and escaped into Virginia.” Lee’s army,
though foiled and compelled to retreat, won
commendation from foes as well as friends.
	“It had fought its way to the Potomac,
crossed the stream, met the Union troops
in two heavy engagements, fought the bat-
tle of Antietam—the greatest pitched bat-
tle of the war—and then recrossed the
Potomac, back into Virginia.
	During all this time, covering the full
space of a month, these troops rested but
four days. And let it be always remembered
to their honor, that of the men who per-
formed this wonderful achievement, one
fifth were barefoot one half in rags and
the whole half famished. ”
	McClellan remained in Maryland till

[Column 3]
about the first of November, when he
crossed the Potomac, but President Lincoln
was dissatisfied with his inactivity, ap-
pointed General Burnside in his stead.
Burnside, believing that the true line of
operations against Richmond was the direct
one,” advanced to the Rappohannock, op-
posite  Fredericksburg. Lee anticipating
Burnside’s movements, had meanwhile
marched along the south side of the river
to Fredericksburg while the Union troops
were waiting for pontoons to cross the river.
Lee improved the time by planting
batteries on the hill in the rear of the city.
The pontoons finally arrived and the river
crossed, but the heroic attempt to carry the
batteries were repulsed with terrible loss.
Thirteen thousand men of Burnside’s army
fell, and he was compelled to recross the
river. Money was easy enough to satisfy
everybody. The financial system adopted
by the Confederate Government consisted
in the issue of treasury notes enough to
meet the expenses of the Government.
Coffee was bought at forty dollars and tea
at thirty dollars a pound. A Southern
writer says ‘‘before the war I went to
market with the money in my pocket and
brought back my purchases in a basket, but
now I take the money in a basket, and
bring the things home in my pocket. ” A
cavalry officer, entering a little country store
found a pair of boots which fitted him,
he asked the price, ‘‘Two hundred dol-
lars” said the merchant, A five hundred 
dollar bill, was offered, but the merchant 
having no smaller bills could not change it.
	‘‘Never mind said the cavalier, I’ll take
the boots anyhow keep the change. I
never let a little matter of three hundred
dollars stand in the way of a trade.” 
	In the third year of the war, President 
Lincoln said, ‘‘My object is to save the
Union and not either to save or destroy
slavery.”
	‘‘If I could save the Union without freeing,
any slaves, I would do so. If I could save it
by freeing all the slaves I would do so, or by
freeing some and leaving others I would do
that.” Events controlled him. Then
“black regiments,” began to organize to
fight in the national service. At the close
of the year fifty thousand colored men were
in actual service.
	In the third year of the war President
Lincoln by an act of Congress recruited
the army by drafting. This caused great
excitement in New York a riot broke out
which lasted four days and was only put
down by the most determined effort of the
police and the military. (The battle of
Chickarauga.)
	The Union forces driven behind the in-
trencliments of Chattanooga. With the
Confederates holding the surrounding
hills, Lookout Mountain and Missionary
ridge—their position was critical indeed. 
	They were also in danger of starvation,
the “mud was so deep” wrote one of the
soldiers that we could not travel by the
road, but we got along pretty well by step-
ping from mule to mule as they lay dead from
starvation by the way. Still General
Thomas telegraphed “I will hold fast till 
all starve.” It was evident that something
must be done at once and quickly. Rein-
forcements under Hooker were therefore
rushed by rail to the aid of the troops.
Grant was ordered to take the command.
	General Sherman, who had distinguished
himself under Grant at the West was also
called to command, Thus Sherman com.


menced his march to the sea Nov. 15th,
after many cold nights Fort Mcallister was
reached and carried by assault, and on the
morning of the (21st, of Dec.) Sherman
entered Savannah. In announcing his
triumph to President Lincoln, he said  "I
beg to present you a Christmas gift, the
city of Savannah, with one hundred and
fifty guns, plenty of ammunition,  and
twentyfive thousand bales of cotton"
	Sherman halted at Savannah only long
enough to rest his troops.  Commanded a
march in the depth of winter to join Grant,
who was confronting Lee near Richmond.
	Lee saw he must either evacuate Richmond
and Petersburg,or be captured.  He then
began to form a plan by which he might
retreat into North Carolina, and there join
Johnston. Grant anticipating this issued
orders for a general movement against Rich-
mond. On the morning of March 29th,
it began, Sheridan's advance led to the bat-
tle of five forks, next morning at daybreak,
a general assault upon the Confederate lines
was made and before noon the entire
exterior defenses were captured. “In
Richmond, for it was Sunday the people had
assembled in the churches, knowing little of
what was going on twenty miles distant at
the fort. Davis had repaired to his custom-
ary place of worship.  There came up the
aisle a messenger, who handed him a dis-
patch from Lee, which contained intel-
ligence of the most momentous event of the
war. The news passed quickly from lip to
lip from church to church.
	Men women and children rushed from
the churches. Wagons on the streets were
soon loaded and driven to Danville depot.
The banks were all open and depositers
were as busy as bees removing their
deposits.  Hundreds of thousands of dol-
lars of paper money were destroyed.  From
that moment, law and order ceased to exist.
The warehouses were fired. The rams on
the James river were also fired. The
bridges leading out of the city were also
fired and were soon wrapped in flames.
	Morning broke upon a scene such as those
who witnessed it can never forget. “The
sun was an hour or more above the horizon,
when suddenly there ran up the whole
length of Main street the cry of ‘Yankees!
Yankees’!
	The Union soldiers were indeed entering
the city, and Potsburg was also in possess-
ing of the National forces, and over both
cities the stars and stripes again floated.
	Lee retreated to the south west, hoping to be
able to join Johnston in North Carolina, but
his troops were hotly pursued. “There was
no rest for them either by night or by day.
If they attempted to snatch a few moments
sleep, they were roused by the hoof-clatter
of Sheridan’s cavalry.” At last, overtaken
and seeing no hope of escape.  Lee agreed
to surrender.  In the largest building of
Appomattox Court House which boasted of
only five dwellings, he and Grant met.
“Lee stood beside a table, wearing a bright
blueish gray uniform, a military hat with a
gold cord, buckskin gauntlets, high riding-
boots and a beautiful sword. Grant, with
his slouched hat, dark blue frock coat
covered with mud, pantaloons tucked in his
soiled boots—wore no sword.
	They shook hands, sat down and soon
agreed to the terms of surrender, April 9th.
	The surrender of Johnston soon followed
and the Great Civil War was at an end.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1] 
IN MEMORIAM.

	IT is with deep regret, that we record the death
of our beloved President, Esther B. Fletcher,
who departed this life September 30, 1893, after
an illness of nine days.
	Sister Fletcher was born in Mass, where
she embraced the Gospel, and with her family
gathered with the saints in Kanesville on the
Missouri River, and in 1850 arrived in Salt Lake
City, and settled in the 8th, Ward, where she re-
sided until the day of her death. Her husband
died shortly after her arrival, and she was after-
ward united in plural marriage to Brother Royal
Barney.
	She was the mother of nine children, seven of
whom survive her, and are honorable members of
the Church of Jesus Christ.
	She held many responsible positions, being
appointed a teacher in the early organization of
the Relief Society about the year 1852, and again
to the same office, at the general organization
about 1867, she was afterward appointed secre-
tary, and again counselor, which position she
filled with great satisfaction, and in 1882, she was
set anart as President, the duties of which office
she faithfully performed until the time of her
death.
	She was president of the Primary Association
for seven years and a half; and in the early days
of Utah, taught school, she did a great work for
her dead ancestors in the St. George and Salt
ake City Temples showing her life to be filled
with good works. She was a woman of great in-
tegrity full of love for the principles of the Gos-
pel, never swerving, firm and steadfast. May
her children, with whom we sympathize, follow
in her footsteps, and may we her co-laborers,
emulate her virtues, and profit by her good
example,
	C. K. Palmer, President.
	S.J. Chamberlain,   
	Jane Cowan,  Counselors.
	J. Mortenson Sec’y.
	E. E. Frost, Treasurer.
IN memory of Sister Louisa E. Young who
died Sept. 18, 1893, aged 30 years, she leaves a
husband, six small children and a host of friends
to mourn her loss. Where-as in the Providence
of God with His unbounded wisdom, we have been 
deprived of the companionship, help and as-
sociation of Sister Louisa E. Young a faithful
officer and member of the Relief Society;
	Resolved—That we tender our deepest love
and sympathy to the memory of the dear de-
parted.
	Resolved—That in this great bereavement we
acknowledge the hand of God, and bow to the
nevitable, but realize that in her we have lost an
able and loving associate, whose charities, talents
and charms endeared her to us all.
	Resolved—That we tender our love and sym-
pathy to the bereaved, especially to the dear
husband and children, may the spirit of conso-
lation, peace, and comfort attend them in this
hour of affliction.
	Resolved—That a copy of these resolutions be
placed on the Relief Society Record and a copy
sent to the bereaved, also'a copy to the Ex-
ponent.
	“And you shall shortly know that lengthened
		breath,
Is not the sweetest gift God sends his friend,
And that sometimes the sable pall of death,
Conceals the fairest boon His love can send.
If we could push ajar the gates of life,
And stand within, and all God’s working see, 
We could interpret all this doubt and strife,
And for each mystery could find a key.
We know that her trials are oe’r,
	Weep not that her race is run.
God grant we may rest as calm,
When our work, like her’s is done.
		Naomi R. Theobald, President.
		M. A. Davis, Secretary.
Hinckley, Millard Co.

RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT
	To the Memory of a noble mother in Israel,
Sister Hannah Ann Dubois Dibble wife of Philo
Dibble, who died in Springville,Utah, Oct. 28th,
1893, aged 85 years 2 months and 28 days, was
born in Pedrick Town, Salem Co, New Jersey.
Embraced the Gospel in Philadelphia in 1839,
passed through the persecutions and privations

[Column 2]
of the Saints with her husband; was a member of
First Relief Society in Nauvoo being recommend-
ed thereto by the Prophet Joseph Smith. She
was also the only person present when Patriarch
Joseph Smith Sen’r blessed his sons; gathered to
Utah with the Saints in 1851, was a faithful mem-
ber of the Relief Society in Springville and when
the Ward was divided became a member of the
1st Ward Relief Society where she resided; al-
though too feeble to take an active part therein.
She manifested the utmost patience during her
painful illness of 12 weeks; and:
	Whereas our Heavenly Father has called her
home as a shock of corn fully ripe, therefore:
	Resolved—That we the members of the Relief
Society emulate her noble example, her un-
swerving integrity, her cheerfulness under trying
circumstances and her patient resignation to the
will of her Heavenly Father.
	Resolved—That we hold her memory sacred
and imitate her amiable and patient disposition 
and her many virtues.
	Resolved—That we present a copy of these Re-
solutions to her bereaved family, also that they
be recorded in the Record Book of the 
Society, and a copy be sent to the WOMAN'S EX- 
PONENT for publication.—COM.
