RIVER, FIELD AND SKY.

Once, far away, a river ran 
	Adown a rocky glen,
It sprang from field of ice and snow
	To bless the homes of men, 
And all the rugged hills were clothed 
	With branches broad and fair,
Festooned with many a clinging vine
	And blossoms sweet and rare,
While underneath, new flowers looked up
	To the bright skies of June, 
And little birds sang choruses
	Aye! Every throat atune.

Adown the glen two straying went,
	That day they each one knew
The silent charm the landscape lent
	And, closer round it grew,
’Till one grown freer, sudden spoke,
	Heedless of all beside—
‘‘O, I have loved you all these years,
	And shall, while I abide.”

Like sudden light amid the dark,
	The truth upon his face;
It blessed the rural solitude 
	With grand and tender grace,
And like a token sacred, sweet, 
	Unlooked for yet possessed,
She took the jewel, love, he gave, 
	And hid it in her breast.
And then beside that river bright
	They listened to its song,
Like silver flashing in the sun
	The swift waves danced along;
They seemed to answer to her thoughts—
	‘‘Will he be true alway?”
For every ripple sang at once
	“Forever as today.”

They walked adown the level field
	The moonlight over all;
From out the blooming hedge they heard
	The young birds chirp and call,
And darkening rows of corn shook out 
	Their tassels and their floss,
And rustled all their shining blades
	Like swords that flash across; —
O, all the air was like perfume,
	And silence sweeter yet
Had buried all the jars of day
	When night with twilight met.

They paused, he looking on her face,—
	She, looking to the sky
As though to read some omen there
	Where silv’ry clouds swept by,
And saw—a star fall shining down,

[Column 2]
	Then swift from left to right,
Another shot athwart its track—
	A cross! before her sight.

Again, beside that river bright
	The lady walked alone, 
The autumn tints were on each leaf
	And flower and bird were flown.
‘‘O, river, thou didst hear his words
	That radiant summer day,
Give me an omen: Loves he yet?
	For he is far away.”
That cruel river—(’twas not strange, 
	It sprang from ice and snow.)
It seemed to answer “loves some one.”
	And sped on and away.

The lady walked within her room;
	Her day had turned to night.
She slept and dreamed, a bird sang clear,
	She woke, the sun shone bright,
“O, happy token! stars may fall 
	And rivers dry may run,
But thou art constant, thou art like
	The ever faithful one.”

And while she heard afar
	(Faint music to her ear)
Swift hoof-beats. Who rode so but he?
	And now ’mid Christmas cheer 
He enters ’neath the portals high, 
	They came to bring her word;
O, Christmas Morn! thy tidings are
	The sweetest ever heard.

Go name all bright streams that ye know
	That flow toward the sea;
Go name all fields beneath the stars
	Wherever they may be;
Yet ye shall never, never guess;
	Where’er your thoughts may fly,
Where sang the waves, where, fell the stars.
	No, no one but my love and I.
				AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.
Bountiful, Nov. 20,1893.


[Column 1]
WOMAN SUFFRAGE COLUMN.

Auckland, N. Z., Nov. 30.—The first election
in New Zealand under the female suffrage law
resulted favorably to the government. The wo-
men voted in large numbers, supporting mainly
the candidates who professed Christianity and
favored temperance.


[Column 1]
THE BALLOT FOR WOMEN.
	THE inconsistency of opinion, of which
many people have been possessed, that wo-
man is not in need of the ballot, is rapidly
becoming a thing of the past. What is
dearer to every true American than the
right to exercise his voice at the polls? And
if we stop to reason for a moment, what is
there to deter woman from this inherent
desire? It is not because she is incapable of
understanding the questions at issue, nor
because she cannot appreciate the ability
of the nominees. I know the assertion is
made that the polls are not decent places for
women, and that they are corrupting. No
place is decent from which women are long
excluded, and if they are corrupting, men
had better quit voting, some are fearful that
it will lead to a dangerous intimacy between

[Column 2]
the sexes. There could be no political
intimacy exist, that would exceed that
which already exists between the school
Superintendent and his teachers, the Mer-
chant and his clerks etc. In an Oriental
country a Physician can only prescribe for a
woman by thrusting her arm from behind a
curtain. If we prefer Turkish institutions
we had better go and live where they pre-
vail, but if our own system is the best let it
be consistent with itself and our constitu-
tion, which is a constitution of the people,
for, and by them, so that if we bring this
down to its technical meaning it is not a
government of the people, unless women
are classed outside, for there are more than
ten millions that have no voice in the mak-
ing of our laws, and they are the most
sober and religious portion of American
people. It is assumptuous in men to think
that their interest and affection for women
will lead them to just and wise legislation.
	Their desires may be, and I believe they
are perfectly in accord with the way they
would like to see them treated. But men
have not the intuitive knowledge of grasp-
ing the many reforms which woman can
see is neccessary to bring about a better
state of society; to protect the weak against
the encroachments of the more favored.
As the cooperation of the sexes is necessary
to people a world, so is mutual cooperation
on their part necessary to perpetuate its
virtue, to regulate its tone and to elevate na-
tional character. I am not superstitious
to the extent, that it will right all the
wrongs of women any more than it has
righted all the wrongs of men, but it is the
golden gate to every opportunity, and pres-
cisely the same advantage it gives to one
sex it will give to another. Look at the
interest women have exhibited during the
past few years, since the people divided on
Party lines. Must we ridicule this interest
and assure women that it is only for men
and boys, when they attain their majority,
to relegate to themselves this pleasurable
interest? I fear such comments would be
received with a degree of contempt, I
realize that the mere mention of the
political rights of women in the minds of
many, would indicate that the home would
be left desolate. But woman would not be
true to her instincts of wife and mother, if
the instincts of maternity, of domesticity,
are not persuasive enough to keep them in
the truest sense women, it is the most ser-
ious defect yet discovered in the Divine or-
der of nature; nor should women be de-
barred from this privilege, because the
majority are careless as to its advantages.
It is stated that eight hundred thousand
men did not vote last year. This is no ar-
gument that the rest should be deprived of
this right. Doubtless there were many
slaves who prefered to remain slaves, was
that an argument against freedom? Sup-
pose there is a majority of the women of
this State when it becomes one, does not
wish to vote, is that a reason for depriving
one woman who is taxed of her equal
representation? Or one innocent person of
the equal protection of his life and liberty.
We want no compulsion, like those Old


New England laws, which fined every voter
who did not vote. Men need not fear that
women will become defeminized, nature is
wise on those points. What ever their sex
incapacitates women from doing they will
not do, and their relation to society will not
be disturbed. It is useless for men to
decry the evils of the ballot for women, we
are assured of its power, and of the benefits
accruing from a national adoption of its
principles.
					S. A. Boyer.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


The Annual Convention of the W. S.
A. of Salt Lake Co. for the election of of-
ficers was held in the 13th, Ward Assembly
Rooms, Tuesday afternoon Nov. 21st, 1893.
President Nellie Taylor in the chair, opened
by singing, "Oh, ye fair ones,’’&c. Prayer
by Vice President E. McFarlane, continued
by singing "Do what is right.” Roll
called, the minutes of the previous meeting
were not read. The treasurer’s report for
the year was read and accepted. The
election of officers for the ensuing year was
then carried out, by ballot, Mrs. E.
McFarlane was chosed by unanimous vote for
President, Mrs. A. W. Eardley as 1st, Vice
President, Mrs. E. M. Price as 2nd, Vice
President, Mrs. Julia Druce as 3rd, Vice
President, Mrs. Mary E. Irvine as Sec’y
and Mrs. E. McCune as Assistant Secretary
and Mrs. E. W. Hyde as Treasurer. Mrs.
Nellie Little as chairman of the Executive
Board and Mrs. Mary P. Silver, Mrs. A. W.
Grant, Mrs. E. Lyon, Mrs. M. Holt as mem- 
bers of the board. A vote of Thanks was
tendered to the retiring officers, Mrs. E.
Mc Farlane took the chair. Territorial
President E. B. Wells made some very en-
couraging remarks, touched upon the
questions that were important to suffragists.
Mrs. Nellie Taylor, Dr. E. S. Barney, Mrs.
C. C. R. Wells each made a few interesting 
remarks.
    The membership fee was lowered by
 unanimous vote to 25 cts. a year, with privi-
lege to call for 10 cts. donation. Meeting
stood adjourned ’til the third Tuesday in
December.
			     MAGGIE BASSETT Sec.


[Column 1]
LADIES' SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING
	MINUTES of the Semi-monthly meeting
held in the 14th, Ward Assembly Rooms, 
Nov. 4th, 1893. President Z. D. Young
presiding, sang “O, happy souls who pray”
&c. Prayer by Sister Mary P. Silver,
further sang “For the strength of the hills’,
Minutes of last meeting read and approved.
President Young said she was grateful to
be here today. ‘‘I look upon this organiza-
tion as near the school of the Prophets. I
have never seen a day in my life, that I
have not been thankful that I am a woman.
The man has the Priesthood, but he has to
fight the battle but our labor is to bear and
mould the souls of men. President Young
said when women are living their religion
they can wash and anoint the sick. What-
ever comes to us sisters, let us acknowledge
the hand of God in all things, then we will
be lifted up above the trials  of life. I trust
my sisters that all will speak a few words.”
	Sister B. Smith, said she did not often
meet here as she had many duties owing to
working in the Temple. Also spoke of the 
Deseret Hospital being in need of a little
help. Gave much good counsel and
prayed God that we might all be faithful
and be prepared to dwell with the sanctified.

[Column 2]
	Sister E. Stevenson read a note from
President  E. S. Taylor excusing herself
from coming today, as her daughter needed
her help a few days, also said that our
loved President Horne was better, although
she could not meet with us yet.
	Sister M. W. Wilcox made some excel-
lent remarks thought we would need all
the faith we could get to bear the trials that
are coming, bore testimony of the good
spirit in the Temple, and prayed that we
might all be saved in the Celestial Kingdom.
	The sisters sang “The Spirit of God like
a fire is burning,’’ &c. After which Sister
Gibson spoke a few encouraging words and
bore her testimony. Sister Jane James,
said many encouraging things but felt that
she could not express her feelings. Said
she hoped light would yet reach her people
and prayed that her son, might be faithful
and go to them, as the Prophet Joseph
had predicted. She then spoke in tongues,
Sister Paul giving the interpretation.
	Sisters sang “How blessed is the day,”
&c. A sister in the congregation said the
gift of tongues had been in answer to her
prayer, that we might have the gift today.
May we all be faithful.
	President Young then called on Sister
Lydia D. Alder to read the first lecture on
faith, in the Doc. & Cov. after which Sister
Alder bore her testimony.
	Susan Larson, of the 8th, Ward made
some good remarks and asked the prayers
of the sisters in behalf of the late Sister
Fletcher’s daughter who is sorely afflicted.
Sister Windsor, bore an excellent testimony
and gave many instructions, and closed by
praying for more faith and more of the
Holy Spirit to lead us back to our Father’s
Kingdom. Sister E. Brown also spoke
well and bore a faithful testimony.
	President Young desired the sisters to
read the lectures in the Doc. & Cov. also
asked the Associations to keep up their
Annual Meetings. Thanked God for all
the blessings received here today, and
closed by asking the blessings of God up-
on us. Meeting adjourned for two weeks.
Sang Doxology. Benediction by Sister B.
Smith.
	LYDIA D. ALDER, Sec. pro tem.


[Column 1]
MEXICAN ITEMS.

	WELL it has been raining very hard for a
long time, yes, at timesalmost pouring and
I suppose that on account of my long
silence that my friends thought I had
been drowned or been washed away by
the turbulent streams, but on the con-
trary, I have been quite safe, and busy
studying my lesson,‘well,” some one might
ask, “what have you learned?” “Well I
have learned to a certain extent, so far as
my weak nature is able to comprehend, to
trust in God, and it is the sweetest and
dearest lesson that I have ever learned.
Perhaps some of our dear brethren and
sisters in more favored and civilized places
wonder it our Father in Heaven would
stoop to care for these poor exiles in this
far off land, but it is so, and here in this
place I have learned the sweetest lessons
that the Gospel has ever taught us.  Here
under a lonely roof I have had an op-
portunity of peeping into the lives of
of some of the chosen servants of God,
for instance Nephi one of the forefathers of
this race by whom we are surrounded,
he learned to suffer and put his trust in
God, he was taken through the soul refining

[Column 2]
experience of having to leave friends,
home, riches, and all that he possesed for
the knowledge that he had of his doing the
will of Heaven, and after having made
those sacrifices was called to run the risk of
his own life, and commit a deed that his
own soul revolted against, knowing that he
was doing his duty.  It must have taken a   
great deal of faith on his part, he being a
tender hearted man to raise enough courage
to cut off Laban’s head. Then afterwards
in traveling with his brethren he was con-
stantly blamed for believing that he was
doing right, and his sorrows were great and
his life in danger constantly, on account of
the unbelief of his own brethren, yet his
faith never wavered, but was always firm,
and he said, 11 Nephi 4—24, “O, Lord I
have trusted in thee, I will trust in thee
forever, I will not put my trust in the arm
of flesh; for I know that cursed is he that
putteth his trust in the arm of flesh.  Yea,
cursed is he who putteth his trust in man,
or maketh flesh his arm.”
	His soul was continually poured out in
humble prayer, in passing through affliction,
his life was a continual feast of the divine
blessings and knowledge of his Redeemer.
And such will be the lives of all those who
have come here to the remnants of his
posterity,if their trust is firm in the Lord,
if they find out his will and do it, the rain
may fall and the winds blow, but if their
house is built upon a rock, the rock of
revelation, it will stand the storm.
	God never has called any person, or set
of persons to preform a mission upon this
earth, without having taken them through
many trials, in order to purify and prepare
them for the work.  Even our Savior
withstood the temptations and wiles of
Satan,before entering upon his labor of love
among the human family, and if we read
the lives of all faithful men and women we
find that sometime in their lives they had
passed through a course of trying ordeals
and therein had learned their lesson of
faith.  Those who are determined to do
right will be chastened and tried until they
will acknowledge the hand of God in every
thing, even in their deepest sorrow thank
Him for blessings, thank him for trials, and
suffering, it will be then and only then that
we will not be tried, for if we watch closely
we will find out behind every cloud there is
a sunlight, behind every sorrow a blessing,
and blessed be the Giver of sorrow and
trial, of joy and blessing. How shall we
appreciate friends, home, parents, without
having been deprived of those blessings,
but we will learn in the end that one
blessing we never have been deprived of,
and that is the tender watch-care of our
eternal Heavenly Father.
	S. T. BRIMHALL.
Colonia de Juarez Mexico, Oct. 11th, 1893.


BLACK FRUIT CAKE—Cream together one
pound of brown sugar and one pound of
butter. Beat the yolks and whites of ten
eggs separately. Add the yolks with one
pound of seeded raisins, one pound of cur-
rants, and half a pound of sliced citron,
one-third of an ounce each of ground cin-
namon and nutmeg, and one-quarter ounce
each of ground mace and cloves, also one
pound of flour that has been slightly
browned. Add the whites of the eggs.
Mix and beat well. Turn into a mould and
bake for five hours in a moderate oven.
Before using, ice and decorate with candied
fruit.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THANKSGIVING.

As light o’er the snowy-capped mountain,
	Brings promise of glorious day,
Our hearts over-flow as a fountain, 
	To Father our tributes we pay,
We give praise, and fervent thanksgiving,
	For all of Thy mercies so rare'
E’en for the privilege of living
	On this earth so lovely and fair.
We thank Thee for life, and its duties,
	The grandeur of being on earth,
For this world, and its marvelous beauties,
	Sweet flowers, bright sunshine, and mirth.
We thank Thee O Father for music,
	Expressing the language of hearts.
For poetry, painting,and sculpture
	And all the inventions and arts.
We thank thee for homes, food and raiment,
	For kindred, for friendship, and love—
Though we are but poor humble claimants,
	We praise Thee our Father above!
We thank thee for pain, and for trials,
	And waging of elements drear
With even life’s stern self-denials,
	And melting of hearts with a tear.
We thank thee for all oflife’s'seasons,
	For springtime, for winter and rain;
We know that in time all thy reasons
	To our wond’ring souls will be plain, 
We thank thee for hopes Thou hast given
	Of a life immortal and free, 
That beautiful respite in Heaven, 
	When again we come unto Thee.
	E.R.SHIPP.


[Column 1]
SOME WOMEN'S LIVES.

	THERE is a strange hunger in the average
female heart to be the one and only love of
some other heart. A God given instinct
no doubt, to make the monopoly of mar-
riage dear and desirable, but fatal if the
man be—as is often the case—selfish, need-
ing daily and systematic discipline;
tyrannical, requiring a little wise suppression
and deposing now and then; extravagant
needing constant lessons in economy, vain,
needing an occasional lesson in humility;
and the woman one of those poor, feeble
souls who meekly accept whatever is offered
and who will bear anything and everything
without a murmur rather than be found
fault with. A tender, sympathetic, self-
denying woman who deals with his follies
and faults gently and patiently, as she vain-
ly struggles to forget all the fond unrealized
dreams of her girlhood now so far behind
her; and feeling hopelessly in her inmost
heart that so far as her husband is concerned
the future holds naught of love or happi-
ness for her; feels with an icy pang a chill
sense of desolation and neglect, a
disappointment black as the murky clouds
which hide the blue of Heaven on a stormy
day; knows that her husband who has his
business acquaintances, his friends whom
he meets at the club and elsewhere is surely
growing away from her; that while she has
remained at home busy with household
duties and had grown rusty both in looks
and ideas, he has acquired the superficial
polish of the man of the world and has be-
come even more selfish, more egotistical
until he finally convinces her by continued
coldness and neglect that he is both weary
and ashamed of her. Oh! romantic idealist
with an impossible hero standing as the
type of all that lover and husband ought to
be in that bright fancy land which you

[Column 2]
imagine is the real earth upon which you
tread; how soon must the idol fall from his
pedestal into the dust and lie there degraded,
defaced; a thing to spurn with the foot and
put away with unfulfilled hopes and un-
realized dreams. Many and many a wife
who fulfills all her household duties faith-
fully who sits smiling conventional smiles by
her husband's side in public, live, the life
that honorable woman leads in this en-
lightened century, many and many a wife
looks back with a secret bitter smile to
those other days when she thought her
lord and master a hero, a noble champion,
who was always to be her lover and now!
	Many a neglected wife feels that same icy
chill; and such a pang is only the first of
many pangs which go on increasing until
they finally lose their power to sting so 
keenly; and the wife that meant to be so
patient, so loving and true becomes indiffer-
ent as to whether her husband spends his
evenings with her or elsewhere; oftentimes
finding herself glad that he is with her so
seldom. A strong-minded woman said to
me once: "Some women make so much of
trifles. Why, my husband is always out; 
and I'm sure I never fret over such a trifle,
and as for cards, provided he divides his
winnings with me I never think of object-
ing." And yet those very trifles are the
rocks on which many a poor woman's life
is wrecked.
"These trifles! Can it be they make or mar a
human life?
	Are souls as lightly swayed as rushes are,
By love or strife?
"Yea, yea! A look the fainting heart may break
	Or make it whole;
And just one word, if said for love's sweet sake
	May save a soul!"
	Memory brings before me to night, a fair,
young face, soft, confiding blue eyes and a
wealth of golden hair; now hidden under
a coffin lid; white their owner sleeps the
last sleep, the world forgotten, by the world
forgot. I saw her in her bridal robes when
she fondly imagined life held nothing but
sweets; that life, her life, would be one
long summer day; and I saw her agin
when she had proven the fallacy of it all-
had quaffed deep draughts of the bitter
waters of Marah, was again arrayed in her
bridal robes, but this time, she was the
bride of death. I well remember when
the first consciousness of her husband's cold-
ness and neglect began to dawn upon her.
She said no word uttered no complaint; but
the sweet face grew thin and pale, the
sweet blue eyes inexpressibly sad. Night
upon night she would sit alone watching
and waiting. Carriage after carriage would
roll by; sometimes one would stop and
then she would listen with intense eager-
ness expecting ever moment to hear his
step in the hall and then to see him enter
the room with his usual careless grace.
One night he came home even later than
usual had lost heavily at cards and had
been drinking. His wife was weeping
when he entered teh room. She was but a
girl in years, far away from relatives and
near friends and her "hour of trial" fast
approaching. To night a sense of utter
desolation, of intense loneliness seemed to
shut out every ray of light, of comfort.
Not one single glimmer pierced the black-
ness of the louds that were fast closing
about her. Vain were my attempts at
cousolation. Vain were the bright pictures
of a happy future with her babe. She
would only repeat in a weary despondent 
voice that spoke of bitter unshed tears. 

[Column 3]
	"You are very kind, dear friend and
mean well, but you do not understand. I
cannot tell you why or how but I know
life is over for me; and I am so tired, so tired,
that I am glad of it."
	As her husband entered the room he
laughed scornfully at her tears. For once
she made no effort to hide her grief. What
need? Had he not long since stripped the
veil from their miserable existance reveal-
ing the hollow shame in all its hideousness?
Neither did she offer any explanation when
he, coarsely and with assumed ignorance,
demanded the cause of her grief; not from
any definite reason but simply from a kind
of instinct that makes the sympathetic,
sensitive nature shrink from unfolding itself
to the touch of one that it feels is neither-
because her inmost being felt that he would
hear it with an antagonism that would jar
every fibre. There are so many ways of
letting the life out of a woman's heart
already faint from disappointment; and
for some moments he tried with a drunken
man's persistence to see how far his taunts
and sneers would exasperate the woman
whom he had promised to love, honor and
cherish; her continued silence only made
him angrier and he became for the time a
maniac. The face he turned round upon
her, the eyes blazing with wrath, the
cheeks pale with passion, the lips set was a
fearful face as he staggered to where his
wife stood; and placing both hands on her
shoulders he shook her roughly as he hissed
into her trembling ear these brutal words-
"You're a nice wife for a man to have!
always going around with a long, white
face to make people think you're an abused
woman. Bah! I tell you what it is Alice,
I'm just about sick of your whining and
crying and don't want any more of it.
Do you hear? I shall stay out just as late
as I want to and just as often; so you may
as well make the best of it. Why, there
were at least sixty men at the club to night; 
most of them married, and do you think
their wives sat up and met them with tears
and long faces? No, by—! and from
now, on, I want no more of it. Remember
that, will you?" And with another final
and emphatic shake he pushed her roughly
from him. Too much intoxicated to fully
realize what he was doing he was not aware
of the brutal force he used and neither of
them observing a footstool that stood near,
Alice fell over it striking her head heavily
against a chair before reaching the floor,
murmuring faintly, "Oh Charlie how could
you, when I loved you so?" She sank in-
to unconciousness. They were the last
words she ever spoke. For days she re-
mained unconscious and then the end came.
Never shall I forget the expression of
peaceful content that settled on the beauti-
ful face. Surely she had found rest and
the peace that passeth all understanding.
Of course, Charlie was very penitent and
apparently remorse—stricken for a time;
but yesterday I read in an eastern paper
that Charlie M. or as he was more familiar-
ly known ‘‘Bonnie Prince Charlie” was
about to lead to the Altar the beautiful
daughter of one of New York's richest
men. I wonder if Alice knows—or cares.
	S.E. L.


THE Isle of Man legislature has just en-
acted that duly qualified women shall, in
future, vote for the election to the House
of Keys, thus following, on more limited
lines, the example of New Zealand.

[Column 1]
	A FEW SUGGESTIONS.
	
	THERE has been considerable agitation
on the Woman Suffrage subject in the Ter-
ritory of late, and there is likely to be more
in view of the prospect that we are perhaps
soon to have statehood, when it is firmly
believed and ardently desired by a large
majority of women citizens, that the ballot
so unjustly taken from those to whom it
had been given under the laws of the Ter-
ritory will be restored, with all the rights
and privileges pertaining thereto, making
men and women equal before the law.
	Because women are eligible to office does
not constitute them office-holders though
one might think it really did, listening to
the arguments brought to bear against it.
it is not such an easy matter for even men
to get a nomination, and after that to get
sufficient votes to elect them. Judging
from what we know of women they would
be much more likely to nominate a man, a
good man of course, and vote for him,
than one of their own sex consequently
women are in no danger of being elected
to any office for a long time to come. But
we hope and trust the agitation of these
political questions will continue, par- 
ticularly as it is decidedly educational in its
tendency, and that is what is needed. The
women of this Territory married and
single, should try to become somewhat
familiar with the laws of Utah, and the
political conditions as well, and not neglect
the Constitution and Declaration of In-
dependence which are really the foundation
on which the civil and judicial government
of the country is built. The laws affecting
women and their rights should be investi-
gated by them and see where they stand,
and determine in their own minds whether
they are satisfied with such a status or not;
Not accept things for granted without
knowing for themselves and like the wo-
man who had all the rights she wanted on
hearing that in her state a husband might
bequeathe his children away from the
mother, became very indignant and ex-
claimed “Why do not all the mothers cry
out against such a law?” The answer
given her was in part “People who do not
vote pay no attention to the laws until they
suffer from them.” The actual text of the
law was this.
	“Every person authorized by law to make a
will, except married women, shall have a right to
appoint by his will a guardian or guardians for
his children during their minority.”
	There is not in the statute the slightest
clause in favor of the mother, nor any

[Column 2]
limit to the power of the guardian, nor
any word of positive law to protect the
mother.
	We simply refer to this to show how lit-
tle women have been considered in legisla-
ting in the past. The time has come how-
ever when the higher education of women
has brought about many radical changes,
and many more are needed for the benefit
of both men and women, and it is our opinion
that the womanly and more humane element
if given expression at the ballot box and
eventually in the councils of the nation,
will have a salutary effect. Preparatory
work is always necessary in any important
undertaking and therefore we earnestly ad-
vise the women of Utah to study
systmatically, or until they adopt some
method converse together on political mat-
ters in their own locality, town, city or
county.
	In some of the counties during the past
two years very creditable work has been
done in clubs and in classes under the
auspices of the county or ward organiza-
tions. Do not be discouraged because only
a few will take hold, it is the few who scat-
ter the seed for the many. The farmer sows
a small quantity of seed grain, but he reaps
a large harvest, and that harvest feeds a
hungry multitude.
	In another issue of this paper will ap-
pear some items of the Conventions held
in Rich, Utah and Davis counties also
the meetings in Spanish Fork and Spring-
ville which were very interesting indeed.
At Spanish Fork the W. S. A. had a
sumptuous dinner prepared for all the
members of the Association in that flourish-
ing town, numbering if we recollect rightly,
one hundred and ten besides invited guests,
several from Provo, and from Salt Lake.
Mesdames Wells arid Richards Territorial
officers. At Springville the meeting was
exceedingly interesting; there were three
gentlemen on the program, the Mayor of
the City, the editor of the Springville
Independent and Mr. W. H. Kelsey of that
place. The President Mrs. S. A. Boyer
is a very enthusiastic worker in the cause
and has kept the association up to a high
standard notwithstanding many discourage-
ments and indifference which are always
hard to battle with.


[Column 1]
EDITORIAL NOTES.
	THE quarterly conference of the Relief
Society of this Stake of Zion will be held
in the Fourteenth ward assembly rooms, in
this city, on Friday, Dec. 15th, 1893, meet-
ings commencing at 10 a. m. and at 2 p. m.
as usual. As this is an important time
and there is much need of relief among the
poeple in the several wards, it is ardently
desired by the presiding officers of the
society that there should be represent-
atives from each branch present to hear the 
instruction that is likely to be given in
reference to the care of the needy that there
may be no suffering among the people that
can he avoided. A cordial invitation is al-
so extended to all interested in this benevo-
lent work, and especially to the Presidency
of the Church, the presiding Bishops, the 
presidency of this Stake of Zion and the
Bishops of the several wards.
	WE had the pleasure recently of attending
the opening of Miss Maud May Babcock’s
School of Delsarte and Physical Culture in
the Social Hall in this City and of meeting
there her brother Dr W. W. Babcock, who

[Column 2]
in addition to assisting his sister in the
institution is also Resident Physician at St.
Mark’s New Hospital. During the exer-
cises on that occasion Dr. Babcock gave an
interesting talk, explaining, first a history
of the work of physical culture etc. from
the Greek period down, then took up the
work physiologically—the different systems,
physical examinations extant which
would be the basis of all the work—Miss.
Babcock also treated upon this and described
the apparatus which is all new and the
latest patents—1st, mentioned the pulley
weight system first discovered by Dr. D. A.
Sargent, now of Harvard University, th s
system covers about one hundred and fifty
different appliances, of which what is com-
monly know as the chest weight is most
important. These appliances are especially
useful as being adapted to the weakness of
the weak, and the strength of the strong,
and are essentially important, as they can be
used for the development and strength of
particular groups of muscles they have the
rowing machine, chest weight, bridle
stirrup and quarter circle; they have also
full Germam apparatus for German work,
horizontal and vaulting bar, traveling rings,
flying rings, small and large horizontal
parallel bars. Indian Clubs, iron and
wooden dumb bells, wands and rings in the
Swedish work they use all free-hand
gymnastics, except the climbing rope, be-
cause they consider that the most important,
the apparatus work being only accessory of
the measuring appliances, a complete anthro
pometric. Miss Babcock is assisted by Miss
Belle Salmon and Miss Mae Taylor both well
known in this City, and both attended the
summer school at Harvard this year. The
dressing rooms in the building are fitted up
entirely new, bath room has hot and cold
water (entirely new floor in the main hall)
platform nicely fitted up for office and elo-
cution work. On the evening of the open-
ing Miss Babcock recited “King Robert of
Sicily’’ with music by Miss Kate Romney,
Miss Mary Romney rendered a sweet selec-
tion her exquisite voice telling particularly
well on the high notes, and Mr. Hugh
Dougall gave “The Holy City.’’ He
certainly has a magnificent voice and gives
fine expression to the language of the song.
Miss Babcock’s terms of tuition are
reasonable.


THREE QUARTERS OF A CENTURY.
	ON the 20th of November a very success-
ful party, a sort of ovation was given to
President M. Isabella Horne by a com-
mittee of ladies’ and the officers of the
branches of the Relief Society in Salt Lake
County, it was the seventy-fifth anniver-
sary of her birthday. Mrs Horne has lived
three quarters of a century and merits by
her faithfulness and endeavors for good, in
the interests of the women of the Territory
and of the Church, the utmost honor and
appreciation of those with whom she has
labored so long and diligently.
	The party was held in the 14th, Ward
Assembly Rooms in the afternoon. The
room was decorated with flags and banners,
a handsome carpet and easy chairs in the
centre of the hall, and on the stand and
platform were beautiful flowering plants
and palms and a large century plant fresh
from the green house, besides bouquets in
vases artistically arranged, and to
Mrs. Horne it was a perfect surprise until
about noon of the day; her family however
were aware of the preparations that were


[column 1]
in progress, and of course joined with the
sisters in the celebration. The one draw-
back to the pleasure of all was the absence
of Brother Horne, who was really not well
enough to be present.
	The house was well filled with dear
friends and fellow-laborers, the First Presi-
dency of the Church seated upon the stand,
also President Lorenzo Snow, Apostle
Franklin D. Richards and Heber J. Grant,
the Presiding Bishops of the Church, Presi-
dent Angus M. Cannon, Counselor C. W.
Penrose, Bishop George H. Taylor and
some other brethren, occupied seats of hon-
or, but the company were mostly ladies.
	Mrs. Elmina S. Taylor Counselor to
President M. I. Horne in the Relief Society
of this Stake of Zion, presided during the
exercises and took charge of the program,
Mrs. Helen M. Whitney the other Counse-
lor was not able to attend, which was much
regretted. President Zina D. H. Young
and her Counselors Jane S. Richards and
Bathsheba W. Smith were in attendance,
and many other representitive women.
	Sister Taylor made a few appropriate re-
marks greeting the assembly of her friends
and congratulating Sister Horne in a grace-
ful way.
	The exercises opened with prayer by
President Joseph F. Smith, and singing “O,
my Father”.
	After which President Wilford Wood-
ruff addressed the assembly. He stated
that he never felt able to express his
thoughts in words when gathered among
the Saints of God, congratulated Sister
Horne and alluded to the many pleasant
associations with both Brother and Sister
Horne; “when he looked upon this assembly
especially the aged, he was glad some of
the Saints had lived to carry out the work,
while others had passed behind the vail, no
more interesting generation could have
been or ever had been; no greater dispen-
sation in fact than this one has ever been,
we have great blessings,’’again congratula-
ted Sister Horne on this day, her labors
had been great, while he said this of her,
he said it of others, the gathering called
to his mind the Prophet Joseph and Hyrum
and Brigham and John Taylor; referred to
his labors with them, and the coming of
the pioneers into this valley, recalled the
words of President Young when he came
into the valley, here he said we shall build
a Temple in Zion, said God bless Sister
Horne and her husband; and her children
will follow in their footsteps, thanked the
Lord that a mighty army of people, sons
and daughters was being raised up through
the principles of the order of God, blest the
assembly etc., the remarks of the President
were especially inspiring to all assembled
and will ever be treasured to encourage
those who are laboring in the Relief Society
in which he has always manifested the
deepest interest.
	President Zina D. H. Young after ad-
dressing herself to the First Presidency up-
on the stand and the President of the
meeting, heartily congratulated Sister
Horne, and rejoiced with her in the many
blessings the sisters had enjoyed; referred
to the day when Joseph the Prophet was
taken from us as the darkest day we had
ever seen, but while he was with the
people he had poured out blessings upon
blessings, revealed many things, turned the
key of knowledge, and opened up the
channel of communication with the hea-
vens. Sister Young was thankful to see so

[column 2]
many familiar faces, those who were en-
gaged in labors of love.
	President George Q. Cannon, appreciated
this opportunity very much, thought how-
delightful it would be if all our aged
brethren and sisters could have their birth-
days remembered, spoke of the hall as a
historic building, where many social parties
and reunions had been given; since coming
into the room many memories had crowded
into his mind,'some were pleasant others
were sorrowful, he had danced there more
than once and would like to again, if there
was an opportunity, Sister Horne he re-
membered used to be a very good dancer
and was now probably did not think she
had lost it all, thought our people did not
grow old as fast as some others, and re-
ferred to President Woodruff, Sister Horne
and President Snow, who seemed so young
and well-preserved. Alluded to his boy-
hood and association with Brother and
Sister Horne when they came to the valley
together also President Taylor’s family the
intimacy that existed between the two
families and gave some interesting incidents
of experience in those times; quoted Heber
C. Kimball’s remark concerning respect for
the aged when he said the Lord had more
respect for us than we had for one another,
the Lord had said “My aged servant
Joseph Smith,” (meaning Joseph Smith Sen.
father of the Prophet.) President Cannon
then commented upon the wonderful strides
the sisters had made the last few years,
bringing them forward before the public,
into spheres of usefulness, that opened be-
fore them, he then turned and address-
ing President Woodruff, said, it was one
of the things that impressed him very
greatly, he was pleased to see Sister Horne
take a leading part, an active part side by side
with others in this grand movement for the
elevation and enfranchisement of her sex,
was thankful she had lived to take so
prominent a part in the work for women,
“but you are His servants though you may
not bear the priesthood as we do, and yet
your spheres are enlarged and there is no
limit to your advancement,” felt to bless
Sister Horne whose birthday anniversary
we were celebrating, her husband and their
posterity, Brother Horne was a man faith-
ful and true and God would bless them and
their posterity after them.


WOMAN’S' EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

HISTORICAL SKETCH.

(Of the life of Mrs. M. Isabella Horne.)

	ON this eventful day the Seventy Fifth
anniversary of the birth of our beloved
President, Mrs. M. Isabella Horne, my
memory goes back over the vista of years
and I think of her as I first knew her. Al-
ways a faithful friend, a true saint, and a
steadfast, worker in the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latterday Saints.
	President Horne had the honor of be-
longing to the Relief Society in Nauvoo,
which was organized by the Prophet Joseph
Smith, and had the  unspeakable pleasure
of listening to the melody of his voice,
giving instruction to the daughters of Zion,
he having turned the key for the redemption
of woman. Since that day she has had
the privilege of seeing her sister woman,
gradually climb upward and onward, un-
til now she holds a position of honor, by
the side of her brother man. Long and
oft has this friend of woman, raised her
voice in exhortation, instruction and love,
to her sisters, many of whom can testify,

[Column 2]
that from her lips has fallen the soft Words
that first caused them to think, and
inspired in them the desire to reach out to
more glorious possibilities of intelligence
and progress. Her instructions to maid
wife and mother, explaining the mission of
woman, in bearing the souls of men, her
duties to them, her home and her husband,
are beyond price. And may she yet live to
see all her desires in this respect fulfilled.
	When the Relief Society was reorganized
in these valleys after the move South in
1858, Mrs. Horne was chosen by Bishop A.
Hoagland, to preside over the Society in
the 14th, Ward. This occured on Dec. 12,
1867. Under her wise management the
Society prospered. The poor were relieved,
the distressed comforted, and yet means
gathered up in the treasury. After a time
the building known as “The Relief Society
Hall,” was erected. A granary and a
quantity of wheat was next added, and
blessings attended all the efforts of the
sisters.
	President Brigham Young, having great
confidence, in President Horne, placed on
her another important mission in the latter
part of the year 1869. This was known as
the Retrenchment movement. A meeting
was called in the 15th, Ward Relief Society
Hall—President Young met with and in-
structed the sisters in their duties in this
respect and many blessings were promised
them in their labors. This meeting was
adjourned to the 14th, Ward Assembly
Hall, where an organization was effected,
with Sister M. I. Horne as President with
Sisters Eliza R. Snow, Zina D. H. Young,
Bathsheba W. Smith, Margaret T. Smoot,
Phebe W. Woodruff, Marinda W. Hyde
and Sarah M. Kimball as Counselors.
Lydia D. Alder was chosen as Secretary.
	Out of this organization, grew the 
regular Semi-monthly meetings of today
which have been held in the 14th Ward,
regularly ever since.
	President M. I. Horne was appointed to
preside over the Relief Society of this Stake
of Zion, Dec. 1877.
	In the month of November 1890, Pres’t.
Horne was elected President of the board,
of directors of the Woman’s Co-op store,
all of which positions she holds at the
present time, except that of President of
the Relief Society of the 14th, Ward, she
having resigned the position, and since
moved to the 18th, Ward.
	May her days be lengthened, that she-
may realize all her righteous desires, then
when her tired feet shall rest in the Golden 
City, may she enjoy the reward of the true
and faithful.


Affectionately Inscribed to Mrs. M . Horne on.
her Seventy Fifth Birthday.

How beautiful the sinking sun
	Sets in the darkening West;
’Mid fiery clouds of red and gold  
	it slowly goes to rest.
The day has been a weary one,
	Mayhap so full of care—
Now soothingly the shadows fall,
	And rest breathes everywhere.
The morn was full of promise—
	The noon the battle brought,
The evening is so glorious—
	With blessings ever fraught.
So life goes down at even
	Like to the setting sun—
The day of battle over—
		The laurels fairly won.


Then comes the bright to-morrow
	The sun shines forth again— 
Earth’s children rise to labor, 
	Their blessings to obtain,
And on life’s fair to-morrow
	The great Millennial day, 
May we all meet in Heaven
	Whence none will ever stray.
But with the blest Redeemer,
	And Father good and true
May we walk the fields Elysian 
	Each beauty ever new.
Loved sister, friend and mother 
	May we who’ve sought on earth
To live the life Celestial
	Receive a Heavenly birth.
Lovingly,
	LYDIA D. ALDER

Mrs. Lizzie Thomas Edwards, sang with

play the accompaniment, though he could not

[Column 1]

M. I. HORNE’S SEVENTY-FIFTH
BIRTHDAY NOV. 20, 1893.

	"THE aged woman likewise, that they
be in behaviour as becometh holiness, not
false accusers, not given to much wine, 
teachers of good things;
	"That they may teach the young women
to be sober, to love their husbands, to love
their children,
	"To be discreet, chaste, keepers at home,
good, obedient to their own husbands, that
the word of God be not blasphemed."
Titus, chapt. 2, verses 3, 4, and 5.

Thus anciently, were holy women taught,
	By Christ’s Apostles, great, inspired men;
Today are glimpses of those lessons taught;
	Apostles, Prophets teach the same again.
In this last time, now more than fifty years
	Hath woman’s true position been defined
By God’s appointed servants, Prophets, Seers,
	And Revelators of His will and mind.
How can we praise our Father, God, enough,
	For blessing us with this grand sisterhood?
To aid each other when the way is rough,
	To find no pleasure but in doing good.
To love and help our husbands on in life;
	To love and teach our children righteousness;
None to accuse, make peace instead of strife;
	True women everywhere, no more, no less.
Making each home the dearest, happiest place,
	Kin to the Heavenl homes we seek above;
That every child may sweetest lessons trace,
	From father’s counsel kind, and mother’s love.
I mentioned fifty years, How few remain
	Of that first organized Relief Society,
To whom the Prophet, Joseph Smith, made plain,
	Their place and how to fill it with propriety.\
One of those few, today is Seventy-five;
	Again, our Father, God, thy name we praise.
That Mary Isabella Horne is yet alive,
	With pow’r among us still her voice to raise.
How often that dear voice to us imparts
	Lessons of wisdom for our home affairs,
Teaching us how to touch our children’s hearts,
	And make them partners in our household
	cares.
With Zina, Bathsheba, and other few,
	Teaching the younger women holy truths,

[Column 2]
Which Joseph taught at Kirtland and Nauvoo,
	Sweet lessons’ which the wearied being soothes
For one, I often wish that I could be
	The maker of a home like Sister Horne’s;
So orderly, from envious strife so free.
	Like a bright bed of roses with no thorns.
Yet, time has been when thorns her heart did
	pierce,
And noxious weeds grew round her, tall and
	thick;
And storms have beaten on her, cold  and fierce,
	Calling for vig’rous action, brave and quick.
How is it, thorns and weeds are cleared away
	Clouds have dispersed, the blast’s wild voice,
	is dumb?
She simply learned God’s mandates to obey,
	Has triumphed, and those ills are overcome.
’Tis good, with Sister Horne to celebrate
	This Anniversary of her blessed birth;
The victories she has gained to contemplate,
	Her lessons to recall, of priceless worth.
And then, to forward look, into that realm
	Of many mansions, where we hope to land;
In this life, finding nought that can o’erwhelm,
	No tests nor trials we cannot withstand.
To think, in that fair region of the blest,
	How good, how beautiful, sometime, to meet
Prsicilla, Persis. Dorcas, and the rest—
	The former Sisterhood with ours complete.
And then, I doubt not, we shall turn and say,
	We're glad that we were privileged to be
	born,
To learn and labor in this Latter-day,
	With our beloved and faithful M. I. Horne.
	L. Lula Greene Richards.


[Column 1]

A TRIBUTE.

[By Mrs. E. B. Wells.]

	IN thinking of the long life of our dearly
beloved mother in Israel whose anniversary
of three quarters of a century we celebrate
today, one scarcely knows whether to look
backward at the chapter of events so full of
interest, historical and personal, or to look
forward with prophetic gaze into the mystic
vista of the ever thrilling future, and
calculating the manifold labors so nobly
achieved foretell that which is sure to be
the positive result of the work already so
far accomplished, that the reward is near at
hand. Even now the fruits of the harvest
season are beautifully ripe,and radiant with
promise of a golden fruition. But for one
brief moment we will take a retrospective
glance. 
	In a cottage by the sea, on a chilly
November day such as this perhaps, or
mayhap in one of those dense fogs that
are common in England at this season of
the year, the news that a child was born,
"only a girl" not much thought of it those
days, would not be very thrilling, and the
mother herself could scarce have dreamed
beforehand of all that we assembled here
today are conscions of; nor did she realize
then as we do now, who have the knowledge
of the Gospel of a pre-existence, where the
little one came from so fresh and pink and
rosy or what her mission here on earth was
likely to be. How tenderly she would
have reared this little stranger had she
known but half that would  befall her, what
her destiny, her life-work, how wide her
sphere, how grand her possibilities. These
were all unkown, locked in the bosom of
mysterious nature's living drama. But to
many of us, the living panorama is un-
furled. We know how crossing the mighty

[Column 2]
ocean when only a child, she had those
wonderful experiences that come with a
sea voyage in a sailing vessel, and also the
life in a new country that is full of strange
incidents, and is of necessity partically
educational.
	Like other young women too of 
all ages and times she fell in love, hav-
ing met an interestng young farmer at a
religious gathering a camp meeting, the
friendship begun soon ripened into an
intimacy that resulted in marriage about
two years after; a union which has proved
such a complete and happy one that we see
them even today as much like lovers as
husband and wife: the golden wedding
celebrated when fifty years had passed was
a rare event and now when the frost of
four score years have come to the bride-
groom, and three quarters of a century
to the once youthful bride, we find them as
handsome and as happy in the family rela-
tion as when they first took possession of
each other, and the little farm, home  in
Canada. Yes even more so, for the olive
branches that grew one by one, and two
by two around their hearthstone, have
grown to magnificent proportions, and two
or three generations of these, now crown
the lives and cluster round the hearts of
this venerable couple, whose days have
been so full of blessing, and of good works,
and whose evening of life is so glorious
with rosy light and glowing prommise.
What shall we say, what can we say more
than this, that we who are assembled here
today sahite you—greet you as those
who after a long and adventurous voyage
upon the changeful sea of life, are safely
resting in the haven of Zion ready even
now at this ripe age, to go forward at the
call of duty whenever and wherever the
wise Father, through his servants here on
earth may designate.
	That we love this Reverend mother is
apparent, there is no need to tell her so in
words, that she merits the love of the
daughters of Zion, of the whole house of
Israel is too well known to need repeating.
and in conclusion may our sister love as a
band of women workers be always true,
and our union strong and steadfast as the
everlasting hills 'mong which we dwell,
and may we never grow weary of the
works of charity, philanthropy, and pro-
gress in which we have been so long
engaged,and one of the foremost among the
illustrious women of our age and time will
ever stand the name of her whose birthday 
anniversary we celebrate today,  MARY
ISABELLE HORNE
	President Horne explained how she had
been affected by the honors shown her and
the many expressions of love and esteem
from those who had spoke, how glad she
was to see her fellow laborers gathered there
the tried and true ones, and to be honored
with the presence of the First Presidency of
the Church, she had not the language to
express how grateful she was to her 
Heavenly Father and to her friends for this
tribute, spoke of her pleasure in laboring
with those associated in the Relief Society
and hoped to deserve all that had been said
of her and that we all might continue to
labor together or even more diligently in
the future. 
	Sister Horne seemed greatly overcome
by the many manifestations of love and 
esteem expressed on every side. 
	At the close of the exercises Mrs. Taylor
who had presided with grace and dignity
during the afternoon, thanked the audience


for their attention, the honor they had
shown by their presence, congratulated
Sister Horne, and expressed her pleasure
in being associated with her in many labors
of love and charity, and the program closed
with benediction by President Lorenzo
Snow. Daintily prepared refreshments
were served to the entire company and
general conversation and greetings were
soon followed by adieus as evening ap-
proached.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
R. S. REPORTS.
SANPETE RTAKE.
The Relief Society Conference of the
Sanpete Stake of Zion convened in the
Ephraim School House, September 22nd, 
1893. President M. A. P. Hyde presiding,
Conference opened with singing, “Lord we
come before thee now.” Opening prayer
by Bishop C. C. N. Dorius. Singing ‘‘Re-
deemer of Israel.” Statistical and
Financial report of the Relief Society of
Sanpete Stake, and the minutes of the last
conference were read and approved.
President M. A. P. Hyde hoped we
would have a time of rejoicing in our con-
ference, ‘‘Do not partake of the spirit of
the world but try and draw them to us, so
they can partake of the spirit of the Gospel
of Jesus Christ.”
The branches of the Relief Society of
Fountain Green, Moroni, Ephraim, and
Spring City were represented by their
presidents, all testifying of the good work
that is being done by the sisters.
Counselor Sarah Peterson said she
thought the sisters were doing well and did 
not think they could do any better. Spoke
at some length in regard to the storing
of wheat. Sister E. B. Wells, of Salt 
Lake City, referred to the storing of grain
and the counsel of President Young to
the sisters; also of the respect that was
shown to the Presidency of the Church in
attending the World’s Fair. Considered
the meeting held by our sisters in Chicago
and the visit of the Presidency had re-
moved a great deal of prejudice against us
as a people. Spoke in regard to the
World’s Fair. The impression that was
made on the people of the world at the
Relief Society and Y. L. M. I. A. meet-
ing s at Chicago, were very important.
Sister Elmina S. Taylor Territorial
President of the Y. L. M. I. A. of Salt
Lake City, rejoiced in the organization of
the Relief Society. ‘‘President Young
was inspired when he told the sisters to
store up grain for a time of famine. This
is a time of progress among the women of
the world and we do not want to be behind
them. The Relief Society is a blessing
throughout the land. May the Lord bless
us, that our meetings may be full of in-
struction.” Singing ‘‘Come, come ye
Saints.” Benediction by Bishop L. S.
Anderson.
AFTERNOON SESSION:—President M. A.
P. Hyde presiding; singing ‘‘Praise to the
man who communed with Jehovah.”
Prayer by Bishop Anderson. Singing,
‘‘Our God we raise to thee.” The two
branches of Manti Relief Society were
represented by their presidents as being in
a good condition, Counselor Minerva W.
Snow said: ‘‘My feelings I could not des-
cribe, the joy that filled my heart to hear
from the sisters of the prosperity of Zion,
It is very encouraging to have our brethren

[Column 2]
meet with us in our conference. Let us as
sisters in Zion take no praise unto our-
selves, but be humble and give the praise
unto our Father in Heaven, be thankful
for the blessings we enjoy daily. Let us
be patient and humble in our trials and the
Lord will bless us. Zion is arising and her
light is beginning to shine. Pray to our
Heavenly Father that he will keep our sons
and daughters in purity, that they may be-
come shining lights in the Kingdom of God’’
Counselor Sarah Peterson spoke concerning
hard times but said, “I think it is a good
time for we have plenty to eat and wear.
Advised the sisters to subscribe for the
EXPONENT. Sister Taylor said the
work of the Relief Society is a great and
glorious one. It is no good for us to
belong to any association unless we perform
our duties. Do we go to the Relief Society
meetings to gossip and talk about our
neighbors and the Priesthood? No, we go
there to be taught in the gospel. The
brethren are beginning to realize that wo-
men are a help in the kingdom of God I
thank God for the faithfulness and bravery
of our sisters through their troubles and
trials. Sustain your presidents and coun-
selors by your prayers. Also Sister Zina
D. Young and her counselors.
Be good wives and mothers and watch
your children, for Satan is trying to lure
them from purity and virtue. Ask God to
help you to know your duty and to do it.
Sister Wells spoke at some length on the
Silk Industry. Advised the sisters to plant
mulberry trees and raise cocoons.
Gave a description of some of the exhibits
at the World’s Fair. Referred briefly to
the different meetings of the women at
Chicago. “It is the express wish of Sister
Young that the branches of the Relief
Society should meet twice a month at
least.”
Sister Taylor desired the brethren and
sisters to teach their children their mother
tongue, those of different nationalities.
President M. A. P. Hyde thanked the
sisters for the information that they had
given ns. Sister Mary Petersen was sus-
tained as Stake Treasurer. Conference
adjourned for three months. Singing “The
Spirit of God like a fire is burning.”
Benediction by Brother John Schofield.
MARY ANN FRANTZEN, Sec.


[Column 1]
     HUNTSVILLE WEBER CO.
	Minutes of a meeting of the Relief Society
of Huntsville Ward, held August 24th,
1892.
	There were present on the stand Apostle
F.D. Richards, Bishop David Mckay,
President Zina D. H. Young, her counselors
Mrs. Bathsheba W. Smith;   Jane S.
Richards; Ward President Eliza A. Tracy,
with her Counselors; Stake Secretary,
Mattie Cannon, and others.
	The object of the meeting being to re-
ceive instruction and to lay the corner stone
of a Hall for the Relief Society of this
Ward, to hold its meetings and transact its
business in.
	President Tracy called the meeting to
order when Bishop Mckay offered prayer.
	After stating the object of the meeting
President Tracy requested Sister Jane S.
Richards to address the meeting, who ex-
pressed her great pleasure at meeting with
the Saints on so interesting and important
an occasion as the laying of the corner
stone of a building for the use and benefit
of the ladies who constitute the Relief

[Column 2]
Society of Huntsville Ward. She ex-
pressed her gratification at observing a
spirit of progress that appeared to actuate
the Saints of this place, and congratulated
the Society upon their prospect of a house
in which to work, transact their business,
and hold their meetings. She also urged
the importance of carefully maintaining
united efforts, and referred to some of the
magnificent results that had already been
accomplished thereby.
	Sister Bathsheba W. Smith said this was
her first visit to this ward, and felt very 
much pleased at the present opportunity to
make the acquaintance of her Sisters here.
Remarked how little we realized when the
Prophet Joseph first organized the Society
in Nauvoo, the vast importance which
these associations would attain to, but
time and experience has proven that God
works with His daughters, and they are
doing a great work in the midst of Israel.
	President Zina D. H. Young desired the
Spirit to guide her on this important oc-
casion. Joseph Smith said, early after the
first organization, while addressing the
Relief Society in April 1842, “You will
receive instructions through the order of
the Priesthood which God has established,
through the medium of those appointed, to
lead, guide and direct the affairs of the
Church in this last dispensation; and I now
turn the key in your behalf in the name of
the Lord, and this Society shall rejoice and
knowledge and intelligence shall flow down
from this time henceforth; this is the begin-
ning of better days to the poor and needy,
who shall be made to rejoice and pour forth
blessings on your heads.” Let me say to
all who look upon these associations as idle
gossip, I bear my testimony before you that
God is with his daughters; and to you my
dear brethren, be interested, for there is a
great blessing in this work. Assist the
sisters in their work of righteousness and
their blessings will all be shared by you,
and they will be encouraged to press for-
ward in their good work, and labor of love
to the needy.
	Apostle F. D. Richards said: we must
keep in mind that we are engaged in the
Lord's work. The 25,000 members of the
Relief Society in the Church are doing an
indescribable amount of good in their
various wards. Their Bishops realize their
mighty help, as well as the needy who re-
ceive their kindly aid. All this is part and
parcel of the work of the Lord. The
Presidency in their late visit to the Society
in Ogden expressed their approval of the
societies erecting houses in which to meet,
instruct each other and their daughters in
all things pertaining to life and health that
human suffering may be diminished and a
greater longevity attained,as well as do their
current business and perform their works
of charity. He extended his remarks con-
cerning the duty of the Society, and the
necessity of a house for them to occupy,
and expressed his pleasure at the earnest-
ness with which the people here had un-
dertaken this work. We must persevere
and progress or the work of God will
advance and leave us behind.
	Sister Richards spoke furthur of the
house, and was happy to hear of the inter-
est felt by the young ladies of the Improve-
ment Association in helping their mothers
of the Relief Society, to accomplish its
erection. Said they would live to see the
day when they would praise the Lord for
the course they had taken.
	Bishop McKay and others addressed


meeting with remarks appropriate and im-
portant to the occasion; when the people
repaired to a lot on a block west of the
Tabernacle, where the new building is to
be erected, and where master mason L. M.
Christiansen had all things ready for the
ceremony.
	After Singing and prayer an enclosure,
containing a record of the Society’s work
from its commencement, with names of its
officers and of the Bishopric, No. 5 of
Deseret News, Juvenile Instructor, WO-
MAN'S EXPONENT and Young Woman’s
Journal, was deposited.
	When the authorities present proceeded
to place the South East Corner Stone of
the Rellef Society’s Hall of Huntsville
Ward in position, and declared it was now
laid. The blessing of God was invoked
upon all who shall assist in its erection and
that the way may speedily be opened
whereby the necessary means may be ob-
tained for its accomplishment, which was
responded to with a hearty Amen.
	President Zina D. H. Young and Coun-
selor Bathsheba W. Smith spoke in a very
loving, kind, and energetic manner, feel-
ing fully assured the sisters would never
regret the noble effort they were now mak-
ing to obtain a house of their own.
	Several persons present donated various
sums of money towards the erection of the
building, and the interesting proceedings
were concluded with pleasure and gratitude
that will long be remembered by the good
people of Huntsville, and their visitors
present.
				 ELIZA A. TRACY, President. 
				EMMA BURROWS, Secretary.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HEALTH FOR WOMEN
	THE Viavi Home Treatment is a certain
cure for all female diseases and uterine
troubles. It has cured thousands of wo-
men who have despaired of ever getting
relief. The treatment is applied by the
patient herself, causes no inconvenience
and is absolutely safe and reliable—Cor-
respondence solicited. For full particulars
address Dr. E. B. Ferguson 121 B. Street
Salt Lake City.


CHRISTMAS NUT CAKE—Cream together
two cupfuls of sifted white sugar and half a
cupful of butter. Add the yolks of three
eggs, one cupful of sweet milk, three cup-
fuls of sifted flour and three teaspoonfuls
of baking powder. Stir into the beaten
whites of three eggs one cupful of blanched
and chopped nuts; add to the batter and
bake in small tins. Ice with vanilla icing.


A LETTER TO THE LADIES.

	FOR three years I have suffered pain and misery
in ailment peculiar to our sex. I have been
treated by some of the best physicians of this
city without material results. I took one treat-
ment of DR. DE JOZA’s prescription, and to my
surprise and pleasure, was able to be up working
and hav e been free from pain and other incon-
veniences. Being so pleased with the results I
have consented to give this testimonial and also
recommend it to my lady friends and all others
who so much need it.
	Very truly yours,
	PHEBE I. ALLRED.
Mrs. Allre[---] well known lady of Salt Lake
City. Her [---]ress will be given to any lady who
will write or call at our office. Also other
testimonials at your service. One trial treat-
ment, $2.00. All orders must be sent to the
Lady Managers SALT LAKE MEDICINE CO., 56
West, Second South St., Room 2.”


OBITUARIES.

	CENTER WARD—Has had a shadow of gloom
cast over it by the sudden demise of one of our
number, Maria Ann Simmons who departed this
life, Oct. 24, 1893 of typhoid fever, and leaves a
husband and six small children to mourn her loss,
she was a Relief Society Teacher, and always
ready to do her duties in all her callings. She was
born in Coney Heath Hatfield Hearts England,
Sep, 25th, 1855; and was baptized July 24 1865 by
by William D. Owen at Chamberwell, London.
Emigrated to Utah in the fall of 1881. May she
rest in peace.
			MARY A. WHITTAKER, Sec.
			BETSY G. WHITTAKER, Pres’t.

Dear Sister Wells:
	IT is my painful duty to inform you of the death
of our beloved President, Elizabeth Richards wife
of the late Silas Richards and daughter of Elijah
and Elizabeth McClenahan born June 12, 1809
in Kentucky. She departed this life after one
weeks severe suffering on Nov. 22nd, 1893 at the
residence of her daughter Mrs. Lucy A. Brady.
In 1846, she received her blessings in the Nauvoo
Temple and shared in the persecutions of the
Saints in being driven from their homes in that
city and arrived in Kanesville on the 24th, of
June in the same year remaining there ’till 1849.
	In 1847 when the pioneers returned to Winter
Quarters and a log Tabernacle was built, at
Kanesville Sister Richards fed and lodged thirty
of them for several days and it was at this time and
place where Brother Brigham Young was voted
in as President of the Church.
	Heber C. Kimball prophecied then that Sister
Richards and her family should never want bread
a prediction which has been literally fulfilled up
to this time.
	Sister Richards was a woman of nerve and
ndependence, she drove a team the entire dis-
tance across the plains from Nauvoo to Salt Lake
City she left Kanesville with her husband who
was Captain of one of the companies and her
family on July 10th, 1849 and arrived in Salt Lake
City on Oct,23rd of the same year, she was one
of the oldest settlers of Union, also President of
the Union district South Cottonwood ward for
eight years in the Relief Society, and in the
organization of the Union ward in 1877, she was
appointed to preside over the Union Ward
Relief Society on Aug. 8th, of that year and held
that position when she died, her health has been
feeble for the last two or three years, but she al-
ways had a good word of counsel to give to all.
All that loving hands could do was done for her,
which she very much appreciated.
	She was the mother of six children, twenty-two
grand children and forty-nine great grand
children. The funeral was largely attended, the
speakers were Bishop Joseph S. Rawlins, John
Sharp, Charles Denney and Bishop Ishmael
Phillips, all testified to her upright character and
her devotion to the work of God.
	Through the fiery ordeals of Illinois she passed
with no shelter, oft exposed to the cold wintry
blast yet never did she utter a word of regret at
joining so despised and persecuted a people.

To the celestial law through Joseph revealed,
	This our dear sister, obedience did yield;
To trials exempt thro’ which many have fell,
	She kept the covenant, and with her all is well.
A President, Counselor and a mother in need,
	To the sisters of Union, for years she has been;
Long, long remembered by many she’ll be;
	As they are tossed to and fro’ on life’s trouble-
		some sea.
						M. A. SMART, Sec’y.


MISS GRACE J. HAYNES, formerly a
teacher in Lawrence, Mass., has been elect-
ed principal of Olivet College, Olivet,
Mich.
	CHICAGO has thirty police matrons, with
a head matron over all. They have cared
for 35,119 women and girls during the
past year.
	NEARLY 11,000 women have registered
in Boston for school suffrage, a substantial
increase over last year.
	A VERY successful conference of women
workers was held at Leeds, on the 7th and
8th of November. Papers were read by
Lady Frederick Cavendish on the “The


Housing of the Poor;” by Mrs. Alfred
Ashe on "Health Teaching in Towns;" and
by Miss Hetty Jones on ‘‘The Condition of
Life in Houses of Business.” Many other
useful and practical subjects were discussed.
	A MAN cannot be truly eloquent if he
knows not how to listen.
