CHRISTMAS MUSINGS.

‘Twas Christmas eve the full grown moon
	O'er spread the earth with lucent light,
Radiant stars made all around
	Emit the rays of diamonds bright.
The air was cold, and sharp, and keen,
	And glistened with its silver sheen!
The snow was crisp, and fairly creaked
	Beneath the many moving feet.
The streets were thronged with grave and gay,
	The rich and poor together there,
The sunny-haired, the head of grey,
	Alike the brilliant scene did share.
	For art her beauties well displayed.
With charms enhanced by nature's aid;
	The peasant with his elbows bare
Jostling near the millionaire.
To watch the faces of the crowd
	Was study for an artist’s pen—
With voice subdued, or accent loud,
	Of children small, or stalwart men,
All pushing, crowding, struggling by—
	With gladsome laugh, or stifling sigh—
Each pressing on to reach some goal
	As haven for the anxious soul.
How beat these human hearts tonight?
	When every one should feel good cheer,
Do all bid sorrow take its flight?
	And throw aside each doubt and fear,
Or, are there some amid the throng
	Whose pleasures never tarry long?
Who, even at this Christmas tide,
	Have food, and warmth, and love denied?
Hark! list! we hear the tinkling bells
	Of Santa Claus upon the road!
What winsome joys their music tells!
	But pause—suppose we jump aboard
And take a little trip around,
	And with him see what can be found;
To view the world in varying phase,
	As seen by Santa's generous gaze.
We see the stately tow'ring walls
	Of wealthy homes on every hand,
Where festive music fills the halls,
	And luxury is at command:
Where Christmas bring increased delight
	Wlth all that tempts the taste and sight
Friends fondly vieing to impart
	The greatest joys to otheer hears.
In all their lives they've never known
	The dreaded pangs of cold or want,
Stranger to the sufferers groan,
	And poverty so grim and gaunt,
Then how should they be thought to know

[Column 2]
	The tithe of pain and want and woe,
That those less fortunate than they
	Are doomed to feel by night and day.
They see that Santa’s well supplied
	With varied and ample store;
Their darlings must not be denied,
	Their stockings must be brimming o,er
With all that youthful hearts could ask.
	Sure Santa's is a pleasing task!
With lavish hand they freely give
	When gifts are needed not to live.
Kind Santa Claus' unselfish heart
	Would fain be just and true to all.
He'd gifts alike to all impart—
	To rich and poor, to great and small.
But he is but an agent here
	To visit little children dear,
To give what others have decreed;
	Which often seems unjust indeed.
And this is why this Christmas-eve
	The lowly homes and hovels bare
Such poor and scanty gifts receive,
	And have so little love and care,
Why little children vainly sigh—
	When Merry Christmas, time draws nigh
To have their stockings brimming o’er
	With sweetmeats, and with gifts a score. 
O! could we but invest with power
	The Santa Claus of every home—
That henceforth from this very hour—
	In justice to each home to come;
That ev’ry little fluttering heart
	Might feel it had its ample part,
Nor wonder why the girls and boys
	Of other homes should have more toys.
As echoes by a master woke
	Reverberate in sweetest time,
Oh! Father ope with one great stroke
	The spring of human love sublime.
Oh make us feel as thou didst, when
	Thou gavest thy son to die for men;
That we may celebrate His birth
	With peace, good will, to all on earth.
	E. R. SHIPP.


SYMPATHY’S OFFERING.

	[To the bereaved family of Brother C. R.
Savage—in memory of his beloved wife the late
Mrs. Annie A. Savage who was for many years the
true friend of the Author.]
		EMILY H. WOODMANSEE.

A loyal friend, most worthy of respect;
	A faithful wife, a mother true and fond,
A gentle women, one of God’s “Elect”—
	Has left us for the bright and blest “Beyond.”
No more on earth, we’ll see her genial face—
	The index of her soul’s unconscious grace.
Her’s was the charm of friendly earnestness,
	Above formality, or proud display;
Her ready hand, full oft relieved distress—
	But never in an ostentatious way;
Sweet sympathy, and modest worth combined—
To form a nature lovable and kind.
Fate, fitted her with qualities to share—
	A pleasant home, with all “the Boon” implies,
Whose generous tone, reveals the father’s care,
	The children’s tasks, the mother’s forethought,
	wise,
Alas! where mutual love held gentle sway,


The household Queen is sadly missed today.

’Tis well, 'tis well when true companions go,
	If ne’er we grieved them, with intent unkind,
'Tis well, when, howsoever deep our woe,
	No stinging self-reproach distracts our mind,
’Tis well, when keen remorse, is out of place,
	So happily as in this special case.

Our friend received and loved the Gospel light,
	And prized the comfort that it truly brings,
Her spirit was refreshed e’en day and night
	With ‘‘living water” from celestial springs.
Her hope was like an anchor strong and sure 
Grounded on truth! eternal and secure.

Faith! was her friend, when sickness lingered
		near,
	And left its impress on her features pale;
Consoling faith! still whispered words of cheer
	And led her safely through the shadowy vale.
Sustained her through the spectre-haunted night,
That ushers in the dawn of “Endless Light!”

We would not selfishly recall her, yet
	Fond hearts are thrilled with human grief and
		pain,
Fond hearts are full of unexpressed regret— 
	Of old time friends, so very few remain,
Lo! one by one, they’re wafted from our view,
With hardly time to breathe a last adieu.

How can we fail to note, with sore dismay,
	The broken ranks of those we lov’d in youth?
Whose constancy is like a sunny ray,
	Who know us as we know ourselves forsooth.
Who while they sense our failings,not a few,
Most kindly recognize our virtues too.

Many await us on the “Distant shore”
	Who’ll gladly greet us, when life’s tasks are
		done,
Oh! happy day, that sees our conflict o’er;
	The battle fought, the victory fairly won;
Heaven grant us strength, sufficient for the fight,
	’Till with the loved ones gone we reunite.

	Salt Lake City Dec. 1893.


IN RURAL ENGLAND.

XXXVI.

	WHEN young Lord Walton made up his
mind to work and accepted the official
position, he set about it in earnest though
he had never known the routine, of actual
employment. In this as in everything else
Dr. Bland stood by him, and gave every
encouragement possible. Lady Anna freely
forgave the past and was more hopeful of a
happy future.
	The only serious source of uneasiness 
was the failure of Dr. Bland’s health. His
wife feared the change of climate and con-
ditions had been the cause, but the good
Doctor assured her he would soon be better
and that there was no need of worry about
him, he should pull through all right; the
winter was rather trying, weather severely
cold, and snow and frost such as in England
they had never experienced. Lady Anna
needed constant attention from her cousin
for she was not strong, and had more to en-
dure than usual, and the thought of what
might transpire, were the Doctor not able to
attend upon her when needed, was a
source of uneasiness to Jane Bland. Some-


[Column 1]
times she felt as if she must ask Mr. Har-
rison to come to America and join them, 
that there might be a strong support for 
them in case of any emergency, but with 
this feeling there was always the presenti-
ment which had haunted her from  their 
first acquaintance. She knew if she wrote 
for her cousin Clair, Mr. Harrison would 
certainly accompany him, and besides 
Clair would not be the least use in any 
difficulty; he was very boyish and excellent 
company when all was prosperous, but in 
adversity he would not be able to render 
the moral support necessary. Mrs. Bland 
felt in her inmost soul some desperate trial 
was at hand, and yet there was no one to
whom she could speak of it.  While these 
impressions were so strong upon her, Lady 
Anna Walton was entirely unconcered, 
except occasionally noticing how pale and 
thin the Doctor was growing, but when-
ever she mentioned the fact to her cousin
she changed the subject, and Lady Anna felt
there could not be any danger, or her 
cousin would observe it. 
	The wardrobe of the little stranger soon 
expected occupied much of Lady Anna's 
time and thought, and her little Edward 
was growing quite interesting, and she was 
really more contented and happier than she 
had been before.  Maitland made many 
new acquaintances in his business, and as 
the men he met were mostly substantial 
enterprising men of ability and sterling 
character, his own aims in life expanded 
and his ideas changed so much that he was 
really companionable to Dr. Bland, who 
enjoyed his society and conversation and 
his friendship was a real pleasure and
satisfaction.
	The Doctor realized in part his failing 
health, and when he found his cough 
so troublesome as to keep him indoors, he 
reflected seriously as to the future; he 
hoped his wife was not aware of what he 
knew himself concerning his condition, 
and she, poor woman had the same feeling. 
It was painful to both, and yet each dreaded 
to acknowledge the sad truth. 
	And so the winter wore on, there was 
much to interest the Doctor in this new 
country, and his advice was asked by men 
in grave matters of state, as well as in his 
profession, he ranked high in the community 
was liberal in his views, and often a 
mediator between those who differed 
radically in opinions, especially of a re-
ligious character. Such men are useful 
members in any society, and in trying
times, when cummunities are being formed, 
such stalwarts are invaluable. The ad-
vantage a physician has of intimate as-
sociation with people in all conditions of 
life is highly educational in the best and 
broadest sense of the word, and Dr. Bland 
was naturally a man of superior qualities 
and attributes, and withal so modest and 
unassuming that aspiring, ambitious men
were not afraid to trust him.  He inspired 
confidence in all and his sympathies were deep and strong. 
	In the long winter evenings when his 
wife was busy with her needle if his cough 
would permit they would talk freely of the 
future of the colonies, and of their own two 
families and the Doctor dwelt much upon 
the future of their boy, cautioning his wife 
in regard to the discipline he must have to 
prepare him for life's battles.  And yet 
these two husband and wife could not 
open their hearts to each other concerning 
that which was of such vital importance. 
	When alone in his study Dr. Bland oc-
[Column 2]
cupied himself with writing out his 
thoughts and views, determined that if he 
must leave his wife and child his affairs 
should be so plainly  understood that there
could be no complications to perplex and 
distress his wife whom he loved devotedly 
and reverenced as brave and wise men ever
do when happily mated. 
	Spring came at last, though it was late 
in April before the frost and snow entirely 
disappeared, the winter had been long yet 
not unpleasant, for in many ways there had 
been true domestic enjoyment and agreeable 
social intercourse among the friends and 
neighbors.
	No news had come from over the sea ex-
cept from Maitland's mother, there were 
few near relatives, to write and Mr. Har-
rison and Clair were still wandering they 
knew not exactly where.  Some of the 
servants had letters from the vicinity of 
the old Castle, telling of vague rumors 
concerning Walton Hall and the doings of 
the Italian Count with the horrid foreign 
name, but Mrs. Bland warned the servants 
not to speak of it to her cousin, and though 
they were fond of gossip they kept it 
among themselves.  Finally news came to 
Maitland that there was a separation,
there had been an open quarrel caused by 
the Count's jealousy, and Maitland was 
wanted at Walton Hall.  It was a critical 
time for him to leave home, his wife needed 
the most tender care; the Doctor's health 
precarious, in a new country where every-
thing was so different, it was a most perp-
plexing dilemma.  His lawyers at home 
insisted upon his actual presence, and would 
take no refusal.  Mrs. Bland was a sort of 
oracle in family matters and the subject 
was referred to her for a final decision. 
   Mrs. Bland could not conscientiously 
consent to Maitland's going away until her 
cousin had passed the ordeal that was now 
very soon expected, and the new mansion 
was unfinished except a few rooms 
they had moved into and occupied during the 
winter. 
	"My cousin is too delicate at present to 
be left, wait a few months or weeks at least and 
see her made more comfortable before 
you think of going for you may be detained 
a long time, these family matters per-
taining to business and property are not 
settled hurriedly, and Maitland, if you 
should leave Anna and anything serious 
were to happen, you would never cease to 
regret it," said Mrs. Bland.
	"When I do go Jane I shall trust all to 
you, I have no one competent to take 
charge in my absence, Simpson does well 
enough when I am here to give orders, but 
Anna has no idea of managing and I can 
only depend upon your supervision. It 
seems a great deal to ask of you, and the 
Doctor so poorly, but I'll try and recom-
pense you by bringing you some luxuries 
when I come back again, such as cannot be 
obtained in America."
	"I shall do whatever is possible for my 
cousin under any circumstances, without 
expecting remuneration, it is a pleasure to 
me to be with her, and were anything to 
happen," here Mrs. Bland completely broke 
down, and Maitland was at a loss to know 
what she meant.  He could not understand, 
but was completely adrift, for he considered 
Jane a heroine equal to any emergency, and if she failed what could be done, he would not dare to go away. Mrs. Bland regained her voice in a few moments, and went on to say, that she realized her husband's condition to be serious and in view of Anna's sickness too, he needed great 
courage. "The servants are very good especially Anna's maid and the nurse you brought from Waiton Hall, but we need 
some one who has greater knowledge, and I have been looking about to secure such help, but have not succeeded."
	"I shall bring my mother to America when I return and we shall feel more settled, I will make some disposal of the 
estate, for after what has happened mother will not live at Walton Hall alone." Mrs. Bland made no reply she felt how weak 
Lady Walton would be were any real trouble to come, but could not say so to Maitland of his only near relative. Maitland assured Mrs. Bland he would remaiu until she thought proper for him to go and so matters went on as hertofore Dr. Bland improved a little as the weather grew warmer so he could ride about over the hill and visit his patients, and his wife grew more hopeful of his recovery. Maitland wrote his mother he could not leave his wife as yet, and the lawyers must pospone the business. 
	One beautiful morning in May, in the 
new mansion, a daughter was born, and for 
days after Lady Anna's life was despaired 
of; Dr. Bland watched her constantly and 
her cousin Jane never left the sick room, 
Maitland's visit to England was given up, 
or never alluded to, and throughout the 
house, all was dark and quiet, except the 
whisperers of the attendants, save when in 
her delirium the young mother talked 
hysterically, and confusedly of the old 
castle and of Walton Hall. Mr. Barrington 
the young minister who had made such a 
decidedly religious impression upon 
Lady Walton came often to see her with Mait-
land' consent, for he was so utterly power-
less to render any assistance himself, he was 
glad to have the support of so faithful a 
friend as Barrington had been from their 
first metting, and Mrs. Bland felt spiritually 
uplifted by the presence of this devoted 
minister of the "new faith" as it was called 
by them; Lady Anna was always more 
passive and rested better when he was in 
the house. 
During the early days of her illness Anna 
had spoken to her cousin prophetically of 
what might transpire and Jane had carefully 
noted it all, for her own premonitions con-
firmed it and she had told the Doctor, and 
also talked to Maitland, of her fears. The 
whole household mourned, for the lady 
was greatly beloved; she did not seem to 
suffer severe pain, but gradually grew 
weaker and weaker, and finally passed in-
to a deep sleep never more to waken in this 
world; just as her mother died or as Mrs. 
Bland had heard it from some of the old 
servants at Hilliard castle. 
	Maitland' grief was uncontrollable, he 
could not and would not be comforted, his 
remorse was pitifnl, and Dr. Bland had 
hard work to persuade him to leave the 
room after all was over while the attendants 
with Mrs. Bland performed the last sad 
services for their "sweet mistress"


WOMANS EXPONENT


MISS GRACE CHISHOLM, of Cambridge
University; Miss Maltby, formerly of
Wellesly College; and Mary F. Winston, of
Chicago, have received special permission
from the German government to enter the
University of Gottingen with the same
privileges which the men enjoy. Miss
Winston and Miss Chisholm are in the de-
partment of mathematics, and Miss Maltby
in that of physics.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


SUMMERLAND.

(Suggested by reading Book of Alma 353rd, page.)

	'Tis a land where the waters of life,
		Gently flow through the vales of the blest,
	Where the fainting and thirsty may drink,
		There the Souls that are weary find rest.

		We shall meet—we shall greet—
	All our loved ones in fair Summerland,
		We shall meet—we shall greet—
	All our loved ones in fair Summerland.

	'Tis a land where the fairest of flowers,
		Deck the vales with their sweet Eden-bloom;
	Where the angelic petals shed forth,
		Everlasting celestial perfume,
							We shall meet &c.

	In this beautiful land of the blest,
		Where sorrows and tears never come,
	There is sweet rest for souls that are faint
		In that glorious, bright, beautiful Home.
							We shall meet—

	’Tis a land where the Pilgrims find rest—
		When their trials and troubles are gone:
	Where the bright Angels welcome them home,
		To this land of the sanctified ones.
							We shall meet—
								C. L. W.


TO THE SISTERS.

	OUR Conference is now numbered with
past blessings; how gratifying it would
have been for all the Saints to have par-
taken of the precious instruction we re-
ceived; but believing all prayerful saints
will receive a portion of the same spirit
that will strengthen comfort and encourage
them in every circumstance and in all trials
is a great consolation to me, the instruc-
tion from our brethren to all that would
favor Zion and that the key was turned in
favor of Zion, the thinning of the curtain
that has separated from us the far off bless-
ings.
	In the Sisters’ Conference President F. D.
Richards instruction was precious; assur-
ances for us to continue in our labors as we
have ever done in our society in all its
branches; it would have cheered every
heart to have heard it, and I trust it will
for God surely gave the pattern and all
will prosper if we are humble and faithful
to our duties, to be sure that the needy do not
suffer, Heavenly mission; that we may
meet our Father’s approval, and if great
wisdom be used in our words to cheer, and
with our deeds of kindness, that all may
have the spirit of gratitude to God for our
many blessings and I pray that they may
fill the soul of every sister that we may ad-
vance in good works and that we may be
strong and united.
	We three sisters, myself and my Counse-
lors, Sisters Jane S. Richards and Bathsheba
W. Smith are one in desiring God’s special
blessings upon our sisters, yes all, but
especially those who have so nobly labored
in the Relief Society. It was very much
regretted by me that Sister Richards had to
be absent from our Conference on account
of ill health (but trust she will soon re-
cover) as it was suggested in her absence,
that the President of each Stake organiza-
tion of the Relief Society would act as aids
for us to call upon; (I have said I, but we
three are the only ones that I wish to con-
vey in meaning) that we all may be blest
spiritually and temporally.


Take the Book of Doctrine and Covenants, 
we three as one with the Presidents of
Stakes, read and study the Lectures on
Faith, and that the Presidents of Stakes
would take it as a mission to ask all local
Presidents to have these lectures read in
their meetings that we may understand
alike, and mothers be able to instruct the
children and converse with the Primary and
Sunday School children to make it truly
profitable.
	My sisters this needs no comment,
that when we rest from our labors our
works will follow us in the hands of our
children. Let us be wise, humble, peace-
ful and prudent, not distrusting God’s
Providence and all will be well.
Oct. 15, 1893.				Z. D. H. Y.


To all our sisters throughout the world.
Since writing the above I can surely recom-
mend having the Book of Doctrine and
Covenants especially the lectures on Faith,
read by all the sisters, as in the Temple all
that work there meet, read and discuss these
lovely and eternal truths that edify and
satisfy the reasoning soul. We do not say
go and do, but come let us be strong, we
do not forget while in that precious place,
the absent and tried; the lectures are read
and questions every week, if there is a
power in reason that removes doubt and
gives assurance I have been fully assured,
for when the thought came tome it had not
been tried by the sisters in the Temple. I
trust that every Stake President will com-
ply and not delay in making it a request
and eveu if read at home, have some one
read it when together, you will have a por- 
tion of the same spirt by which we are
edified in the Temple, by faith and prayer
nothing doubting we shall see eye to eye,
having assure foundation. Our Sabbath
School children can be more strengthened
at home by their intelligent mothers, who
may be weary with toil, but by a little
management the reading can be a pleasant
home entertainment; we all should be
familiar with these lectures that we may res-
pect the Great Giver of these blessings and
the Prophet of the living God who has
laid down his life for the truth’s sake.
					ZINA D. H. YOUNG.
Dec. 5, 1893.


U.W. P. C.

	THIS organization met on the 29th, day
of the month, November, with the new of-
ficers in charge. The meeting was held in
the apartments of President Ellis R. Shipp
who occupied the chair, and was called
promptly on time, 7-30, p. m.
	The meeting was opened by prayer of-
fered by the honorary President E. B. Wells
and the roll call was responded to by
Thanksgiving Sentiments, original or
selected.
	Mrs. Nellie C. Taylor and Mrs. Elizabeth
Stevenson, were admitted to membership
and Mrs. M. A. V. Greenhalch was voted
in as Vice President at large for the club.
	The Secretary, read the By Laws of the 
club, and following a suggestion of Presi-
dent E. B. Wells, they were amended to
admit of the acceptance of members on
verbal recomendations instead of the writ-
ten ones hitherto required by the By Laws;
which amendment was readily adopted by
all of the members.
	The evening program was then carried
out as follows:


New England Thanksgiving stating the
origin of the custom among the Pilgrims
and the way of celebrating it ever since.
This interesting paper was prepared and
read by Mrs. E. B. Wells.
	The Sec'y then read a paper written by
Mrs. Lillie T. Freeze on the "Influence of
Thanksgiving" on the spiritual being;
making some quotations given in literature,
ancient and modern, etc.
	Miss Olea Shipp favored the club with a 
choice instrumental selection which was
much appreciated.
	President Ellis R. Shipp read an original
poem on the subject of the hour. "Thanks-
giving."
	Several of the members gathered around
the piano and sang Will Carlton's Thanks-
giving Hymn.
	Mrs. M. A. Y. Greenhalch, in compliance
with a request of the President, addressed
the members. After expressing her
pleasure at meeting with the club this be-
ing her first attendance, though a member,
she described in a pleasing style the man-
ner of observing Thanksgiving in Old
England, gave the origin of the custom in
that country which dated back to the
Druids.
	This completed the program and the
regular meeting was adjourned, benediction
pronounced by Mrs. E. Stevenson.
	Dainty refreshments were served ac-
companied by the usual pleasant chat,
during which time Miss Olea Shipp rendered
some low, sweet music much to the enjoy-
ment of all.
	The numbers for the December program
are: Answering roll call by naming
Authors, and a quotation from their most
noted work. Essay on the Sixth Sense (or
something else) L. L. G Richards;
Biography of J. G. Whittier, Dr. R. B.
Pratt; Selection from Whittier, Miss
Patterson; Music, Gladys Woodmansee;
New Year’s Poem, Mrs. Dickinson.
		GLADYS WOODMANSEE, Rec. Sec.


REORGANIZATION.
	A SPECIAL meeting of the 8th, Ward,
Relief Society was held in the Ward as-
sembly rooms, Salt Lake City Oct. 24, 1893;
for the purpose of reorganizing and select-
ing a new president our former one having
passed away.
	The meeting was opened by singing “We
thank Thee O, God for a Prophet.” Prayer
by J. D. H. Mc.Allister. Singing “Oh
my Father.”
	The following sisters were then selected
and set apart; Catherine K. Palmer, Presi-
dent, Jane Cowan first counselor, Sarah
J. Chamberlain second counselor, Jennie
Mortensen secretary, Jennette McMurin
Ass’t. Sec’y, Eliza Frost treasurer.
	The Primaary Association was also re-
organized as follows, Sister Ann Cartwright
president, Jennie Mortensen first counselor,
Nettie Frost second counselor, Annie Cart-
wright secretary.
	Remarks were then made by Sisters Elmina
S. Taylor and E. B. Wells and Bishop
Sheets, after which the meeting was ad-
journed. Singing “Guide us O, Thou
Great Jehovah.” Benediction by Charles
Tuckfield.

—Socrates.

TO OUR PATRONS AND AGENTS.
	WE very much regret being obliged to
issue our Christmas and New Year’s paper
in one number, but unavoidable circum-
stances compel it, and we earnestly entreat
the patrons and agents of the Exponent
to use their utmost influence and exertions
to increase its circulation, and urge upon
agents in all localities to solicit subscriptions
and make collections of outstanding accounts
wherever possible, meantime taking into
consideration the valuable service it has
rendered the women of the Territory and
throughout the Relief Society everywhere,
as well as the advancement of women
generally especially in reference to the
ballot for women. All members of the
Relief Society and all who believe in
Woman’s Suffrage and woman’s uplifting
in all directions should subscribe for the
first woman’s paper published in the Rocky
Mountain region.


[Column 1]
A HAPPY NEW YEAR.
	A HAPPY NEW YEAR is the salutation
that greets old and young at the present
time, and it is pleasant to hear the cheerful
tones repeating the hopeful words that
never grow old, but each year brings them
back fresh and crisp as ever.
	The new year comes full of hopefulness
from all for better days, and yet the last
dear old year brought with it many bless-
ings. After all none of us know whether
these days of adversity when money is
tight, and a panic seems to have struck the
country are not good for us all; sorrows have
their purpose and if these trials and per-
plexities will make us more gratified and
appreciative of the many blessings we do
enjoy, and lift our hearts to Him who is the
fountain of all light and love, if there is a
lesson to be learned through suffering, then
we should not repine, but accept our cup of
bitterness, if such it be, trusting in Him
who will overrule all for the good of those
who acknowledge His hand in all things.
	If we as a people should enumerate the
blessings of the year that is now numbered
with the past, we should find much to re-
joice over. The Salt Lake Temple to
which so many have looked anxiously for-
ward has been finished and dedicated to
the Lord, great promises have been given
on conditions of faithfulness, that should
encourage all who believe in the Latter
day work. None of our great leaders have
been taken from us, during this past year,

[Column 2] 
their lives have all been spared; throughout
the vales of the mountains peace and plenty
has reigned, and no great calamity has
befallen us here, such as has visited many
other countries and localities. For this
protection, and these blessings and many
others innumerable, we should be humble
and give the praise to our Father in Heaven
who controls the elements, guides all
things, and can turn the rivers of water
whithersoever He will.
	If some extra exertion is necessary that
the poor and unfortunate may have food
and raiment during the winter, it will
bring into active exercise the kindly and
generous feelings and sentiments of those
who minister to the needy, and every bless-
ing bestowed will enrich the giver, for
charity bestowed in a true spirit is a bless-
ing and development of good and brings a
rich reward and satisfaction to Him who
gives freely and wisely. The present con-
dition of scarcity too, may awaken thought
in the direction of providing against such a
state of things in the future; employment
for the working people is one of the best
preventives of poverty and want. Men
and women who can co-operate together
for charitable purposes, should also be able
to co-operate to establish industries, that
men may work for their bread, and women
too, and not be dependent on charity; this
would be truer charity than the giving of
alms.
	Certainly no thoughtful person can help
feeling that the past year has been a mar-
velously great one in the advancemeut of
the world. The Columbian Exposition
alone would mark it as an epoch in the
history of all time, significant for its bril-
liant success and the knowledge of industry,
science and art, placed within the reach of
so many millions of people for their enlight-
enment upon these fundamental subjects.
The great Congresses and Parliaments too,
where men and women eminent for learn-
ing and achievements from all parts of the
world read and sopke to the thousands upon
thousands, was an education in itself of the
higher and grander truths of humanity and
the universe. Reflecting upon these won-
drous events with all the details in one’s
mind that can possibly be comprehended,
one queries whether the new year just
opening can give us as great opportunities
and as much more food for cultivation as
the one just gone.
	In summing up the achievements of the
years it must not be forgotten the rapid
strides forward in the cause of woman’s
suffrage. New Zealand afar off holds up a
beacon light to the world. Australia is
marching onward in the same direction and
that which was least expected has already
come to pass. Colorado our near neighbor
has done herself proud in admitting woman
to equal suffrage. Everywhere the cause
is gaining, and ere long other brilliant stars
will be added to Wyoming and Colorado.
	The tendency of the World’s Fair was
decidedly in the direction of woman’s po-
litical emancipation. This fact was con-
stantly apparent. The conspicuous part
taken by women in the actual business of
the Fair, the good judgment and financial
ability displayed by the Board of Lady
Managers, and the many women com-
missioners and foremost workers, has
caused many men and women heretofore
opposed to woman’s enfranchisement to
very materially change their views upon
his subject. Not only has this been a

[Column 3] 
wonderful year but the 19th century is,
we believe, unparalleled in history since the
advent of the Savior the “Light of the
World” the Christian era.


CHRISTMAS-TIDE.

		“AT Christmas-tide the open hand
		Scatters its bounty o’er sea and land,
		And none are left to grieve alone,
		For love is heaven and claims its own.”

	The Christmas-tide gives each and all an
opportunity to do good in some way, to
each other, to a friend or foe and certainly
he who forgives is more blest than he who
bestows alms, for to forgive those who
have wronged us is greater. The Apostle
Paul said if we bestow all our goods to feed
the poor and have not charity it avails us
nothing, then true Charity is something
more, something higher, and what is there
more difficult than to forgive, yet we expect
to be forgiven daily for our offences. Can
we not once in a year cherish the spirit of
forgiveness to all mankind. Be at peace
with all the world. Do good to those that
have wounded us or hurt us. Be
magnanimous and try to follow the example
of Him whose birth we celebrate. Instead
of this many of us follow our own inclina-
tions, do those things that are pleasant and
agreeable, eat drink and are merry, forget-
ting the sick, the sorrowing and the hungry,
we shut ourselves up in our own narrow
circle, and might the unhappy and disconso-
late to whom a kind word and a trifle would
be so precious. So we harden our hearts
and pass on our way heedless of the great
tide of humanity around us, and yet we
justify ourselves and say we have done what
we could.
	Christmas-tide is the overflow of bless-
ings temporal and spiritual, the reaching
out to the needy in body and soul, ad-
ministering the good things of this life and
also that which will lead upward to the
higher and more exalted state, and above
all else the forgiving each other’s weakness-
es and shortcomings. Wherein we fail to
do this our Christmas-tide is not to us to our
own souls the satisfaction it might have
been. We have lost something by our lack
of humility, we shall never reach the high-
est cultivation in our own character, in our
own souls until we can forgive as we hope
to be forgiven. We who live in this
favored land of Zion are wonderfully blest
in many ways, our homes are full of plenty,
if we have not the luxuries of the wealthy,
we have the substantial blessings of food
and raiment, homes and comforts, and the
protection thrown around us that uplifts
and cheers the toiler in life’s battles.
	Everywhere almost throughout the world
we hear of the cry of the working people
for better conditions, better dwell-
ings, higher wages, consideration of needs,
and the thoughtful and sympathetic feelings
of those who bear rule and those who em-
ploy help are being exercised upon these
momentous questions of the time, labor
and capital. Here these conditions do not
exist. Working men are not compelled to
submit to tyranny of any kind, there is
such freedom and equality that there can
scarcely be a complaint of this sort, there is
more land to till, more soil to cultivate,
more deserts to subdue, and it is not neces-
sary to crowd into large towns or cities, but
the independent, ambitious young man can
go to and consider a field and buy it and
plant a vineyard, and be his own master.


For these and many other blessings in-
numerable almost, we as a people should
be grateful and at the blessed Christmas
time should not forget to render praise and
thanksgiving to our Father in Heaven for
His mercies to this people, and in our heart
of hearts not only remember this, but his
children not so favored with good things to
whom we may impart a portion of the good
things we enjoy.
	To teach the children to share with others,
one should set the example himself (or her-
self) it is so much better than precept, and
if the little ones are somewhat selfish of toys
and trifles they will remember in after years
the pattern of their parents, for these
memories do follow, and when the best be-
loved are far away, or have gone to the
“other side,” the lessons they taught, the
examples they set are more potent; one
goes back to childhood on such uncommon
days, involuntarily remembering the olden
time, when as boy and girl, or youth and
maiden, all the world was like one great
holiday and sorrows were unknown, or
were the exception; and all the beautiful
traits of character in the loved ones at home,
seem to beam forth out of the distant past,
and a solemn determination to imitate the
excellence of these heroes and heroines
whose memories one fondly cherishes is the
result of many a fireside, or lonely reverie
at Christmas time or such red letter days.
	It is good to observe these days set apart
for love and friendship, for this age is so
eminently practical that without some such
observances, social and family life would
lose much of its tenderness and sweetness.
The reunion around the festive board, and
better still the gatherings at eventide by
the hearthstone at home, awaken the most
affectionate recollections, or call forth the
simple tales one loves to hear repeated of
the former days, of the daily labor, or of ad-
venture by land or sea, or the old songs,
and the early lessons at school, the sport
the games, or the invigorating exercises of
work or pastime.
	Such pleasant evenings when large
families assemble at the paternal mansion,
are waymarks in life forever after, and the
custom of families’ assembling together
ought to be perpetuated, for it increases
love, and regard for each other, and for the
head of the family, and if simple meals
were prepared and less show and display
made, these occasions would not be so rare;
it is often the expense that hinders, and
one has to consider this—to be sure, but if
simpler preparations could be the general
rule there would be more real social and
domestic happiness, and less ruin through
debt and extravagance.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
RELIEF SOCIETY CONFERENCE.

[Continued from Oct. 15.]

	AFTERNOON SESSION:—Meeting opened
with singing, "Do what is right," &c.
Prayer was offered by President M, Isabella
Horne, continued by singing "Come come
ye Saints. '
	President Mary Pitchforth of Juab Stake
was the first speaker, she reported all the
branches of the Society in good condition
except Eureka, the mines there having
closed had caused a depression in every-
thing, spoke about the wheat that had been
stored and of blessings in general and
mentioned the large number of yards of
carpet the society had made and donated to

[Column 2]
the Manti Temple which was very much
appreciated, the sisters were always willing
and generous when called upon to do to
the very extent of their ability.
	President Susan Grant of Davis Stake
spoke principally of what had been done in
saving grain, urged the mothers to be united
with the fathers in using their influence
at home and elsewhere, union in the
family was necessary for the training of
the children.
	President Elizabeth Yates of Millard
Stake explained that they were all working
people not many wealthy and none very
poor, she tried to be careful of the wheat
and keep adding to it, she often felt sur-
prised to get the little things that are do-
nated from time to time, and the good ac-
complished through the generosity of the
sisters who have little enongh for their own
comfort, yet are always willing to divide
	THE next speaker was Apostle F. D.
Richards who stated that he felt compli-
mented in being invited to attend the
Sisters’ conference, was glad to see so many
present, thought we should realize what we
were called to do, as a society, and not get
in too great a hurry, but take things
quietly; a sentiment prevailed largely about
strong-willed persons, but that will should
be regulated if good were to be the result;
but when one says "I can do nothing of
myself only to do the will of my Father
that sent me," that was the right spirit and
feeling.
	President Richards referred to the meet-
ing held in the morning at the Church
University Building in which President
Woodruff had spoken to the brethren as-
sembled in regard to the grain that had
been stored up by the sisters for a time of
want or need, said the President had given
excellent advice which would be published
for the benefit of all interested in the
matter.
	The Lord has promised many adversities
and famine is one of them; the sisters had
been helping the brethren all the time, to
build houses, and now the speaker
thought the brethren would help the sisters
in return, thought if we solicited their help
in the spirit of kindness, and asked for
what we wanted and kept on asking and
importuning we would get it; the speaker
urged the sisters to take good care of the
sick and needy and not hesitate because
they are not good, they must have atten-
tion, "cherish the attribute of love, that
will help you in all your labors, it is an old
saying "Love laughs at locksmiths," it is
certainly a potent power, we should all
cultivate it, we all need it;" he then gave
some advise to the sisters in regard to buy-
ing wheat, study the markets, knew wheat
would be rising in value. President Young
and other brethren traveled through the
Stakes of Zion counseling the people to lay
up wheat; quoted from the 14th Chapter of
Revelations "Another angel flying etc.
for the hour of his judgment has come;"
a hurricane has just swept over the coast
which has killed two or three thousand
people at one fell swoop; when people build
banks, hotels etc., let them remember the
hour of His judgment has come." Presi-
dent Cannon has been speaking recently
upon false doctrine being insidiously in-
troduced and that much mischief is being
done, one family had asked to have their
names taken from the Church books.
Elder Richards warned the people against
these false doctrines, said every Latter-day
Saint who don't know the truth of this

[Column 3]
work, should get a testimony by faith and
prayers; sometimes in sickness people run
after the Doctor and lose their faith in the
servants of God and the priesthood; "these
people may make great pretensions but the
Lord has warned us against those decep-
tions, could we stand firm if we saw fire
come down out of heaven? by and by there
will be magicians, they will be equal to
Moses day when sticks were turned to
serpents and back again to sticks;" Presi-
dent Richards also alluded to Theosophy,
and other erroneous doctrines, these things
are moving more among the sisters than
the brethren; then referred to the doctrine
of reincarnation, said it used to be called
the baby resurrection, it was brought to the
attention of Joseph the Prophet, by a
polished gentleman, Joseph said there was
no truth in it, other errors sprang up,
Millerism, second-adventists, spiritualists
and mesmerizes, "the Lord is not going to
tell us, beforehand the day nor the hour of
certain events: in the Savior's time, no one
knew except the Father and Him only,
stick to the scriptures and hearken to the
words of the servants of God, and the
Gospel as revealed to you in your native
lands, thank the Lord for every blessing."
Elder Richards closed his remarks by bless-
ing all assembled and said go home rejoic-
ing, your hearts light, may the Holy Spirit
comfort you and gladden your souls, impart
to others, your sisters and associates in this
work, the good things you have heard here
and during the Conference that has just
closed.
	President Zina D. H. Young spoke of
the love she felt for the sisters, and blessed
them in her tender motherly way, referred
to her visit to Chicago and the many
noble women she met there, knew that
they were working for the good of their
sex. Joseph the Prophet had turned the
key of knowledge for all women, and ever
since that time woman's influence had been
greater in all directions for good. Presi-
dent Young then said she wished to in-
crease the sphere of usefulness of the
sisters who were presiding in the several
stakes and have them become aids to the
General Presidency of the Relief Society
to herself and her Counselors, that they
might visit among the adjacent Stakes of
Zion either by invitation or appointment,
stated that it was sometimes impossible for
her or either of her Counselers to attend
the Sisters' Conferences that were remote
from here and in this way it might be
beneficial to those at a distance to have the
President or Presidents near attend each
others Conferences, much good might be
effected by the interchange of ideas and
thoughts, and it would promote greater
union and love by becoming familiarly
acquainted with each others' labors, she had
been inspired to give this counsel; it had
come to her like a power and a glow and
joy, and she added "if you do this you will
have joy in your labors, and in cooperating
together and it will increase faith and a
better understanding of our great work
and give us renewed strength to perform
our duties. It is our privilege to be hum-
ble and faithful and if we are we shall be
prepared for all our duties, the Lord bless
you my dear sisters and give you his Holy
Spirit for your guide.
	President Mary A. Hyde Sanpete Stake
felt we had enjoyed a feast of fat things,
said in that county the sisters had nearly a
thousand bushels of wheat not much
chance to glean now as they did formerly


because of the machinery taking up the
grain so clean, that none was left for glean-
ing, Sister Hyde made other excellent re-
marks.
	President Sarah M. Kimball thought of
many subjects that might be presented, but
felt time was short, spoke of the little fund
the sisters laid up, a missionary fund in fact,
though the means should be used wisely
and for improvement, mentally, spiritually
and financially, thought the Lord would
not give us means, if we did not make a
success, there were many things that made
us feel akin and and reach out to all the
brotherhood of man and the sisterhood of
woman. Referred specially to the meeting
of the Woman's Congress held on Sunday
in the Art Palace in one of the great halls,
when sixteen ordained women ministers
sat upon the platform representing thirteen
denominations, one was clad in the sacer-
dotal robes of the Episcopal Church, it was
a beautiful sight to see these grand women
thus assembled; the speaker asked the
Lord to bless and restore Sister Richards
(who had not been able to come to the con-
ference) and to bless Brother Richards who
had done so much to elevate woman and to
help us all in the Relief Society; said a few
words in regard to women speaking at
the funerals of the sisters thought it was
quite proper that they should.
	At the close of the Conference, Apostle
Richards made a few appropriate remarks,
said Sister Young's inspiration concerning
the Presidents of Stakes was timely," two
or three visit together in convenient local-
ities, invite each other, and so on, it will be
a mutual benefit and there will be a corps of
laborers under the direction of the President
and her Counselors. The sisters of the
Relief Society are laborers and mothers in
Israel in thought and experience; and
women are more inclined to be
spiritual-minded, than the brethren,
and are more refined,—we have a fore-
taste here of the life that is to come, would
like to mention the National Woman's Relief
Society, you have in it a legal organization
of holding property in the Society; some of us
are afraid of the name, we have grown a
little, and we have a right to the name,
that should not alter the spirit but
strengthen the work, men have been or-
ganized into an Ecelesiatical organization in
wards also stake organizations, it has not
altered things at all, neither will it with
you, but put you into a position to hold
your property, these corporate powers
ought not to confuse you, or lead to differ-
ences: Constantine the Emperor turned over
everything to the law, we have as a society
become national and perhaps will be In-
ternational, this society is a great body and
is of some consequence and standing among
other great Associations, and the name
should correspond." President Richards
then announced the Rev. Howis lecture on
music and morals to be given in the evening
at that place. Singing "Lord Dismiss us
with thy Blessing." Benediction by Apos-
tle F. D. Richards. Conferenoe adjourn-
ed for six months.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	MINUTES of the Ladies’ Semi-monthly
meeting held in the 14th, Ward Assembly 
Hall, Saturday Nov. 18, 1893.
	Counselor Elizabeth Webb presided.
The meeting was opened by singing "Arise

[Column 2]
O, Glorious Zion,”&c. Prayer by Sister
Diana Reid. After singing, minutes of
last meeting read and approved. Counselor
Webb gave some excellent instructions to
the sisters and desired them to bear their
testimony that the time be not wasted.
Spoke at some length on Temple work, ad-
vised the sisters to attend to that labor and
closed by saying “Let us be faithful sisters
and not lose all we have labored for, these
many years. Gounselor Elizabeth Steven-
son said we will come here to be educated
and instructed. Felt to praise the Lord
for strength to labor in the Temple, urged
the sisters to put away all light mindedness
and follies, to strive to be refined, for these
are some of the requirements of the king-
dom of God. Prayed that we all might be
faithful, and desired the sisters not to
quench the spirit but speak of the goodness
of God.
	Sister Price, spoke of the goodness of
God to her, and bore her testimony to the
truth of the Gospel. She also desired to
be one with her sisters to the end of the
race.
	Sister Hazon bore a faithful testimony
to the truth of the Gospel, desired to be
yet able to work in the Temple and prayed
that we might all enjoy the good things
prepared for the righteous.
	Sister Illsmough bore her testimony that
Joseph was a prophet of God felt that the
Lord would strengthen her to do good,
prayed God to bless us in this great and
glorious work.
	Sister J. C. Howe felt to endorse all that
had been said to day. Spoke of the work
she was doing in the Temple, felt that all
souls were precious and equal in the sight
of God and then to think that in our weak-
ness, we are enabled to redeem some of
these precious ones. Also gave much
good counsel and encouragment to the
sisters.
	Sister Phelps was thankful for the spirit
of God that she felt with us today, said she
loved to see the faces of her sisters, who
meet here. Was glad that she was able to
meet with them sometimes. Desired to be
humble and faithful to the end.
	Sister Forsythe bore her testimony, re-
joiced that her health was improved that
she could meet with her sisters, desired
that we might all be among the blessed
faithful ones.
	Sister Morris felt to bear her testimony,
was thankful that she had heard and obeyed
the Gospel and testified to the truth of the
same. Made many excellent remarks.
	Counselor E. Stevenson spoke at some
length concerning our boys and girls, the
children of the Covenant, felt that they
would yet perform a mighty work and bear
off the kingdom. Advised the sisters to
properly instruct their children, for many
mighty ones are being raised up, who are
valiant for God and his Kingdom.
	Sisters sang “Mid scenes of confusion”
&c. A sister in the congregation bore her
testimony and desired to be faithful. Sisters
Corbett and Ford each bore testimonies true
and faithful and desired to be numbered
with those whom God would honor when
he made up his jewels. Sister Julia Druce
and Sister Workman also bore testimony
and desired to be faithful. Counselor Webb
said to the sisters "go in and work in the
Temple if you have only one name, the
way will open and more will be made
known to you, gave other instructions.”
The meeting was adjourned for two

[Column 3]
weeks. After singing “Satan’s
empire long has flourished” &c. the bene-
diction was pronounced by Sister Julia
Pack
LYDIA D. ALDER, Act. Sec.


WOMAN SUFFRAGE COLUMN.

HON. C. W. PENROSE.

[Items from his address at the Convention
of the Utah W. S A. held in Salt Lake
City Oct. 5th, 1893.]
	HON. C. W. PENROSE address at W. S.
Convention. The speaker dwelt upon
necessity of organization, upon faith in the 
cause and confidence that it would succeed.
He urged that the work of converting the 
indifferent and skeptical, and inspiring the
lukewarm should go on, and said there is
nothing new to offer in the way of argu-
ment, but advocates of the cause should
study the objections of those opposed to
the franchise and be able to answer them.
There are no arguments against Woman
Suffrage, only objections which can be 
answered. In conclusion he advised the
ladies to refrain from any agitation of the 
suffrage clause in the bill for statehood, as
it might only retard the progress of the
bill without accomplishing their purpose.


UTAH W. S. A. CONVENTION.

	The Woman’s Suffrage association met 
in convention at Provo, Mrs. Electa Bul-
lock presiding. Nov 23, 1893.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells, recently elected
president, and Mrs. F. S. Richards, first
vice-president of the Territory, were
present, and also quite a number of local
presidents. The object of the meeting was
to nominate county officers and to transact
other county business.
	The results of the nominations were
placed before the house, and the following
are the officers for the new year:
	Electa Bullock president; Mary John
first vice-president; Marilla Daniels second
vice-president: Rebecca Doolan secretary
and correspondent secretary; Mattie Beesley
assistant secretary; Alice M. Wilkins
treasurer; L. L. Dalton chairman executive
committee.
	The chairman introduced Mrs. E. B.
Wells, recently elected Territorial Presi-
dent, who spoke of the retiring president,
Mrs. Kimball, as having been made honarary
president of Utah Territory during her life
time. She then branched out on the great
value a vote possessed in certain places,
and the injustice that no woman regardless
of her business and education, may not
even cast a vote to protect her property.
Onr sanitary condition would be better
when women could have a voice. The
work of police matrons, and the work that
might be done for those who go astray was
referred to. If women study up these
questions they might show our men that
something might be done in this regard, a
great deal better than it is done now.
	“This is suffrage work” said the speaker,
and when we get our suffrage we will but
progress along these lines. We voted as
intelligently as a whole when we had the
franchise as men do. I heard of a
legislator recently, who; when it was sug-
gested to him that there were some laws to
be amended, said with astonishment ‘I
thought we were here to make laws, I did
not know there were any before.’ The


Relief Society has given us so much
practice in organized work, that we are
much better prepared than the women of
other places."
	She referred to Colorado’s recent ad-
vancement; and how soon the women of
that state would stand by the side of
Wyoming in another presidential campaign.
The trend of all women’s work at the
World’s Fair was toward the ballot. The
taking up of some regular work, such as
civil government, and other proper sub-
jects, calling on men for assistance, and
giving them the privilege of membership
was recommended. Also that we draw in
and work harmoniously with all classes of
suffragists.
	Mrs. F. S. Richards, first Territorial vice-
president, made a few appropriate remarks,
following in the lines opened by Mrs. Wells.
She spoke of the many handsome compli-
ments she had received at the World’s
Fair in behalf of the women of Utah, and
the noble work they had done.
	Mrs. Lapish made a few very favorable
remarks.
	The president closed with a broad sum-
ming up and promises for the future.
Reporter E. J.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
DAVIS CO. W. S. A. CONVENTION.

	THE Convention met at 2 p. m. in the
meeting house at Farmington, President
Lucy A. Clark in the chair. Oct. 27, 1893.
	Singing "Wyoming" from the Suffrage
Song book. "Proud State of the Union,
we love thee, A symbol of Justice thy
name; The folds of our flag wave above
thee, The star of thy greatness to claim."
etc.
	Prayer by Susan Grant. Singing, "Our
country now from thee, We claim our
liberty in freedom's name."
	The minutes of the last annual conven-
tion were read and approved also the
financial report.
	Dora Wessels, Helen Perkins, and Mary
Millard were appointed a committee on
credentials and reported the following named
ladies entitled to seats in the convention:
	Kaysville, Minnie Williams, Ellen Bar-
ton, Sarah B. Layton, Jane Blood, R.
Mansel and Dora Wessels; Farmington,
Elizabeth Coombs, Helen M. Miller, Clara
Stayner, Aurelia S. Rogers and Mary Mil-
lard: Centreville, Margaret Randall; East
Bountiful, Jane Holt; West Bountiful,
Martha Fackrell and Helen Perkins; South
Bountiful, Mary Ann Page, Eliza Nelson,
Maria Hatch, Sarah Howard, Emma Moss,
Phoebe Hales and Rhoda Atkinson. 
	Speeches were made by President Min-
nie Williams, of Kaysville, President
Elizabeth Coombs, of Farmington, Vice-
President Margaret Randall, of Centreville,
President Martha Fackrell, of West Bounti-
ful, Jane Holt, East, and Mary Ann Page,
of South Bountiful.
	Those who had held meetings in their
precincts reported that they had made some
progress in the study of civil government,
history, politics, etc.
	The following officers were elected:
President, Lucy A. Clark; Vice Presidents,
Lizzie Smith, Rose Hatch and Dora Wes-
sels. Secretary Leone Rogers, correspond-
ing Secretary, Hattie Knowlton; Treasurer,
Mary Millard. Auditors, M. E. Knowlton
and Julia Hess. Chairman of executive
committee, Athalia Steed, members of com-
mittee, Minnie Williams, Elizabeth Coombs,

[Column 2]
Margaret Randall, Alice Neville, Maria
Hatch and Ellen Perkins.
	Honorary Vice Presidents, Susan Grant,
Jane Holt, Mary S. Clark, Sarah B.  Lay-
ton, Martha Fackrell and Aurelia S.
Rogers. 
	President L. A. Clark said she was proud
of the record of Woman Suffrage in Utah;
they hold the second place in the nation,
New York being the only state that has an
association with a greater membership,
spoke of the progress of women of the
present day.
	While in Chicago attending the con-
gress of representative women, the grandest
gathering of women ever recorded in the
history of the world, was present at a ses-
sion when fourteen national associations
were represented and two of the fourteen
had head quarters in Utah.
	The National R. S. and the National Y.
L. M. I. Associations. And by comparing
statistics and reports, showed that there
were more organized women in Utah than
in any of the other states or territories.
Truly the advancement of woman will ad-
vance human happiness and prosperity.
Every step she has gained has been ac-
complished not by force or compulsion, for
she works her revolution, with the weapons
of peace and love and with such weapons
of defense her advance may be slow but
must eventually be sure.
	May the flames of equality, that have
been kindled in your bosoms, continue to 
burn steadily and true until all the icebergs
of prejudice with which you are surrounded
shall have melted away, and, "Hasten the
day when freedom's proud banner, pro-
tecting no tyrant shall shadow no slave."
	Song: "The song that reached my
heart," was sweetly rendered by Minnie
Williams, of Kaysville. Essay "Woman
versus Man," by Aurelia S. Rogers. Or-
gan solo by Ella Barton, of Kaysville.
	After singing and benediction the con-
vention adjourned.


NATIONAL-AMERICAN CON-
VENTION.

	THE 26th, Annual Convention of the
National-American Woman Suffrage As-
sociation will be held in Washington, D.
C., at Metzerott’s Music Hall, Feb. 15-20
inclusive, 1894.
	The Government’s recognition of women
in the Board of Managers for the World’s
Columbian Exposition, the World’s Con-
gress of Representative Women—the
greatest convocation of women ever as-
sembled—the participation of women in the
entire series of World’s Congresses, the
gaining of woman suffrage in Colorado,
give to our demand for political equality for
women unprecedented prestige in the world
of thought.
	From this vantage ground let us assemble
in our 26th annual Convention to work for
the realization of an ideal Republic, with
the assurance of success in the near future.
	With the question of woman suffrage
pending in Kansas and New York, it is our
manifest duty to concentrate our efforts up-
on those two States, and one business ses-
sion of the Convention will be devoted to
the consideration of ways and means to
further this object.
	Besides the presidents of the auxiliary
State Associations, the speakers invited are:
Mme. Fanny Zampini Salazar, of Italy, and
Mme. Hanna Korany, of Syria, who


were delegates to the World’s Congress of
Representative Women; Mrs. Carrie Lane
Chapman, who was a most important factor
in the Colorado victory; Rev. Marion
Murdock, Rev. Ida C. Hultin, Rev. Anna
H. Shaw, Mrs. Elizabeth Boynton Harbert
Mrs. Julia ward Howe,  Miss Mary H.
Krout, Mrs. Mary Lowe Dickinson,
secretary of the King’s Daughters, Mr. H.
B. Blackwell, and others. 
	SUSAN B. ANTHONY, President.
	RACHEL FOSTER AVERY, Cor. Sec.


IN WOMAN’S REALM.

	Every lady is thinking about the coming
holidays. What would be the nicest present to
offer? Before deciding remember that health is
to be considered, before everything else. How
can you enjoy a Christmas Dinner or a new dress
if you are not well and happy? DR. DE JOZA's
famous prescription is a dowry to every lady suf-
fering with painful and irregular menstruation.
In order to give those who have not already
tried it an opportunity of doing so, we will send
one trial treatment only to each person for $2.00.
Regular price, $3.00. We send a written guaran-
tee to cure any case, no matter of how long
standing, in two treatments or refund money.
This is the genuine prescription of the famous
Dr. DE JOZA; hundreds of testimonials at your
service. This world renowned treatment was
discovered in 1849, and has been adopted in all
principal hospitals of France All orders and
inquiries must be sent to the “LADY MANAGER,
SALT LAKE MEDICINE CO., Salt Lake City,
Utah.” Write for particulars and remember
that we are responsible for our guarantee. Lady
manager wanted in every city and town.


ANOTHER RELIEF SOCIETY.

	MINUTES of the organization of the
Relief Society of South Taylorsville held in
the School house Oct. 31st, 1893.
	Bishop Heber Bennion presiding. Meet-
ing opened by singing, “Praise to the man.”
Prayer by Bishop Heber Bennion. Singing
“Now let us rejoice.”
	Bishop Bennion then spoke upon the
necessity of organizing a Society in this
part of the ward, and upon the duties and
strength of the Relief Society, in the
several wards.
	Sister Pixton made the motion that Sis-
ter Mary Parker be appointed president;
and a unanimous vote was taken.
	Sister Parker was then set apart by
Bishop Heber Bennion.
	Sister Jane Bringhurst was selected as
first counselor and set apart to that position
by counselor William Panter.
	Sister Mary Palmer was chosen second
counselor and set apart by Bishop Heber
Bennion.
	Sister Sarah E. Bringhurst was selected
as secretary and set apart by Bishop Ben-
nion.
	Sister Alice Newbold was chosen treasur-
er and set apart by counselor William Pant-
er.
	There were present, Bishop Heber Ben-
nion and counselors, Sisters Julia C. Howe
and Lydia D. Alder, of Salt Lake City,
Sister Elizabeth Pixton, Sister Mary Ben-
nion, Sister Webster, all of whom spoke
very instructively to the Sisters.
	Services closed by singing “The Spirit
of God,” benediction by Elder William
Parker.
ALICE J. HARKER, Sec.


PRIMARY ANNUAL.
	The 14th, Ward Primary Association
held its annual meeting Sunday evening
Dec. 3rd 1893, under the direction of the


President Ella W. Hyde; present were the
Bishopric of the ward, Counselors Clara C.
Cannon and Camilla Cobb of the Territorial
and stake organization, parents of the
children and others. Meeting opened by 
singing “Gather them into the fold.”
Prayer by Master Fara Fox, singing
“Trust the children.”
	A very interesting program was carried
out as follows. Bessie Crismon and Ray
Workman gave a recitation entitled “Two
little welcomes.’’ Fera Fox gave a lecture
on “Prayer.” Sentiments, called Resolu-
tions were repeated in concert by the
children, conducted by Joan Campbell.
Freddie Wilcox gave a recitation entitled,
“Catching the colt.” A motion song was
given by Frankie and Georgie Young.
The articles of Faith were repeated by
Alice Cannon, Hattie Folsom and
Frankie Young. Little Roy Taylor gave a
recitation entitled “Three little cats.”
	An interesting sketch of the life of Eliza
R. Snow was read by Lyle Young, followed
with a song by Ruby Workman.
Questions and answers on the “Word of
Wisdom” was next given, questions asked
by R. M. Fox, answered by eight girls.
Vida and Lester Fox gave an original
dialogue composed by R. M. Fox. A duet
was nicely rendered by Daisy Fox and
Minnie Goss the children joining in the
chorus. Ivy Crismon gave a rectation and
Master Bertie Clive a Violin Solo. After
which interesting and encouraging re-
marks were made by Counselors, Camilla
Cobb, Clara C. Cannon and Bishop George
H. Taylor. Singing “Dare to do right.”
Benediction by Master Joseph Goss.
	On the following Friday afternoon Dec.
8th, a picnic was given to the children of
the Primary, in the basement of the 14th,
Ward Hall, when between sixty and
seventy children were present. After the
opening exercises, refreshments were served
and games engaged in by the little folks un-
til a late hour when they were dismissed,
and returned to their homes with happy
beaming faces, the officers feeling well re-
paid for their labors.
	E. W. H.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WAYNE STAKE.
	Relief Society Conference of Wayne
Stake, convened Aug. 28th, at 2 p. m.
President Jane S. Coleman presiding, the
choir sang “We thank thee O, God for a
prophet,” prayer offered by Elder Joseph
Eccersley. Singing “O God we raise to
thee.” This being our first Conference
there were no minutes to read. The
President said she was pleased to meet with
the sisters of the Relief Society and also
the Brethren, spoke upon the duties of the
sisters, hoped we would be able to ac-
complish much good, appreciated our Stake
organization, as a great blessing, also
spoke on faith and good works.  Verbal
Reports were given, President Louisa Tay-
lor, Fremont, Margaret Taylor, Loa, Helen
Maxfield, East Loa, Coun. Mary Bullard,
Thurber, Prest. Mary Ann Lords, Blue
Valley, all reported their branches in good
condition and the spirit of union prevail-
ing.
	Counselor Mary E. Hanks, reported for
Cainsville and Aldridge, spoke favorable,
made remarks upon the great blessings we
enjoy, spoke of the good instructions given,
hoped we would practice them in our


lives and increase in faith and the gifts of
the Gospel.
	Counselor Sarah S. Forsyth, of Loa,
said it was a pleasure to meet with the
Relief Society, was gratified to see so
many brethren present, gave much good
counsel to parents, spoke also upon sus-
taining the WOMAN'S EXPONENT by sub-
scribing for it, spoke upon other subjects
pertaining to our holy religion practical and
good.
	Counselor G. Bastian of the stake Presi-
dency, spoke in loving terms of the sisters
of the Relief Society, their good works
their charity in an appieciative way gener-
ally. 
	Our esteemed President Willis E.
Robinson, made encouraging remarks on
the labors of the sisters had been edified
with the instructions given, prayed the
blessing of God to rest upon the Relief
Society in all its branches.
	Singing “O say what is Truth.” Bene- 
diction by Elder U. V. Stewart. Con-
ference adjourned for three months.
Florence M. Williams, Stake Secy.


MISS MARGATET IRWIN, one of the As-
sistant Labor Commissioners of England,
reports that in the tailors’ workshop of the
Co-operative Society of Glasgow, the wo-
men were lately taking work at less wages
than men, in work usually done by men.
The men struck because their demand
that the women should be dismissed was
not granted. The Tailors’ Union made
peace by arranging that the women should
be paid the same wages as men. This is a
good way of settling troubles.
	IMMEDIATELY after suffrage was given
to the women of Colorado, the sixty-eight
leagues of the Equal Suffrage Association
resolved themselves into leagues for polit-
ical study. The book selected is John
Fiske’s Civil Government.—Ex.


IN MEMORIAM.

Editor Exponent:
	IT is with regret we have to announce the
death of one of our number. So sudden and
unexpectedly Sister Sarah P. Crosby died at
her home in Panguitch Garfield Co. Utah on
Nov. 13th 1893 of pneumonia after a severe ill-
ness of three days.
	Deceased was born in Iowa Sep. 20th, 1848; was
the daughter of Lorenzo and Beulah Clark and
wife of Jesse W. Crosby Jr. was the mother of
ten children, two of whom passed away in infancy.
Her husband, five children with many relatives
and friends surrounded her death bed powerless
to render any relief. Three absent sons arrived
soon after.
	Sister Sarah has been an active worker in the
Relief Society, holding the office of Counselor to
our Stake President, Sister Barbara Myers, since
its organization on June 5th, 1878, has also acted
as Stake Secretary until our last Conference, Aug.
31st, when she was released from that position by
her own request, feeling it too much responsibility.
Although very feeble in health the past year her
energies seemed doubly increased in her public
labors. In nll our associations she will be greatly
missed whether public or private; she was a 
regular attendant at all meetings, and we know
all is well with her.
	Rest in peace dear sister,
		Your work is nobly done,
	Life's battle has been fought
		Eternal peace well wom.
	Although we miss you sadly,
		Since your parting gave us pain,
	We are reconciled in knowing
		Our loss is Heaven's gain.
BARBARA MYERS, Prest.
H. A. CROSBY, Cor. Sec.


MEN like women to reflect them, no
doubt; but the woman who can only re-
flect a man, and is nothing in herself, will
never be of much service to him. The
woman who cannot stand alone is not likely
to make either a good wife or mother.—
George Macdonald.

				SARAH M. POMEROY.
