SPRINGTIME.

BRIGHT Spring, if I should ask you,
To hasten in your flight,
To bring no fair sweet blossoms
To gladden mortal sight,
No cool and sparkling dewdrips,
That kiss the brow of night;
To steal the gentle breezes,
That fans the morning light.
To stay the rushing waters,
In their ever onward flight.
The hilltops ne'er adorning,
With aught but pearly white;
To hear no feathered songsters,
Arrayed in plumage bright,
Trill forth the sweetest music,
From out their leafy height.
Witholding welcome showers,
From lovely Nature's sight,
Nor clothe her vales with verdure,
Whose beauties all invite.

Fair Spring, if this I ask you,
Would I an answer find?

Ah, Springtime, gentle Springtime,
Have not a doubt for me;
I would not have you other,
E'en though a change could be.
I love your fragrant flowers,
And graceful waving trees,
Your verdant vales and valleys,
And smoothly covered leas;
The rugged clifts and ledges,
That round me I can see,
Grim sentinels of safety,
They ever seem to be.
The winding rills and rivers,
That flow to meet the sea,
With rippling sounds of laughter,
And roaring shouts of glee.
All these are dearest pleasures,
Whose freshness ne'er can flee,
From the depths of true affection,
Safe locked with memory's key.

No, no, happy Springtime, linger,
Forevermore with me.
HYACINTH.
May 1, 1893.


THE soul that does good to others grows
in goodness. He that is a medium of
blessing to others is himself blessed thereby.
Hence, selfishness is a folly, as well as sin;
for while it prevents our doing good to
others, in the same degree it prevents our
doing good to ourselves.—Jean Paul.


[Column 1]
WOMAN'S SPHERE.

	AMONG the unsolved problems of the
day, remains that of the limits of woman’s
sphere.
	Ages past have endeavored to determine
the bounds that should be prescribed for
her, as being suited to her capacity, of
both mental and physical development.
It has been a cherished idea with man that
there were limits beyond which she must
not venture, beyond which her aspirations
must not attempt to soar. But she has so
persistently not only advocated, but de-
manded of her lord, the man, possession of
rights so consistent with her relations to
him, that his own moral sensibility has in-
duced him to make concession from time to
time, until now some dare to say there is
no limit to her sphere within the circle of
man’s ultimate destiny;—that her intel-
ligence, her moral obligations, her suscepti-
bilites and sensibilities;—her place and
power, and her appreciation, all render her
competent to be a complete companion of
man, through all the conditions and
vicissitudes of time; a sharer of all his
honor and glory beyond the limits of this
probationary experience.
	It seems to be justly claimed that her
importance has not been appreciated by man
as was designed in the beginning it should
be, when it was immutably decreed that a
man should leave both his father and
mother, and cleave to woman as an indis-
pensable companion to his future welfare,
that when woman is to take precedence of
both father and mother in the eternal
economy, she may not be lightly esteemed,
must not be looked upon with indifference,
not be set aside in any condition as some-
thing that can be dispensed with. Jesus
himself, recognized this law of the Creator,
and gave it complete endorsment as bind-
ing upon man, when he quoted it in de-
fence of his views, that from the beginning
there was no such thing as a separation
contemplated, after the relation of man and
wife had been consummated.
	Certainly it is by and through her that all
men are, ever were and ever shall be; and
must she not share in the accountability
which man shall render of his life? Where
then is the time or place in which she is
not entitled to share with him in all the
responsibilities and honors that man is
capable of bearing, or legitimately acquir-
ing?
	To govern is made her duty from the day
of man’s birth. She is both fitted and
competent for the honorable performance of
that duty. To learn his needs, and provide
for and administer to them she is equally
proficient; and in respect for, and in the
performance of duty she is ever the peer of
man. For integrity and honor she may
be fearlessly trusted, as the prevailing rule
of her life.
	It has not been so much by the encourage-
ment that man has given, as by her own
persistent efforts, that woman has risen
in the higher civilization of Christian nations.
It is certainly a great advance of the time
when she was subjected to a seclusion

[Column 2]
amounting almost to imprisonment, or to a
servitude equal to slavery; when she was
not considered as the ornament  and refiner
of society, the companion and friend of
man; but among the higher castes of society
was often required to sacrifice herself upon 
his tomb.
	The influence of Christianity has changed
to a great extent these conditions of her 
life; have released her from the actions
and degrading restrictions in which she had
almost become the soulless thing which 
she had been represented to be, and restored
to her many of her natural rights which
tend to establish a just and true equality of
the sexes; and a woman now is often
acknowledged the absolute sovereign of
a great kingdom as in hereditary monarchies
like those of modern times. In our day
she has not only acquired excellence but
prominence in many of the professions, as
well as in science and art, which places her
in the front, and makes good her claim to
the right of equality with many of the
sterner sex.
	It has now become a universal sentiment,
that nothing marks more decidedly the
character of man, and of nations, than the
manner in which they treat woman. In
fact, woman’s influence in society for good, 
cannot be overestimated. She is no longer
considered to be a mere plaything for
treacherous man. She is of that im-
portance that no man can be properly
educated, or fitted for life, who has not
experienced the influence and spiritual
divinity as embodied in woman and mother.
	An inspired writer has said of woman
that her price is far above rubies, and that
by getting one for a wife, man finds favor
with God. Who can estimate the value of
God’s favor? Or who can declare the worth
of a virtuous woman? She is in fact the
link that connects the temporal with the
spiritual, earth to the heavens, and man to
God. Well did the poet write:
		‘‘Heaven when it had created man,
		Unfinished found creation’s plan
		Nor would the links together meet 
		’Till woman made the chain complete.”
	The Father chose her by and through
whom all his spiritual children should come
to earth, and so ordered that she should be
the first to know the advent of the spirit to
its home, “a house not made with hands,”
and not until she has endowed it with the
virtues of her soul does she present it a
priceless offering to her Lord. Thus wo-
man is recognized by the eternal decree of
the Father to be the first to cherish
humanity; as also, she was the first to be-
hold immortality in a risen Son and Savior.
	The fact that no man is without the wo-
man in the Creator’s economy, makes it
difficult to find a place between  the advent
of humanity, and the immortality of the
risen dead, where woman’s sphere may not
be found, in which she may not tread with
that triumphant womanly pride that shall
fit her not only for the associations of men,
but of angels and of God.
	In view of the fact that there can be no
man, priest, king or God, without woman,


we may cease to talk about woman's
sphere as though it had a limit, other than
the world in which she moves.
	The author of the following lines, may
have exceeded even our own appreciation
of women:
	“There’s not a place in earth or heaven,
	There’s not a task to mankind given,
	There’s not a blessing or a word,
	There’s not a whisper, yes or no,
	There’s not a life, a death, a birth,
	That has a feather’s weight of worth,
	Without a woman it it.”
		S. W. RICHARDS.


WOMANS EXPONENT


[Column 1]
LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	MINUTES of the semi-monthly meeting
held in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Hall,
March 10th, 1894, Counselor Elizabeth
Webb presiding. Meeting opened by
singing, “Arise, O Glorious Zion.” Prayer
by Sister Frances Smith. Continued by
singing, “O, Say What is Truth.”
Minutes of previous meeting read and ap-
proved.
	Counselor Webb: Let us think of the
blessings we enjoy here in Zion. Here we
have our glorious Temple which has been
dedicated to the living God. Spoke of
“Christian Science,” the many they were
drawing to them. We know that their
foundation is a sandy one, also that we are
built upon the rock, let our lives and good
works bear testimony of this truth. Satan
will pass off twenty truths perhaps to one
lie, yet that one will destroy all the truth
he has told. Think of the words of the
Savior, he said, love one another. Let us
build up the kingdom of God.
	Sister Mary Silver spoke of the General
Relief Society who had given employment
to poor women, making comforters and
rugs, thought they had done much good;
some of the comforters were for sale, stated
their quality and price, and called on the
sisters to purchase some if they could. Ad-
ded that she was thankful that she was a
Latter-day Saint. Thought we should be
careful of our words and our influence, for
we will be held responsible for the same.
	Sister Julia C. Howe said, sickness was
often sanctified to us, knew how it had been
when we have been healed by the laying on
of hands God expects us to arise and
testify to it. He likes us to tell of his
goodness. God bless you all my sisters.
Made other good remarks.
	Sister Morris told the sisters to bear their
trials and not let their children know of
them, keep them as much as possible to
themselves. Sister Paul bore an excellent
testimony to the truth of the Gospel, spoke
of those in our midst who had been healed by
other means than the laying on of hands.
She had seen some healed, and some die,
although the same means were used.
Sisters be not deceived, let us cling to the
truth. I know we are built on the rock, and
I have seen the signs follow as promised.
Sisters Forsythe and Reid each bore
testimonies to the truth of the Gospel and
earnestly desired to be faithfnl. Spoke of
the Relief Society doing good to the poor.
Sister Aird bore testimony, and said “The
Kingdom of God or nothing for me.”
	Sister S. E. Brown and M. Brown each
spoke well, and encouraged the sisters. A
sister in the congregation bore an excellent
testimony. Sister Irvine thought all should
worship God as they desired, but warned
the sisters to look well to themselves, and
serve the true and living God. Thought

[Column 2]
the most of those who turned away from
the Church had never possessed much
faith, the seed had fallen in stony places,
and when other influences were brought to
bear, it withered up because it had not
much root.
	Sister L. D. Alder spoke of the power of
the adversary, how often he deceived those
who were not on the watch tower. Let us
study our own religion, and live near to
God. Let us shun the paths where many
have backslidden and pray for God’s help
to be true and faithful. Counselor Webb
made a few closing remarks. Sister Alder
read a note from President M. I. Horne
stating she was not able to meet with us
today and announcing the Quarterly Con-
ference.

	MINUTES of the semi-monthly meeting
held in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Hall,
April 21st, 1894.
	Meeting opened by singing, “Jesus the
Giver.” Prayer by Sister Julia Pack.
Continued by singing, “Now let us re-
joice in the day of salvation.”
	President M. I. Horne presiding
Minutes of the First Ward Primary were
read by the secretary.
	President Horne said there were some
sisters present who have been healed by
the laying on of hands in faith, and she
wished to hear from them, encouraged the
sisters to bear testimony, for the Savior had
promised to testify before his Father of
those who bore testimony of him.
	Sister B. W. Smith spoke of her labors
in the Temple, and bore testimony of many
wonderful healings through prayer and
faith of the brethren and sisters in the
Temple. “This is a world of trial and
trouble to prepare us for a better existence.
The Prophet Joseph said it would take a
thousand years to perform the work for the
dead, so let us press on, doing good and no
hurt. I pray that we may be prepared to
meet those who have gone on.”
	Sister Mary Ann Hyde, President of the
Sanpete Stake of the Relief Society said,
“I make it a point never to refuse anything
I am called upon to do. I have a diffidence
in speaking. We do not live as near the
Lord as we should, so that we may enjoy
the gifts. I have been in the church about
fifty years; and I was taught the principle
of plural marriage by the Prophet Joseph
himself, so I know it is true, and I have
lived in that order nearly as many years as
I have been in the Church. I honor it, and
feel that great blessings are in store for
those honorable ones who have lived in it,”
spoke also of her hip being injured years
ago. After coming to the hospital, the
many doctors pronounced her case hopeless,
that she could never walk again. Said she
went on crutches for a long time, but
through prayer and laying on of hands,
and the power of the priesthood, she could
walk as well now as ever she could.
	Counselor E. Stevenson stated that she
had been at Grantsville, so that she had not
attended the last meeting, “the brethren and
sisters there were so anxious to attend the
meetings, coming sometimes twelve miles
in a wagon, yet, look at the empty benches
here today. It is true the nearer the church
the farther from God?”
	Sister Delamare spoke on the fulfillment
of prophecy, as seen in gathering hosts at
Washington, and bore excellent testimony.
Sisters Baubrick, Morris and Frances Smith
each bore instructive testimonies, and
Louie Stevenson and Sister Bregher.

[Column 3]
	President M. I. Horne said “I have 
enjoyed the testimonies of the sisters. If
we have had lessons to learn, let us culti-
vate our gifts and learn them. God desires
us to do this and then we will know for
ourselves. We should have revelation for
our own guidance. The Lord will heal if
we seek for faith. It is a day to seek unto
God. Look at the promised signs, showing
great events at no distant day. But the
Lord will be sought after and then
He will bless us, and save us from the great
calamities that have commenced in the
world. You come here for spiritual food
sisters, and I pray that we may all be faith-
ful and true. Sister Lizzie Shipp announced
a lecture to ladies for the following week,
and a class to commence the first Monday
in May on obstetrics.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks. Sang,
“Satan’s empire long has flourished.”
Benediction.
				LYDIA D. ALDER, Act. Sec.


NATURE.

	WHAT is nature? It was the decree of 
God that went forth when worlds were
made. It is shown in all His works.
How grand, how beautiful! The heavens,
the earth and the sea declare His name.
The sun, that glorious orb of day, the
source of light, heat and life. The moon,
the queen of night, and the countless
numbers of stars that light up the firma-
ment. The air we breathe. The seasons
as they roll around, bringing seed time and
harvest. Old ocean that years change not,
lakes, rivers and forests. The mountains
whose lofty peaks tower toward the sky.
Nature, what a field for thought and
adoration! Her book is open to all. The
animal, mineral and vegetable kingdoms,
with man at the head. Man, the Creator’s
greatest work. Placed here on earth for a
purpose. Will the time ever come when
mankind will realize as they ought, why
they are here, and what they are here for?
How much sorrow and suffering might be
avoided if they but thought of these things
in early youth. The laws of God would
be regarded in all things, spiritual and
temporal, and they themselves receive the
blessings. The mineral kingdom—what a
source of wealth to man? Its supplies are
inexhaustible. The vegetable kingdom,
without which man could not subsist.
What beauty! What perfection and order.
There are seldom any freaks in nature and
in her wise economy nothing is lost, al-
though on one hand constant wear. Yet on
the other continual building up. Should not
men’s hearts flow with gratitude to the
giver of all good whose wisdom passeth all
understanding. Could such a world have
been made by chance? No, man alone has
said, “There is no God.” The wonders of
nature are to be seen all around, above,
beneath. The unbeliever is to be pitied.
His mind, that God-like gift of intellect,
must be dark indeed, cheerless and hope-
less. Religion, the love of God is man’s
only solace.
	“Creator yes! Thy wisdom and thy word
	Created me! Thou source of life and good!
	Thou spirit of my spirit, and my Lord:
	Thy light! Thy love, in their bright plentitude
	Filled me with an immortal soul, to spring
	Over the abyss of death; and bade it wear
	The garments of eternal day, and wing 
	Its heavenly flight beyond this little sphere,
	Even to its source, to thee, its Author there."
								E. H. LYON


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.   131


BIRTHDAY GREETINGS.

(TO MY ESTEEMED FRIEND, ANNA B. J.)
YOUR birthday, dear friend! I wish you joy,
And though your life’s cup is mixed with alloy,
Think of the gladness your dear presence lends
To parents, to husband,dear ones and friends.

Dost remember the palace where primeval you 
	strayed,
The gardens of grandeur where soft breezes
	played? 
Or Father or Mother in whose courts you were
	reared, 
With love past revealing—and nothing was 
	feared? 
Where visions of beauty were old to the sight—
Where the glory of God so wondrous—so bright,
Encircled His children as in arms of love,
And rapture supreme reigned below and above.

Did the green sward of earth entice thee to
	come,
To leave joys supreme in that distant home?
Or was it the mother love drawing you here
Away from the life in yon glorious sphere?

Dear friend a mission was given to thee,
’Twas said, “Make a home and you shall be free”
But choose to do well, we will help you in love,
Will whisper of Heaven wherever you rove.”

Then when the steep pathway your tired feet
	tread,
And pitiless storms beat fierce o’er your head,
O, think of the love that is waiting for you,
When Heaven is gained and you have been true.

Then cheered be thy heart! And joyfully
	borrow,
The sunshine of hope, for lo, on the morrow
The clouds will all pass, and Heber will come,
With songs and rejoicing to you and his home.
						 LYDIA D. ALDER. 
January 26, 1894.


[Column 1]
THE OLD TEMPLE WALL.
[Salt Lake Temple, Nov. 7th, 1894.]
THOU old Temple wall 
	Which circles around,
What is dear to all Saints,
	That blest spot of ground;
On which stands our Temple
	So lofty and grand—
How thought crowds upon thought
	As gazing I stand.

As my thoughts backwards turn
	And seek memory’s page,
I see there inscribed
	How the wicked once raged
When they sought the possession
	Of our sacred halls,
Which thou hast guarded for years,
	Dear old Temple Wall!

How thrilling the knowledge!
	The remembrance, how sweet!
When calling on “Father,”
	We knelt at his feet,
And cried, Father, forgive them!
	Forgive them, we pray!
But spare our loved Temple
	From those who would slay
Thine anointed and chosen,
	Thy Prophets and Seers,
The prayers of thy people
	Oh God! Wilt thou hear!

How blessed! To know
	That our knowledge extends
To a God who doth hear,
	And raise up to us friends
And lo! Like the clouds 
From a clear summer sky,
	Roll back all our troubles,

[Column 2]
And we know He is nigh.
	Then, “Dear Wall” many times
Have I walked in thy shade,
	When the joy in my heart 
It’s own music hath made.
	Went in at thy gates,
And sat down in thy halls,
	And listened enraptured
To truth, as it falls
	From the lips of God’s servants,
How soothing and calm
	Is the peace that it brings
To the heart, what a balm!
	When troubles hang o’er us, 
Like a funeral pall,
	Oh!  How can we part with thee,
Dear old Temple Wall?

Above thy foundation
	The moss doth appear,
In patches, in crevice,
	Now there and now here.
Already the house in the corner is razed.
	Naught, soon will be left
On which we have gazed,
	In years that have gone,
With loved ones who sleep,
	Ah!  Tread gently, I dare not touch
That sacred spot lest I weep!

Soon thy mission is ended,
	And the time draweth nigh
When an enclosure more elegant,
	More chaste, (to the eye,)
Will surround our loved Temple.
	(Perhaps more pleasing to all)
But not dearer, to us, than
	Our, Old Temple Wall!
The rich and the poor, alike
	Enter thy gates,
Of the blessings within
	They alike may partake.
At the altars may kneel,
	When on “Father” we call,
Through thee, the Temple we enter,
	Dear old Temple Wall!

Must we part with thee?  Thou steadfast,
	Thou old and tried friend! 
Could’st thou not have stood
	And endured to the end?
Ah yes! But improvement
	Demands thy downfall;
But thou’lt live in our memories,
	Dear old Temple Wall!

						C. C. R.W.


OUR AGENCY, WHEN DOES, IT BEGIN.

	IT is true that an all wise Heavenly
Father has given us our agency? If so
when did we receive it? We came here
with faculties all dormant, not knowing
where we are or what to do, only to cry for
food that we may gain strength and intel-
ligence adequate to this occasion. We are
helpless, yes, helpless in every sense of the
word. Yet we find this food gives strength,
and we, after a time, discover that we have
faculties we can call into action; can grasp
and hold with the hands, and shortly use
our feet to walk.
	How delighted the child is when he can
accomplish this, and with what pleasure he
wlll do anything his parents or guardian 
will tell him to do.
	Yes he appreciates the faculties given
him, and the love of the parents for him
creates a love in the child, also and it is his
delight to do everything in his power to
please those who love him so tenderly.
Is not this typical of ourselves and what


we are or should be in the sight of God
our Father. 
	Yet the opposite spirit, Lucifer, tells us
you need not pray, Our Father knows what
you need before you ask Him. This is an
easy lesson, but is it a profitable one?
	Our Elder Brother’s lesson is quite the
opposite to this. He says, “seek and ye
shall find, knock and it shall be opened un-
to you.” In this way our faculties are
developed and drawn into action. Our
Father sees that since we are made in His
image, we must develop those faculties to
become God-like and partake of His nature.
While Lucifer would teach us that he has a
shorter and a better way to gain the same
result. Here we need wisdom to know
which lesson to learn first.
	Since we are told to become God-like we
must learn both spirits; that we may give
each one its true value and its proper place. 
	Now you must cling to the parent or
guardian who loves you, and has already
gained the experience you seek, and
remember that our Heavenly Father’s
voice is the first to listen to, to be safe from
evil, for after you have decided which to
follow you will find you have a prompter
in either case, either on the upward or
downward grade, after those faculties are
developed; but before this time his Satanic
majesty has no power, over little children.
This is the decree of God our Father.
	How wise, how merciful and good He is
to his erring children, knowing as he does
that the path through this world is a rough
one, yet it will teach us to seek wisdom
from the fountain of light and intelligence,
to arrive safely into port, to our eternal
home.
	When we read the history of our
Lord and Savior we see how many trials
He met with, yet He gained strength every
time, because of the help he received from
his Father who delights to bless all those
who put their trust in Him. In this way
they make a wise use of their agency, and
will find that every trial will be a benefit
to those whe can acknowledge the hand of
God in all things.
				ZION'S CONVERT.
Dec. 1st, 1892.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	YEARS mellow some men and petrify
others.
	THE earth is our workshop. We may
not curse it; we are bound to sanctify it.
—G. Mazzini.
	HONESTY is the best policy, because it
is the only policy which insures against
loss of character.
	NEVER bear more than one trouble at a
time. Some people bear three kinds—all
they have ever had, all they have now,
and all they expect to have.—Edward
Everett Hale.
	THE effective life and the respective life
are one.  No sweep of arm that does some
work for God, but harvests also some more
of the truth of God, and sweeps it into the
treasury of life.—Phillips Brooks.
	REMEMBER that if the opportunities for
great deeds should never come, the op-
portunity for good deeds is renewed for you
day by day. The thing for us to long for
is the goodness, not the glory.—Frederic
W. Farrahr.

space, one time, $2.50; per month, $3.00. A liberal dis-

ng, Main Street, opposite Z.C. M, I. Business hours from

SALT LAKE CITY,				MAY 15, 1894


[Column 1]
VISIT OF PEN WOMEN.

	WE were delighted to meet and welcome
to our City on Sunday morning, May 13th,
some of the members of the National Wo-
man’s Press Association and their guests,
whose long expected visit here we had
looked forward to with such pleasant antici-
pation; and though their stay was only
very brief, yet it was a satisfaction to see
and converse even for a few hours with these
bright, brave women, who had accomplished
so much in the world of letters, becoming
so widely known from their work as some
of these women writers have, during the
last few years.
	A few of the officers and members of
the Utah Woman’s Press Club met them at
the depot and drove with them to the
Hotel Templeton, in the big Utah and Mrs.
McCune’s handsome carriage, and after-
wards drove over some parts of the City.
After lunch they went through the Bee
Hive, once the residence of President
Brigham Young, thence to the Tabernacle
and from there to Saltair Beach. Mr.
Nephi W. Clayton, the generous and amiable
manager, running a special train out as a
compliment to the press women.
	The visitors were homeward bound, hav-
ing visited Southern California, the Mid-
winter Fair and places of interest, includ-
ing the Yosemite, the Geysers and big trees,
and also attended the Woman’s Congress in
San Francisco, some of the ladies having
read papers and also spoken at the Congress.
Most of the party were from Washington,
D.C., others from Boston, Baltimore and
Chicago, and one very bright little woman
from South Carolina. They traveled in a
special car all the way, the Demarara, hav-
ing started on their trip westward April 14;
from here they went on to Denver and
Manitou, expecting to reach Washington
on the 17th inst.
	Mrs. Mary E. Lockwood, chief of the
party, is the President of the U. W. P. A.
and was one of the Vice Presidents of the
Board of Lady Managers of the Columbian
Exposition, has published some books and
is editor of the “American Monthly
Magazine,” she has a home at the National
Capital; Mrs. H. W. Balston, correspondent
of the Woman’s Tribune, and a poet of some
celebrity, known best by her poem of 
Columbus, written expressly for the World’s
Fair; Mrs. A. Maynard Richardson of
Boston, best known in art work from her
Limages pottery and in the literary world
for her art criticisms, and she did valuable
work at the World’s Fair as one of the jury

[Column 2]
on art. She treats on other subjects also
and is a very clever writer, and brilliant
women; she has a studio in Boston and is
well up in art technique; she hopes to
come west next year to make some
sketches.
	Mrs. Emily Charles Thornton (Emily
Hawthorne) has written poetry for papers
and magazines all her life, has published
some of them in book form, elaborately il-
lustrated; she is a correspondent of Advanced
Thought. Mrs. Julia A. Anderson is cor-
respondent of the Philadelphia Daily News,
has been during the World’s Fair a secret
service writer, but she is now devoting
much of her time and best efforts in writ-
ing a book which will soon be published.
	Mrs. Joanna Turner, an ex-army nurse,
had seen some actual service, and is a cor-
respondent for the Wisconsin State Register.
Mrs. Elvira Bliss Sheldon, correspondent
of Washington Daily Times, and Canton
Clipper, Ill. Mrs. J. L. McCreary, cor-
respondent of Humbolt (Iowa) “Kosmas.”
Judge Clark of Washington, D. C., was
among the gentlemen of the party, also Mr.
Geo W. Grey, whose daughter was also
one of the party; and Mr. Henry Sampson
and wife;—one or two other gentlemen of
the party had hurried homeward without
stopping over.
	Mrs. B. W. Moses, of Washington, in-
terested us very much on account of hav-
ing heard of her through Bishop Whitney,
who was at one time a guest at her house in
Washington in company with her nephew,
Jim McKnight, well known here in his boy-
hood. nephew of Feramorz Little and
grand-nephew of President Brigham
Young. Mrs. Moses is a brilliant talker
has seen much of the world, has traveled
extensively in foreign lands and has some-
thing interesting to tell. She intends com-
ing to Salt Lake again in the near future.
There were other women of note not
regularly engaged in press work, but doing
some special  correspondence for Eastern
papers while away.
We hope and trust the party will have
some pleasant memories of Utah and their
visit here, and certainly those of us who
met and conversed with them, will always
be more interested in their welfare and suc-
cess because of the bond of union and love
formed by the one day’s pleasant associa-
tion and interchange of kindly thought.


UTAH FEDERATION OF CLUBS.

	THE Utah Federation of Woman’s Clubs
held its first Annual Convention in this
city, Thursday May 10th, delegates from
the Ladies’ Literary, Utah Woman’s Press
club, Reapers’ Club, Cleofan and
Woman’s Club, of this city, the Nine-
teenth Century Club of Provo, the Spring-
ville Woman’s Ciub, the La Coterie, the
Magazine and the French Circle Club of
Ogden being present.
	The morning meeting was an executive
session of the general officers and of the
several clubs; the chief business consisted
in the election of officers for the ensuing
year. A nominating committee was ap-
pointed by the President and the vote was
by ballot, which resulted in the election of
Mrs. C. E. Allen, President; Mrs. Patten (of
Ogden,) Vice President; Mrs. Emma
McVicker, Secretary, Miss Georgia Webber,
Treasurer.
	At the afternoon session there was a
large attendance of members from the


several clubs, in the Federation, and the
variety of colors in ribbon denoting the
respective clubs with the artistic floral
decorations, added to the bright intellectual
faces of the women present, made a charm-
ing and delightful picture. Most of the
ladies spoke without notes or memoranda.
Mrs. Jennie Leonard of Springville gave
quite a practical address on the subject, “Is
the limitation of club numbers desirable?”
The next subject was “Home Talent and
Outside Talent.” Mrs. Nellie Little gave a
very able address upon “Parliamentary Law”
and Miss Josephine Kellogg the President
of the Nineteenth Century of Provo made an
exceedingly interesting speech and regular
law argument on “Helps and Hindrances
of Club Life.”
	The representatives of the Ogden Clubs
invited the Federation to meet there next
year and the offer was accepted.
	In the evening there was a session at the
First Congregational Church, the ladies of
the Cleofan had beautifully ornamented the
platform where the officers and speakers
were seated. Mrs. Carlow of Ogden, the
retiring president, made a most efficient
officer; and her charming appearance, and
gracious presence are fitting and appropriate
to the position. An organ solo by Mrs.
Hamilton opened the evening session and
the first speech was made by Mrs. Farlow
on the “History of Woman’s Clubs” and
her manner, voice, style and the impression
upon her audience was telling in the ex-
treme, and she handled her subject in a
masterly manner. Miss Geneva Jennings
sang two airs, has a clear soprano voice,
has been studying recently in Chicago.
The Treble Clef Society composed of
ladies sang the “Water Lily” and
Lullaby” song sweetly, Professor Krouse
accompanist. Mrs Patton gave artificial
society some severe cuts, her subject was
“The Social Phase of Club Life.”
	Mrs. C. E. Allen’s address came last and
she was heartily applauded, her subject
was “Educational Influence of Woman’s
Clubs” she dwelt impressively upon self-
culture, and also the good fellowship pro-
duced by the opportunities for mutual
help afforded by Woman’s Clubs.


A FEW THOUGHTS.

HOW FAR SHOULD HOME TALENT BE CUL-
    TIVATED AND TO WHAT EXTENT SHOULD
	OUTSIDE TALENT BE INTRODUCED
IN WOMAN’S CLUBS.

	[Federation Notes not used.]
	THIS subject was assigned to the writer
to speak upon at the Convention of the
Utah Federation of Clubs held May 10th.
	Some of our ideas may suggest thoughts
and methods to others, as it is an entirely
new subject and one upon which we have
never heard an opinion expressed; it cer-
tainly does and must necessarily depend
largely upon the personnel of the club
itself. To begin with I should say local
clubs are principally and first of all for the
development of home talent— its higher
cultivation or to bring out the most and
best of one’s possibilities for mutual help-
fulness and interchange of ideas of all in-
terested in the work mentally and in a
literary way; also sympathetically, for un-
less one’s sympathies are enlisted and one’s
whole intellectual nature fully awakened to
improvement and advanced thought, there
never can be any great achievement pos-
sible along the superior lines of what is


[Column 1]
all, and out of forty members or perhaps
termed the heights and depths of 
knowledge.
	How often have we heard women re-
gretting that their school days were over,
feeling that something tangible had gone
out of their lives, that they are losing, that
they are not advancing, in fact even those
who are happily married always speak with
a marked affectionate earnestness of the
school or class association, this lack is great-
ly obviated in club life—these clube are
sometimes spoken of as “married wo-
en’s colleges”—they aim to fill a place left
vacant, as woman grows away from the
friends of her girlhood, often called, to a
new home and surroundings, away from
all that was familiar, perchance to great
responsibilities for which she may be
totally unfitted either by birth education or
training; what outlet has she for the active
forces within, that would respond to some
call for expression, some ventilation of views
or opinions that might be a positive relief
to an overburdened heart, an antidote for
loneliness, and even uplift those around
her as well as her individual self. Often
the tired mother would be glad to converse
intelligently and sympathetically on equal
terms with some sister woman, but calls are
formal, dinners and teas are stupid affairs
usually, but the club comes in where all
meet on an equal footing, not to gossip, or
to talk scandal, but for mutual benefit and
that greater good to the larger number.
	If twenty-five or thirty women, or more
or less, unite to meet regularly for mutual
improvement, even then it may happen,
that a subject is sprung that is new to them
or to the world generally, for new ideas are
generated, or new phases of thought, as
well as new discoveries made. In such cases
certainly secure for the club the most able
exponent of the subject, to lecture or speak
upon it, that the whole club may get the
best that can be brought to bear upon the 
question.
	In all clubs there are some exceptionally
bright people who enthuse the majority,
carry them forward (so to speak), yet people
brought into close communication soon get
to know each other’s capacity and to compre-
hend what each one is equal to—and one
has to be diligent as well as brilliant not to
grow commonplace in the eyes of those
who meet them regularly. The saying “a
prophet is not without honor save in his
own country and in his own house, ”is a very
true one, think of it as you will; we all
know with what enthusiasm we go to hear
some new orator, or lecturer, even though
he may not be intellectually above those
with whom we are daily associated, yet it is
different, and stimulates our energies and
faculties. A change is wholesome, the great
world of nature teems with variety; and
there is nothing more disadvantageous to
the growth of the soul than to get into a 
rut, either in individual life, or in club
life, “variety is the spice of life,” and the
club needs new wine every now and then
to infuse and effusive, giving vigor, fresh-
ness and animation; people grow weary of
sameness, and pull contrariwise sometimes,
not because they are disagreeable but they
need uplifting. A club may and often
does tire of general work planned for them
even though they have consented to it,
those who take part may not be as brilliant
in dealing with questions as had been
anticipated, and in such cases it would seem
wise and expedient to have some outside
talent brought in; no one person knows it

[Column 2]
more, there may not be one who could tell
of actual experiences in Africa, or travels
up the Nile, or who had visited the Holy
Land, or been in East India, or a thousand
and one other things equally entertaining
and instructive. We are all of us likely to
tire of familiar themes and a new element
introduced occasionally increases the vigor
and power of development, brightens the
dull edge of wit, and sharpens the intellect.
In fact whenever a club talent introduced just
as you would send for a doctor in case of
sickness, sometimes two or three doctors
are needed; tone up the club with some-
thing strong, just as you would a patient,
apply a remedy that will be effectual, if
help can be procured.
No particular method can be laid down;
it seems to me these things must be left to
the discretion of the management, or a
committee wisely chosen, but certainly a 
club should take advantage of experts
along any line of thought, or of work in
which it is desirable to get the best and
most reliable information.
	All knowledge does not come from books,
there is the Great Teacher who inspires us all
in a greater or less degree, and none of us
know what may yet be developed in this
progressive age of the world, and whether
this new truth may be given to a sister wo-
man, or brother man, we may find our-
selves wiser, and better by living in a con-
dition to receive it. In summing up the
matter I would say outside talent may per-
haps depend too upon the financial condi-
tion of the club, that sometimes controls
larger interests in the community than club
life. To some people thoughts came pour-
ing in like great rivers, to others in little
streamlets clearer and purer perhaps, the
club may be the outlet and get the benefit
of the overflow of the great river, which
may refresh the parched soil, and the lit-
tle streamlet may, winding about here and
there, gladden many hearts with purer in-
spirations and be more desirable than the
great river.
	One great advantage of woman’s clubs is
the awakening of thought that bears fruit
in ideas, often very original ones, and per-
haps crude, but if one would not hesitate to
present these to the club, they might be
polished up and have a most brilliant set-
ting; only be sure and preserve the kernel,
or the essence of that which first came to
the individual, the seedling, dont lose the
positive thought through some sophistry of
expression. The world is moving onward
and the good time is surely coming when
we may grasp the ideal as well as the real,
instead of letting it soar away from us in
the dim distance, when we who have al-
ways longed to scale the heights inaccessi-
ble, may be uplifted above the clouds and
reaching down, help others to climb to a
place of safety above the storm and tempest,
but while we journey onward and upward,
let us clasp hands as sisters in sweet assur-
ance of helping each other to cross the
desert of ignorance and the dark river
of tradition, where the little boats of error
and superstition glide in and out alluring
the unwory. We will watch for the
beacon light of truth that shines from afar
over the Elysian fields of knowledge and
understanding. And while we pursue
our pioneer march, let us not forget the in-
numerable company that follow in our
wake, and leave for them shining waymarks,
hopeful harbingers of success in attaining
the goal of our—ambitions,


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


A UTAH WOMAN INVENTOR.

	MRS. JULIA FREKE SAMSON, of this City,
has invented an unique music holder
which she had patented Feb. 21st, 1893,
and which has been highly recommended
by musical people here and elsewhere, in
fact it will be a great convenience as the
following letters which are correct copies of
the original abundantly testify.
THE KNUTSFORD,
				Salt Lake City,
						Sep. 27, 1892.
	The music holder designed by Mrs. J.
Samson, examined by me this morning,
combines several advantages which will
commend it to musicians and students.
	Music can be sorted, held in position,
carefully protected from injuries and con-
veniently packed and carried about, by
means of this very comprehensive and
practical arrangement.
	The undersigned expects to save wear
and tear of music and considerable incon-
venience in the future by its use, 
	(signed) WILLIAM H. SHERWOOD,
Director Piano Department, 
				Chicago Conservatory.
	This Testimonial is certified to officially
by a Notary Public.
	Here also is another copy of a letter also
certified to in the same way.
		SALT LAKE CITY, Sep, 28, 1892.
Mrs. Samson:
	MY DEAR MADAME:—Your music
holder, the “Tallyho” is the very thing
our choir singers need to carry their books
in, and to protect from rains etc.
	Thousands of dollars are wasted yearly
by singers having to roll their books in
their hands in their way to and from re-
hearsal. This can now all be alleviated,
your ingenious device making it possible to
carry them straight—also to place them al-
together where nothing need be lost or
forgotten when wanted.
	What is so useful to choristers, must be
equally useful to all others who have, similar
books to carry and keep together. It must
succeed when known and understood for it
is so much needed.
			Yours,
				EVAN STEPHENS.
	Conductor of Tabernacle Choir and
Choral Society Salt Lake City.
	The “Tallyho” can be manufactured
here, and will, it is hoped, soon be put up-
on the market; the difficulty has been lack
of means, and yet it does seem very hard
when home products and articles are so
much talked of, that there cannot be means
furnished to manufacture what would
be so useful and economical in our own
community. The “Tallyho” was on ex-
hibition at the World’s Fair and elicited
considerable attention, but it wants
patronage as well as money. Those so
ardently interested in home work should
take pains to look into this matter. Wo-
men inventors are rare, and Utah should
make the most of those who have shown
ability in this line.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	ON Tuesday evening, April 17th, a few
of the friends of Mrs. Emily H. Wood-
mansee met at her residence to show their
esteem and love for one, a sister and a poet,


who has always bravely defended the cause
of Zion under all adverse circumstances,
and in the days of prosperity has sung its
praises, and in joyful strains portrayed its
beauties and excellencies. Her poems are
all, of them rich with the inspiration of
Gospel truths.
	There were about thirty people present
and most of the sisters answered to the roll
call with an extract or verse, sometimes
more than one from Sister Woodmansee’s
own writings, which abound in sentiment
suitable to repeat on such occasions. There
were no formal addresses but some af-
fectionate expressions of good will and
such fellowship as promotes harmony and
an increase of love among friends whose
daily lives are full to overflowing of 
practical everyday matters in this work-a-
day world. Mrs. Woodmansee was
given a handsome copy of George Elliot’s
Poems. Mrs. E. B. Wells, in behalf of
the ladies,made the presentation; two of the
late lamented Sister Elizabeth Howards
daughters were present, all the way from
Big Cotton Wood. Mrs. Kate Howard
Brockbank, whose husband Elder Isaac
Brockbank, accompanied her and Mrs.
Mary Howard Brinton, Bishop Elias Mor-
ris and wife of the 15th Ward, and Bishop
George H. Taylor and wife of the 14th
Ward, Sisters M. Isabella Horne,
Lula Green Richards, Margaret
Y. Taylor, Mr. and Mrs Edward Steven-
son, Mrs. C. S. Thomas, Dr. E. R.
Shipp, Helen Mar Whitney, Mrs. Vilate
Romney and others, most of the sisters
spoke a few encouraging words, and Miss
Myrtle Woodmansee favored the company
with some piano music, giving choice selec-
tidns and delicious picnic added to the
social enjoyment, as refreshments ever do,
when the conversation is highly intellectual
and even spiritual.
	THE ladies of the World’s Fair Associa- 
tion of Davis County presented to the Salt
Take Temple the handsome set of furniture
made for their exhibit, and upholstered in
homemade silk (sage green,) the wood
carved in sego lilies, the floral emblem of
Utah. This furniture attracted much at-
tention in the parlor of the Utah Building,
it was a meritorious piece of workmanship
and now it is in a place appropriate to its 
artistic value. The design was by Utah
women and they may well be proud of
their brilliant success. A colored portrait of
Mrs. Amelia Spencer Rogers, surrounded
by some of the boys and girls of the Pri-
mary Association, handsomely framed, was
also given with the furniture. Mrs. Rogers
was the originator of the idea of our
Primary Associations, and the writer was
present when she first gave expression to
the thought to our former president and
leader, Sister Eliza R. Snow, who grasped
the situation, and with the assistance of
others as helps, organized the associations
that are now doing such good work
throughout the valleys of the mountains
we term Zion. The committee who pre-
sented the valuable exhibits to President
Lorenzo Snow in the Salt Lake Temple
were Sisters Lucy A. Clark, Jane Holt,
Athalia M. Steed and Anna Williams.


WOMANS EXPONENT.


THE Arena (Boston) closes its ninth
volume with the May number. The Arena
has made steady progress since its founda-
tion, and now firmly holds its place as one
of the three leading American reviews.


RELIEF SOCIETY CONFERENCE.
	[Concluded.]
	AFTERNOON SESSION:--Opened with
singing "O say what is truth" prayer by
Mrs. Lydia D. Alder, singing "For the
strength of the hills we bless thee" &c.
Minutes of previous October Conference
read (afternoon) Counselor L. M. Hart
Bear Lake Stake gave an excellent report
in a concise manner of the work in which
the sisters of that locality were engaged.
Mrs. Angeline Hinckley reported Millard
Stake as doing well and especially in regard
to the needy. Mrs. M. A. Grover in a 
very able manner spoke for Juab Stake, one
partiular feature of her remakrs being that
the young mothers come to meeting with
their babies. Mrs. M. A. Stoddard was
authorized to represent Cassia Stake in the
interest of which she did remarkably well.
	President Zina D. H. Young then spoke
upon the subject of silk culture and the
necessity of making preparations in time to
accomplish good results, explained in detail
some experiences in this direction, also
spoke particularly of the care of the sick
and of the ordinances of the Church, the
blessings which were to follow the be-
liever the cultivation of faith and the
virtue of prayer and of promoting greater
union.
	Counselor B. W. Smith made some ex-
cellent remarks about the Temple and the
sweet spirit prevailing in that sacred place,
thought all the Saints must be truthful,
upright and honest; increase in love and
good works, love each other and promote
the cause of righteousness and help for-
ward the interests of this kingdom; ex-
horted mothers to teach their children to be
industrious, and knew if they would, such
children would be more likely to grow up
honorable men and women, urged the
silk culture and the circulation of the WO-
MAN'S EXPONENT, and many other home
industries and home subjects.
	Mrs. M. I. Horne gave out a notice con-
cerning the Woman's Store and the Circular
that had been prepared, solicited the
patronage of the women of the County,
thought the store should be better sus-
tained. Counselor Reese from Box Elder,
said some branches in the Stake were very
remote and could not be visited often but
once a year the President and others gener-
ally succeeded in reaching them; spoke
very highly of their President and  her
noble work.
	Counselor Jane S. Richards spoke very
interestingly upon a variety of subjects
urged the sisters to sustain the EXPONENT,
and to economize and do those things that
were necessary, felt that visiting the sick
and assisting and comforting them was in-
deed a labor of love; thought we should en-
courage silk raising, many of the trees
planted years ago are dead, but the people
should put out new ones, spoke of the
cyclones and many calamities that were
coming and felt if we wished to avoid these
things we must live near the Lord; those
who were aged should be diligent in season
and out of season and the young had no
time to lose.
	President F. A. Hammond of San Juan
Co. said he represented one of the largest
counties in area, but they only had eighty-
five votes, believed in woman suffrage, felt
that the Relief Society was a great power
and aid to the people, they had no Bishop's
trials, no family troubles, and no need of
difficulties, there were no whisky shops,
no gambling dens; the EXPONENT was ap-
preciated among them, he felt it was im-
portant for the sisters to have it and always
read it himself with great interest, the
speaker did’nt know much about seri-culture,
but he remembered the flax and tow, knew
how it could be utilized, the Lord has told
us, to adorn ourselves with our
own hands.
	President Z. D. H. Young made some
telling remarks about the EXPONENT urg-
ing the sisters to sustain it by their sub-
scription, asking those who did take it to
raise the right hand that she might see how
many there were; spoke of the request she
had made through the EXPONENT that all
the branches of the Relief Society should
study the lectures upon faith and seek to
get the spirit of them and that in so doing
they should be blest, said "have we left the
homes of our fathers and the graves of our
ancestors to come here and forsake the truth
and descend to lesser things? verily no, we
must keep the commandments of God in
that alone there is safety for us."
	The secretary, by request, read the
circular issued by the officers of the Wo-
man's Co-operative Store, which was com-
plete in itself and called for support from
the women of Zion, made some remarks up-
on subjects connected with the work of the 
society.
Mrs. Emily S. Richards spoke of the silk
of the Utah Exhibit in Chicago, described
the silk curtains, and the embroidery in
sego lilies, also the Provo and Logan cur-
tains and the linen exhibit from Springville,
and many other articles, described hur-
riedly her work at the World's Fair, and of
the great interest manifested in our women
and especially at a meeting in the organiza-
tion room where she had spoken of our
different associations, the Relief Society, the
Young Ladies' and the Primary, referred to
some influential women who had expressed
their astonishment, and Miss Anthony's
reply, in which she stated that she had
known of the organized work we had
been doing for years.  Mrs. Richards ex-
pressed her great satisfaction with our
position before the world and our connec-
tion with the National Council of Women,
explained how it first came about and
what an advantage it had been and would
be in the future.
	President J. N. Smith of San Luis
Colorado, spoke of the blessings and
privileges of the Gospel enjoyed by the
Saints in that locality, though all were poor,
and referred to it as the magnanimous state,
where women enjoyed and could exercise
the right of the ballot. President Ricks, of
Bannock Idaho, referred to the days of the
Prophet Joseph and gave some interesting
incidents of that period. President Haight
of Cassia, Idaho, related some facts that had
come to his notice in that Stake when the
Relief Society had accomplished very
material good, alluded to one instance
where the Society had helped to lift the
mortage from the property of a widow
which was a great temporal blessing, urged
the sisters to help in sustaining home
manufactories. President Horne gave
some notice of the circulars for the Wo-
man's Store and the congregation joined in
singing the Doxology. Benediction was
pronounced by President Thomas Ricks,
and Conference adjourned for six months.
	E. B. WELLS, Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


U. W. P. C.
	THE Club met at Dr. Shipp's Office on
the evening of April 28th; an extra large
number of ladies’ in attendance. The roll
call was responded to with choice quota-
tions from the poets. Ella W. Hyde read
a paper which she had prepared, on the
“Life and work of John Howard Payne,”
author of the old but ever touching “Home,
Sweet Home." An original poem,
entitled, “Fickle April,” was read by Ruth
M. Fox, who was also the author. Olea
Shipp favored the club with music.
Dr. E. B. Ferguson read a very able and
scholarly paper on the subject of
Psychology, dealing especially with the
Intellect: showing its several divisions and
subdivisions, the actions and province of
this department of self as distinguished
from the other powers of the mind. 
	The second part of this subject, assigned
to Miss G. Woodmansee, was carried over
for one month by request. Mrs. A. E.
Bennion followed with a lecture on the
“Will.”' Explained the importance of the
position which it occupies in the mental
economy. By way of illustration, the
lady refered to the benefit the world has
reaped from the persistence or will of
Columbus, and other discoverers and inven-
tors. As examples of those possessing
wonderful will power she named, Patrick
Henry, Joseph Smith, Brigham Young
and among our intimate friends, E. B.
Wells, Dr. E. R. Shipp and others. How
would they have succeeded without this
power? Mrs. C. Louise Boyden, who was
pleased to be with the club again, after a
long absence, gave a recitation, entitled,
“The Old Church Organ,” as told by a
yankee; rendering it in her well remember-
ed and pleasing manner.  Pearl Russell
gave a pretty vocal selection and E. B.
Wells read a poem, entitled, “Leona.” 
	Miscellaneous business was attended to
and the critic wishing to be excused from
that task until next month, it was dispensed
with. Benediction by Mrs. Z. D. H.
Young. Refreshments and discussion fol-
lowed until the time for adjournment.
	Programe for May 31st:—Lecture on
Psychology, “The Feeling,” Gladys
Woodmansee; Music, Lizzie Shipp; Poem,
original, Dr. E. R. Shipp; Paper on
Theosophy, Dr. R. B. Pratt; members to 
respond to roll with sentiments from Pope. 
	GLADYS WOODMANSEE, Rec. Sec.


R. S. REPORTS.
  
		JUAB STAKE.
	MINUTES of the Quarterly Conference
of the Relief Society of the Juab Stake
held in the Nephi Tabernacle, Dec. 15th,
1883. President Mary Pitchforth presiding.
Sang, “Come, dearest Lord, descend and
dwell,” prayer by Counselor Charles
Sperry. Continued by singing, “Come
thou desire of all thy Saints," minutes of
the last Conference read and approved.
President Pitchforth, in her opening ad-
dress, prayed that the Spirit of God might
rest upon her, as also those assembled that
every word might be dictated thereby.
Spoke at some length on the advancement
of the young, was very much interested in
the young ladies and gentlemen of Nephi;
asked the blessings of God on all present,
that at the close of our conference we can
say it is good to have met together. Sang,
“Now let us rejoice in the day of Salva-
tion."

[Column 2]
	Sister Wilkey represented First Ward of
Nephi, Sister Lerwell, Mona; Sister
Christenson Levan branches, all being in
excellent condition. Meetings were well
attended and a unity of' feeling existing in
their midst.
	Singing “Fear not, I am with thee.”
By the suggestion of President Pitchforth.
	Sister S. A. Cazier offered an inspired
prayer in behalf of Sister Udall. Sister
Udall having left the meeting to appear be-
fore the commissioners.
	Counselor Hannah Jones referred to the
prayer offered by Sister Cazier, she was
truly inspired; referred to Sister Wells’
speech in the suffrage meeting, had learned
more than she had ever known of that
association. Was in favor of the associa-
tion, and the rights of women.
	Sister E. B. Wells spoke of the shadow
that had fallen on our meeting through the
arrest of Sister Udall. Sister Zina Young
had sent her love to the sisters, would very
much like to have been with them, desired
the sisters to cultivate the spirit of faith,
read the Lectures on Faith in the Doctrine
and Covenants, advised that practice to be
introduced in all the branches of the Relief
Society. Not any of us were safe without
faith, we always need it. Spoke of the
good that had been wrought for Zion, the
benefits of the Salt Lake Temple and how
much good had been done there, referred to
the storing of grain, also the respect shown
to the Salt Lake Choir in attending the
World’s Fair, had been very much in-
terested in the reports of the sisters. Urged
them to cultivate more and greater faith
in our midst. Sang, Doxology. Prayer
by Brother Hartley,adjourned until 2 p. m.
	AFTERNOON SESSION:—President Pitch-
forth persiding. Singing, “I know that
my Redeemer lives.” Prayer by President
Paxman. Sister Udall represented Second
Ward of Nephi in good condition, felt
grateful for the prayers offered up in her
behalf, had felt the influence of the prayer,
testified that it had been answered,
desired to prove true and faithful. Sister
Bryan felt blessed in a knowledge of the
Gospel, prayed God to bless all, with every
blessing that would be for our good.
	Sister Wells spoke at some length on
the silk industry, advised the sisters to
plant mulberry trees, and raise cocoons;
gave a discription of some of the exhibits,
at the World’s Fair, referred briefly to the
different meetings of women at Chicago.
	Reading by Sister Chappell, “He
willed it so.”
	President Pitchforth had been very
much interested in listening to the remarks
of Sister Wells, felt that the God we wor-
ship will protect us in the faith we believe
in. Was thankful for the gospel and all
the blessings we receive.
	Recitation by Sister S. A. Cazier,
“Wherefore and Why,” composed by
Sister Emily H. Woodmansee.
	President Paxman was pleased to have
Sister Wells with us to explain so intelle-
gently what transpired at the World’s Fair,
referred to the silk industry; felt that the
time would come when we would dress in
the silk raised here. Spoke of
the manifestations of God in many ways to
his poor and how full of gratitude we
should be, prayed God to be with us and
seal upon our hearts what had been said.
	Singing, “We thank thee O God for
a prophet,” benediction by Bro. Charles
Sperry.

[Column 3]
	RELIEF Society Conference, March 16,
1894, President Mary Pitchforth presiding.
Sang, “Praise ye the Lord.” Prayer by
Brother Sperry. “My God the spring of
all my joys.” Elder John L. Nuttall was
the first speaker. Had become very much
interested in the Relief Society in all its
stakes and branches. Explained the new
organization of the Relief Society, said it
is a church organization, the same church,
the same laws and the same sisters; spoke
of the energy of President Young in these
societies dwelt at some length on the duties
of the officers and members of the Society,
also on the necessity of storing grain.
Choir sang, “Redeemer of Israel."
Minutes of previous meeting read and ap-
proved.
	President Pitchforth then read the
Eureka yearly report, also a letter from the
president of the Levan branch, represent-
ing the Levan Ward in good condition,
spoke of the energy of the sisters and their
faithful labors, felt it a privilege to bear
testimony to the truth of this work, hoped
that the Lord would accept our repentance
for what we have failed to do in the past
and be merciful to us in the future.
	Sister Wilkey represented First Ward and
Sister Udall Second Ward of Nephi as being
in excellent condition; a unity of feelings
existing with officers and members.
	Singing, “How firm a foundation."
Counselor Bigler was very much interested
in listening to the reports, also the speakers;
felt that we were truly blessed of the Lord
at all times. Counselor Jones said her
soul rejoiced in the blessings of God to this
people, felt that all the branches in the Juab
Stake were filled with the same spirit, spoke
of the growth of the soctiey, and the in-
telligence of the young.
	Sister Bryan knew that the work was
true, wished to live worthy of all the
blessings in store for the faitliful. The
Lord knew how to chastise his people, 
He always did it in a proper manner; asked
the blessings of God upon all present.
	President Pitchforth presented the officers
of the Juab Stake, which were sustained by
unaminous vote. Elder James Paxman
said his heart had been made glad to hear
the excellent reports of the sisters, the
work of the Relief Society was a great
labor, the sisters hearts were as large as
mountains, had nothing but blessings in his
heart for them; spoke of the good results of
a loving kind influence at home, the
responsibility rests upon mothers, your
mission is at home, let home be first, other
duties after. Spoke of the testimonies of
the sisters and the good they had done.
	President Paxman, said if we could
retain what had been said we should be
truly benefited by coming, referred to the
Relief Society, said that the bishops had
testified to him of the help and comfort the 
sisters were to them. Spoke on the storing
of grain, encouraged the storing of corn and
beans as Brother Nuttall had previously
referred to it, thought that the articles of
faith instructed by Sister Young to be read
in each society would be of great benifit to
us, spoke in praise of the officers of the
society, the Lord was with them at all
times to sustain them and build them up.
Concluded by saying “may the Lord pour
out his blessings upon all.” After singing
and prayer conference adjourned for three
months.
	ELLEN L. GOLDSBROUGH, Sec


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


W.S.A.

	MINUTES of W.S.A. held April 17th,
1894, third Vice President Julia Druce in
the chair. Singing "Search for Freedom"
prayer by Mrs. E.M. Price, singing “Help
the Working women.” Roll called by the
Secretary. Minutes of the previous meet-
ing read and approved. Dr. M. Hughes
Cannon being the first on the program
responded, gave a very interesting address
on the rights and and ability of woman, dwell-
ing on the force of character of the
Spartan women and the courage of the
Roman women; recited "The Gladiator."
Spoke on women being accused of too
much slavery to fashion to have anything
to do with politics brought to bear the time
when men shaved off their hair and wore
wigs. Mrs. E.M. Price spoke very
eloquently on Queen Elizabeth's Statesman-
ship. Mrs. Ella W. Hyde spoke on Suf-
frage proper; thought there was not the
shadow of a doubt but what we would one
day get the franchise, exhorted us to study
the suffrage subject. Mrs. May Talmage
was invited to say a few words, stated she
had been absent so long did'nt feel prepared
said it was the same with suffrage as other
things we must study and keep ourselves
bright. Mrs. Fox made some interesting
remarks; recited a poem “My Fortieth
Birthday” composed by herself which
she did in a very sympathetic manner.
	Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball Honorary
President gave a short address and alluded
to the honor conferred upon Mary the
mother of the Savior in a very interest-
ing way and referred to traditions concern-
ing Mary and the angel Gabriel.
	Mrs. M.A. Freeze thought if immoral
men like Colonel Breckenridge could help
make laws, when the women got the
franchise they would elect better men.
President E.J. McFarlane suggested we
had a spirited poem recited or read every
meeting, her remarks were followed by a
few words of counsel from Vice President
Ardella Eardly, the ladies adjourned to the
parlor and were entertained with music and
singing by Miss Fay McCune. Meeting
was then adjourned for one month.
	M.E. IRVINE, Sec.


NOTES AND NEWS. 

	MRS. LOUISE CHANDLER MOULTON WILL
sail for Europe about May 26.
	MR. and MRS. RUDYARD KIPLING and
the baby Kipling have sailed for England,
where they will spend the summer.
	JOHN BRISBEN WALKER, publisher of
the Cosmopolitan, has decided to move his
plant and publication office from New York
to Irvington-on-the-Hudson, where he now
resides. 
	MRS. OLIVE THORNE MILLER has put
into a book called "A Bird-Lover in the
West” her observations and experiences of
birds in Ohio, Colorado, and Utah.
	WHILE a student at Oxford, Mr. Glad-
stone was one of the competitors for the
Ireland scholarship but failed. "Desultory
beyond belief,” is what the principal
examiner wrote on Gladstone’s paper, and
he went so far as to charge the future states-
man with “throwing dust into the
examiner’s eyes, like a man who, when
asked, who wrote ‘God Save the-King?’
answered ‘Thomson wrote ‘Rule Britanna.”


UTAH SILK ASSOCIATION
BYLAWS.

Article I.—Name.
	THIS Association shall be known as the
Utah Woman’s Silk Association.
Article 2.—Object. 
	The object of this association shall be to
encourage silk culture in Utah by dis-
seminating information, distributing silk
worm eggs and acting as a central bureau
for the sale of cocoons and the manufacture 
of silk.
Article 3.—Officers and Executive Board
	The officers shall consist of a Board of
Directors of seven members, to be elected
annually by the Association; and the
Directors within a week after their election
shall from their number, elect a President,
Vice President, Secretary and Treasurer.
Article 4.—Membership.
	Any woman residing in Utah may become
a member of this Association, by signing
the by-laws and paying an initiation fee of
twenty-five cents. The membership fee
and all other moneys raised by the Associa-
tion may be used for the expense of the
Association at the direction of the Executive
Board.
Article 5.—Meetings.
	The Board of Directors shall meet fort-
nightly on Tuesday at 11 a. m. A quorum
shall consist of four members. The As-
sociation shall meet twice a year at such
time and place as the Board of Directors
shall designate. One third of the member-
ship of the Association shall constitute a 
quorum.
Article 6.—Amendments.
	These by-laws may be amended at any
regular meeting of the Board of Directors
by a two thirds majority vote of the mem-
bers present, notice having been given at a
previous meeting.
Margaret B. Salisbury, President; E. B.
Wells, Vice-President; C. M. Allen, Secre-
tary Margaret A. Caine, Treasurer. 
	Board of Directors:—Margaret B. Salis
bury, Isabella Bennett, Zina D. H. Young,
Amelia F. Young, Emmeline B. Wells,
Corinne M. Allen, Margaret A. Caine.


OBITUARY.

	IT is with feelings of sadness we have been
called to part with one of our sisters. Sister
Amanda Person who suddenly and unexpectedly
departed this life on the morning of the 24, of Jan.
at her home near Sandy, the cause of her death
being premature child-birth, after a brief sick-
ness of only a few short hours.
	She was born in Ostamar, Sweden, her age
twenty seven years and two months and was the
wife of Henry M. Person. She was the mother of
five children, four of whom, including the in-
fant buried with her, are dead. One only, a little
boy five years old, being left.
	Sister Amanda Person joined the church in 1878
and emigrated with her parents in 1880, her
residence being at Sandy, where she gained love.
and confidence of all who were associated with her
She was a true wife and mother and a faithful
Latter-day Saint. She was a member of the
Relief Society. The funeral services were held
in Sandy Meeting House the 27th Jan. The
large concourse of sympathizing friends evidenc-
ed the respect and esteem, in which the family
were held.
Dear Sister Amanda, thou art gone 
	To dwell in realms above,
To await the resurrection, morn
When you’ll meet with those you love.
	WILHILMINA OLSEN, Prest.
	HILDA LARSON, Sec.
